nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Segment Trees and Fenwick Trees
nodes.label: Segment Trees and Fenwick Trees
nodes.excerpt: Segment trees and Fenwick trees both answer range queries + point updates in O(log n), vs O(n) for naive prefix sums when data changes. Segment tree: more powerful — supports range min/max/GCD/arbitrary associative operations, lazy propagation for range updates. Fenwick tree (BIT): simpler, less memory, only supports prefix-sum-based queries (range sum, frequency counting). Segment tree built on array of size 4n; Fenwick tree on array of size n+1. Both are log n but Fenwick has a smaller constant.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Shortest Path Algorithms (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, A-star, Floyd-Warshall)
nodes.label: Shortest Path Algorithms (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, A-star, Floyd-Warshall)
nodes.excerpt: Four shortest-path algorithms for four different constraints: BFS (unweighted), Dijkstra (non-negative weights, O((V+E) log V) with heap), Bellman-Ford (negative weights/cycles, O(VE)), A* (heuristic-guided single-target), Floyd-Warshall (all-pairs O(V³)). Dijkstra's key invariant: once a node is popped from the min-heap, its shortest distance is finalized — this breaks with negative edges. Negative cycles make shortest paths undefined because you can loop forever to decrease cost.

nodes.label: Skip Lists
nodes.excerpt: A skip list is a sorted linked list with multiple 'express lane' levels. Each element is promoted to higher levels with probability p (typically 0.5), giving O(log n) expected search, insert, and delete. Unlike balanced BSTs (AVL, red-black), skip lists require no rotations — just random promotion. Redis uses a skip list for its sorted set (ZSET) because it supports O(log n) rank queries and range traversal naturally. Worst case is O(n) but this is astronomically unlikely with good randomness.

nodes.label: Sliding Window Technique
nodes.excerpt: Sliding window maintains a contiguous range [left, right] over an array or string, expanding right to include new elements and shrinking left when a constraint is violated. Fixed-window: move both pointers together (max sum subarray of size k). Variable-window: expand right always, shrink left until constraint satisfied — gives O(n) because each element enters and exits the window exactly once. Key patterns: frequency map with character counts (longest substring without repeats, minimum window substring), monotonic deque for window max/min (O(n) vs O(n log n) heap).

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms — Comparison-Based (Merge, Quick, Heap, Tim)
nodes.label: Sorting Algorithms — Comparison-Based (Merge, Quick, Heap, Tim)
nodes.excerpt: The four comparison sorts worth knowing deeply: merge sort (stable, O(n log n), O(n) space, preferred for linked lists), quicksort (in-place, O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst-case — fixed by random pivot or median-of-three), heap sort (in-place, O(n log n) guaranteed, but poor cache locality), Timsort (Python/Java default — exploits natural runs, galloping mode, O(n) best on nearly-sorted data). Lower bound: any comparison sort requires Ω(n log n) comparisons — proved via decision tree argument. Stability means equal elements preserve original relative order.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms — Non-Comparison (Radix, Counting, Bucket)
nodes.label: Sorting Algorithms — Non-Comparison (Radix, Counting, Bucket)
nodes.excerpt: Non-comparison sorts bypass the Ω(n log n) lower bound by using structure in the keys rather than pairwise comparisons. Counting sort: O(n+k) for small integer ranges (k=max value). Radix sort: O(d×(n+k)) for d-digit integers, stable, processes one digit at a time using counting sort as subroutine. Bucket sort: O(n+k) average when input is uniformly distributed, O(n²) worst case. The catch: all require assumptions about keys (bounded integers, fixed-width, uniform distribution) that don't hold for general comparison-based sorting.

nodes.label: Stacks and Queues
nodes.excerpt: Stacks (LIFO) and queues (FIFO) are constrained-access structures whose value lies in the constraint itself — it forces a discipline that makes certain algorithms correct and efficient. Monotonic stack is the single most underutilized interview pattern: maintain a stack where elements are always increasing (or decreasing), enabling O(n) solutions to 'next greater element', histogram area, and trap water problems. In Python: list as stack (append/pop), collections.deque as queue (append/popleft — never use list for queue, popleft is O(n)).

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/String Matching Algorithms (KMP, Rabin-Karp)
nodes.label: String Matching Algorithms (KMP, Rabin-Karp)
nodes.excerpt: KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) finds all occurrences of a pattern in a text in O(n+m) by precomputing a failure function (LPS array) that tells how far to shift the pattern on mismatch — never backing up in the text. Rabin-Karp uses polynomial rolling hashes to compare windows in O(1), making it O(n+m) average but O(nm) worst case (hash collisions). KMP is better for single-pattern exact search; Rabin-Karp is better for multi-pattern search (compare hash to a set of target hashes). The Z-function provides an alternative O(n+m) approach.

nodes.label: Topological Sort
nodes.excerpt: Topological sort linearly orders vertices of a DAG so every directed edge u→v has u before v. Two algorithms: Kahn's BFS (repeatedly remove zero-indegree nodes — also detects cycles: if output has fewer than V nodes, a cycle exists) and DFS post-order (add each node to the front of a list when all its dependencies are done). O(V+E) for both. The canonical interview pattern: course schedule / task dependency problems.

nodes.id: Resources/CS Theory & Algorithms/Trees — Binary, BST, AVL, Red-Black
nodes.label: Trees — Binary, BST, AVL, Red-Black
nodes.excerpt: Binary Search Trees give O(log n) search/insert/delete when balanced — but degenerate to O(n) if the tree becomes a linked list (sorted input is the classic trigger). AVL trees maintain strict balance (|height_left - height_right| ≤ 1) via rotations after every insert/delete. Red-black trees use 5 color invariants for looser balance — O(log n) guaranteed, fewer rotations than AVL, used in Java TreeMap and Linux kernel. In-order traversal of a BST yields sorted order. Python has no built-in BST — use sortedcontainers.SortedList.

nodes.label: Tries (Prefix Trees)
nodes.excerpt: A trie (prefix tree) stores strings by sharing common prefixes — each node represents one character, paths from root to leaf nodes spell out words. Lookup, insert, and prefix search are all O(L) where L = key length, independent of the number of stored keys. Key advantage over hash map: prefix queries ('all words starting with ca-') are O(L + k) vs O(n) linear scan. Memory: each node needs 26 child pointers for lowercase English — use dict-of-children for sparse alphabets. Radix tree / compressed trie collapses single-child chains into one edge.

nodes.label: Two-Pointer Technique
nodes.excerpt: Two-pointer uses two indices moving through data in coordinated ways to reduce O(n²) nested loops to O(n). Three flavors: (1) opposite-ends on a sorted array (two-sum, three-sum, container with most water), (2) slow/fast pointers (cycle detection, find middle, remove nth from end), (3) read/write pointers (remove duplicates in-place, partition, Dutch national flag). The key invariant: at each step, the two pointers maintain a property that lets you eliminate a portion of the search space — you prove progress by showing both pointers advance.

nodes.label: Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union)
nodes.excerpt: Union-Find (DSU) maintains a partition of n elements into disjoint sets. Two operations: find(x) returns the root/representative of x's set, union(x, y) merges two sets. With path compression + union by rank, both operations are nearly O(1) — amortized O(α(n)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively ≤ 5 for any practical n). Core uses: Kruskal's MST (cycle detection), dynamic connectivity, number of connected components, and grouping problems where merges happen incrementally.

nodes.label: Caching
nodes.excerpt: deep dive: cache layers, read/write strategies, eviction policies, stampede/penetration/avalanche problems, Redis internals.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/Alcohol Metabolism (Ethanol to Acetaldehyde to Acetate)
nodes.label: Alcohol Metabolism (Ethanol to Acetaldehyde to Acetate)
nodes.excerpt: Alcohol metabolism converts ethanol to toxic acetaldehyde and then acetate, mainly in the liver, while shifting NAD+/NADH redox balance and stressing sleep, inflammation, liver metabolism, and cancer risk. Surface this when explaining alcohol as biochemistry rather than just behavior or calories.

nodes.label: Biochemistry — Proteins and Enzymes
nodes.excerpt: Proteins are amino-acid polymers that fold into dynamic three-dimensional machines, while enzymes catalyze reactions by lowering activation energy without changing equilibrium. Surface this when explaining metabolism, muscle, signaling, disease mutations, digestion, drug targets, or why protein shape determines function.

nodes.label: Caffeine Mechanism (Adenosine Antagonism)
nodes.excerpt: Caffeine primarily blocks adenosine receptors, masking sleep pressure and increasing alertness while also affecting dopamine/norepinephrine signaling indirectly. Surface this when Kenny needs caffeine framed as signal masking with half-life, tolerance, anxiety, and sleep tradeoffs rather than free energy.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/Chemical Bonding (Ionic, Covalent, Hydrogen, Van der Waals)
nodes.label: Chemical Bonding (Ionic, Covalent, Hydrogen, Van der Waals)
nodes.excerpt: Chemical bonding explains how atoms and molecules stick together through electron transfer, electron sharing, polarity, and weaker intermolecular forces. Surface this when explaining protein folding, DNA base pairing, solubility, boiling points, drug binding, membranes, or why molecular shape controls function.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/DNA and RNA — Structure and Function
nodes.label: DNA and RNA — Structure and Function
nodes.excerpt: DNA stores genetic information in a stable double-stranded nucleotide sequence, while RNA copies, regulates, translates, and sometimes catalyzes biological information flow. Surface this when explaining genes, viruses, CRISPR, cancer, inheritance, mRNA vaccines, or why nucleic-acid structure enables copying.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/How Drugs Affect the Brain (Receptor Mechanisms)
nodes.label: How Drugs Affect the Brain (Receptor Mechanisms)
nodes.excerpt: Brain-active drugs change signaling by acting on receptors, transporters, enzymes, ion channels, and neurotransmitter release, with effects shaped by dose, receptor subtype, brain region, tolerance, withdrawal, and context. Surface this when explaining psychoactive substances without moralizing or oversimplifying mechanism into safety.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/Metabolic Pathways (Glycolysis, Krebs Cycle, Electron Transport Chain)
nodes.label: Metabolic Pathways (Glycolysis, Krebs Cycle, Electron Transport Chain)
nodes.excerpt: Central metabolism converts fuel into ATP, reducing power, heat, and biosynthetic intermediates through glycolysis, pyruvate oxidation, the Krebs cycle, and the electron transport chain. Surface this when explaining mitochondria, exercise, fasting, insulin, fatigue, alcohol metabolism, or why ATP production is controlled chemistry rather than a calorie slogan.

nodes.label: Nootropics — Evidence-Based Overview
nodes.excerpt: Nootropics are substances marketed or used to improve cognition, but evidence, effect size, safety, regulation, and user baseline vary widely. Surface this when Kenny is considering cognitive enhancers: first identify the bottleneck, then compare evidence-backed basics like sleep, exercise, treating disorders, caffeine timing, and creatine against supplement hype.

nodes.label: Organic Chemistry Basics
nodes.excerpt: Organic chemistry studies carbon-based molecules through structure, functional groups, stereochemistry, electron movement, and reaction mechanisms. Surface this when explaining why tiny molecular changes alter drugs, nutrients, proteins, fats, carbohydrates, DNA chemistry, plastics, and metabolism.

nodes.label: Periodic Table Essentials
nodes.excerpt: The periodic table organizes elements by atomic number and electron configuration, making it a predictive map for valence, bonding, reactivity, size, ionization energy, electronegativity, and metallic character. Surface this when Kenny needs chemistry explained from electron structure rather than memorized element trivia.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/Pharmacology Basics — How Drugs Work
nodes.label: Pharmacology Basics — How Drugs Work
nodes.excerpt: Pharmacology explains what drugs do to the body through receptors, enzymes, transporters, ion channels, dose-response, efficacy, potency, and what the body does to drugs through absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion. Surface this for mechanism-level drug conversations while keeping medical-use boundaries explicit.

nodes.label: Protein Synthesis (Transcription and Translation)
nodes.excerpt: Protein synthesis turns genetic information into protein through transcription from DNA to RNA and translation from mRNA codons to amino-acid chains. Surface this when explaining gene expression, mutations, ribosomes, antibiotics, mRNA vaccines, biotechnology, or how genotype becomes cellular function.

nodes.id: Resources/Chemistry & Biochemistry/The Chemistry of Sleep (Melatonin, Adenosine, GABA)
nodes.label: The Chemistry of Sleep (Melatonin, Adenosine, GABA)
nodes.excerpt: Sleep chemistry involves circadian timing signals like melatonin, homeostatic sleep pressure through adenosine, inhibitory signaling through GABA, wake stabilization through orexin, and stress/arousal chemistry. Surface this when Kenny needs sleep explained as interacting systems rather than one magic molecule or supplement.

nodes.label: Claude Code Skills
nodes.excerpt: how Claude Code skills work: markdown files injected into context, gstack architecture (templates + Bun build), gbrain per-project learnings, how to write your own skill.

nodes.label: Cloud Cost Optimization
nodes.excerpt: Reserved Instances, Savings Plans, Spot, right-sizing, Graviton, auto scaling patterns, S3 lifecycle, NAT Gateway mitigation, data transfer costs, tagging, Cost Explorer.

nodes.label: Cloud Networking
nodes.excerpt: VPC, subnets, IGW, NAT Gateway, security groups, VPC peering (non-transitive), Transit Gateway (hub-and-spoke), VPN vs Direct Connect, PrivateLink, data transfer pricing.

nodes.label: Cloud Services
nodes.excerpt: AWS and GCP services: EC2/GCE, Lambda/Cloud Functions, S3/GCS, EBS, RDS/Aurora, DynamoDB/Firestore, ElastiCache, SQS/SNS/Pub-Sub, ECS/EKS/GKE/Cloud Run, CloudFront, ALB/NLB, IAM, CloudWatch.

nodes.label: Anchoring Bias
nodes.excerpt: Anchoring bias happens when an initial number, example, design, salary, estimate, or framing point pulls later judgments toward it. The antidote is independent estimation, reference classes, ranges, and deliberately generating alternatives before discussing the first proposal.

nodes.label: Availability Heuristic
nodes.excerpt: The availability heuristic estimates likelihood or importance from how easily examples come to mind. Vividness, recency, emotion, media exposure, and personal experience can make mentally loud events feel statistically common.

nodes.label: Baader-Meinhof Phenomenon (Frequency Illusion)
nodes.excerpt: The Baader-Meinhof phenomenon, or frequency illusion, happens when newly noticed information suddenly seems everywhere. The frequency often has not changed; attention, salience, and memory have changed.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Cognitive Biases — Complete Reference
nodes.label: Cognitive Biases — Complete Reference
nodes.excerpt: Cognitive biases are systematic distortions in attention, memory, probability judgment, social interpretation, and decision-making. They are not signs of stupidity; they are useful shortcuts misapplied outside the context where they work.

nodes.label: Cognitive Load Theory
nodes.excerpt: Cognitive load theory explains learning through limited working memory and long-term schema construction. Good teaching reduces extraneous load, sequences intrinsic complexity, and uses examples until the learner has enough schemas to reason independently.

nodes.label: Confirmation Bias
nodes.excerpt: Confirmation bias is the tendency to seek, interpret, and remember evidence in ways that protect an existing belief. It becomes most dangerous when the belief is tied to identity, public commitment, sunk cost, or a plan Kenny already wants to be true.

nodes.label: Decision Fatigue
nodes.excerpt: Decision fatigue is the drop in decision quality that can follow repeated choices, uncertainty, cognitive effort, stress, or self-control demands. The practical fix is not heroic willpower; it is reducing low-value choices with defaults, templates, checklists, and timing important decisions for higher-energy windows.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Dual Process Theory (System 1 vs System 2)
nodes.label: Dual Process Theory (System 1 vs System 2)
nodes.excerpt: Dual process theory contrasts fast, automatic, pattern-based cognition with slower, effortful, analytical reasoning. The System 1/System 2 frame is useful for deciding when to trust intuition, but it is a simplification, not two literal brains.

nodes.label: Dunning-Kruger Effect
nodes.excerpt: The Dunning-Kruger effect is a calibration problem: low skill can impair the ability to judge skill because the same knowledge needed to perform well is needed to notice mistakes. The useful takeaway is not mocking beginners; it is building feedback loops that align confidence with evidence.

nodes.label: Fundamental Attribution Error
nodes.excerpt: Fundamental attribution error is overexplaining other people's behavior by personality while underweighting situation, incentives, workload, information, and constraints. The practical correction is to ask what context would make the behavior understandable before jumping to character judgment.

nodes.label: Heuristics in Decision-Making
nodes.excerpt: Heuristics are decision shortcuts that trade precision for speed, effort, and robustness. They are powerful when matched to the environment and dangerous when treated as universal laws.

nodes.label: In-Group - Out-Group Bias
nodes.excerpt: In-group and out-group bias makes people grant nuance, trust, and good intent to 'us' while flattening 'them' into stereotypes. It can emerge from arbitrary labels, intensify under competition, and distort hiring, politics, communities, and engineering team conflict.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Inductive vs Deductive vs Abductive Reasoning
nodes.label: Inductive vs Deductive vs Abductive Reasoning
nodes.excerpt: Deductive reasoning derives necessary conclusions from premises, inductive reasoning generalizes from observations, and abductive reasoning infers the best explanation. Strong problem-solving uses all three while respecting what each can and cannot prove.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Logical Fallacies — Full List
nodes.label: Logical Fallacies — Full List
nodes.excerpt: Logical fallacies are recurring flaws in argument structure, relevance, evidence, or framing. They are useful for repairing reasoning, but often misused as debate labels that end conversation instead of clarifying the actual weak point.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Loss Aversion (Prospect Theory — Kahneman and Tversky)
nodes.label: Loss Aversion (Prospect Theory — Kahneman and Tversky)
nodes.excerpt: Loss aversion is the tendency for losses relative to a reference point to feel more powerful than equivalent gains. In Kahneman and Tversky's prospect theory, people evaluate outcomes relative to reference points with diminishing sensitivity and different risk attitudes for gains versus losses.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Mental Models — Full Reference
nodes.label: Mental Models — Full Reference
nodes.excerpt: Mental models are reusable simplifications that help explain systems, predict outcomes, and make decisions. A good model library is plural: incentives, bottlenecks, feedback loops, opportunity cost, base rates, compounding, tradeoffs, and second-order effects all catch different failure modes.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Nudge Theory (Thaler and Sunstein)
nodes.label: Nudge Theory (Thaler and Sunstein)
nodes.excerpt: Nudge theory, associated with Richard Thaler and Cass Sunstein, studies how choice architecture changes behavior without removing options. Defaults, friction, ordering, reminders, and framing can help people follow their own goals or manipulate them into serving someone else's.

nodes.label: Optimism Bias
nodes.excerpt: Optimism bias is overestimating the likelihood of favorable outcomes and underestimating risk for oneself, one's team, or one's plan. It is useful for motivation but dangerous when it replaces base rates, premortems, and contingency planning.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Overfitting in Human Reasoning
nodes.label: Overfitting in Human Reasoning
nodes.excerpt: Overfitting in human reasoning is building a rule, identity, or explanation too tightly around limited examples, noise, or recent experience. Like ML overfitting, it feels insightful in-sample and fails when applied to new situations.

nodes.label: Planning Fallacy
nodes.excerpt: The planning fallacy is underestimating time, cost, and blockers because the inside view imagines an ideal path. The strongest countermeasure is the outside view: compare against similar past work, then add explicit risk buffers.

nodes.label: Recency Bias
nodes.excerpt: Recency bias overweights the latest information relative to older but still relevant evidence. It can distort performance reviews, debugging, investing, dating, sports judgment, self-evaluation, and product decisions unless records or rolling windows restore context.

nodes.label: Status Quo Bias
nodes.excerpt: Status quo bias is preferring the current state because it is familiar, default, or already installed. It can protect stability, but it also lets outdated routines, tools, architectures, and assumptions survive without being re-chosen.

nodes.id: Resources/Cognitive Science & Biases/Streetlight Effect (Looking Where It Is Easy)
nodes.label: Streetlight Effect (Looking Where It Is Easy)
nodes.excerpt: The streetlight effect is searching where evidence is easiest to collect instead of where the answer is most likely to be found. It shows up in dashboards, debugging, research, productivity metrics, and any environment where measurable data crowds out important but harder-to-see causes.

nodes.label: Sunk Cost Fallacy
nodes.excerpt: The sunk cost fallacy is continuing because of unrecoverable past investment rather than future expected value. It is powered by loss aversion, identity, public commitment, and the desire to make past effort feel meaningful.

nodes.label: Survivorship Bias
nodes.excerpt: Survivorship bias happens when analysis focuses only on examples that passed a selection filter while missing failures that disappeared. It makes success stories, startup advice, investing backtests, fitness transformations, and career narratives look more repeatable than they are.

nodes.label: Active Listening
nodes.excerpt: Active listening means deliberately understanding content, emotion, and context before responding. It uses attention, reflection, clarifying questions, summaries, and checked understanding to improve trust, coaching, research, negotiation, and conflict resolution.

nodes.id: Resources/Communication & Writing/Async vs Sync Communication — When to Use Each
nodes.label: Async vs Sync Communication — When to Use Each
nodes.excerpt: Async communication is best for durable context, updates, and low-urgency decisions; sync communication is best for ambiguity, conflict, urgency, creative exchange, and relationship repair. The right mode depends on complexity, emotional stakes, urgency, and need for a record.

nodes.label: Conflict Resolution Strategies
nodes.excerpt: Conflict resolution turns disagreement into clearer facts, interests, boundaries, options, and decisions. Useful strategies include de-escalation, active listening, separating observations from interpretations, naming shared goals, and choosing the right forum.

nodes.label: Email Etiquette and Efficiency
nodes.excerpt: Efficient email makes triage and response easy through specific subjects, an early ask, concise context, clear owners, and appropriate recipients. Bad email externalizes thinking cost onto the reader.

nodes.id: Resources/Communication & Writing/Explaining Complex Ideas Simply (The Curse of Knowledge)
nodes.label: Explaining Complex Ideas Simply (The Curse of Knowledge)
nodes.excerpt: The curse of knowledge makes experts forget what beginners do not know. Clear explanation starts from the audience's current model, uses concrete examples, introduces vocabulary gradually, layers complexity, and checks understanding.

nodes.label: Negotiation Fundamentals (BATNA, ZOPA)
nodes.excerpt: Negotiation is agreement-seeking under different interests. Core tools include BATNA, reservation price, ZOPA, anchors, interests vs positions, and traded concessions; preparation is mostly improving alternatives and understanding the other side's constraints.

nodes.label: Non-Violent Communication (NVC — Marshall Rosenberg)
nodes.excerpt: Non-Violent Communication structures hard conversations around observations, feelings, needs, and requests. It reduces blame by separating what happened from evaluation, while still allowing clear boundaries and specific asks.

nodes.label: Persuasion — Cialdini's Six Principles
nodes.excerpt: Cialdini's six persuasion principles explain influence shortcuts: reciprocity, commitment/consistency, social proof, authority, liking, and scarcity. They are useful for ethical communication and for recognizing manipulation.

nodes.id: Resources/Communication & Writing/Presentation Design (Assertion-Evidence vs Bullet Slides)
nodes.label: Presentation Design (Assertion-Evidence vs Bullet Slides)
nodes.excerpt: Assertion-evidence presentation design replaces topic titles and bullet clutter with a clear slide claim supported by visual evidence. Each slide should make one point that the audience can understand quickly.

nodes.id: Resources/Communication & Writing/Public Speaking — Fear and Techniques
nodes.label: Public Speaking — Fear and Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Public speaking fear is a normal evaluation-threat response, not proof of inability. Skill improves through audience-focused structure, out-loud rehearsal, controlled breathing, exposure, pauses, and recovery plans for mistakes.

nodes.label: Rhetoric — Logos, Ethos, Pathos
nodes.excerpt: Rhetoric studies how communication persuades an audience through reason, credibility, emotion, arrangement, and style. Logos, ethos, and pathos are not tricks; they explain why facts need trust and relevance to land.

nodes.label: Storytelling with Data
nodes.excerpt: Storytelling with data turns analysis into a decision-ready argument through audience focus, chart choice, context, annotation, sequencing, and visual emphasis. The goal is not decorative charts; it is making the important comparison impossible to miss.

nodes.label: Technical Writing Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: Technical writing helps a specific reader understand, operate, troubleshoot, or decide about a system. Good docs define audience and task, lead with purpose, separate facts from rationale, include examples, and stay maintained or get deleted.

nodes.label: The Pyramid Principle (Barbara Minto)
nodes.excerpt: The Pyramid Principle leads with the answer, then supports it with grouped, logically ordered arguments. It is useful for executive communication because busy readers need orientation before evidence.

nodes.label: Writing Clearly — Principles (Williams, Pinker)
nodes.excerpt: Clear writing reduces reader effort by making agents, actions, structure, and context explicit. Principles from Joseph Williams and Steven Pinker emphasize concrete subjects, strong verbs, old-to-new information flow, coherence, and empathy for what the reader does not yet know.

nodes.id: Resources/Communication & Writing/Writing for Developers — Docs, READMEs, Commit Messages
nodes.label: Writing for Developers — Docs, READMEs, Commit Messages
nodes.excerpt: Developer writing includes READMEs, docs, commit messages, PR descriptions, comments, runbooks, and ADRs. Good developer writing preserves intent and tradeoffs so future maintainers can safely use, review, debug, and change the system.

nodes.label: DSA Practice
nodes.excerpt: DSA patterns by category (arrays, trees, graphs, DP, backtracking, binary search) with spaced repetition notes.

nodes.label: Data Engineering
nodes.excerpt: ELT vs ETL, data lake (Parquet, Iceberg, Medallion architecture), dbt, Airflow/Dagster, Snowflake/BigQuery/Redshift, Spark, Flink, Kinesis, Glue, CDC/Debezium, reverse ETL.

nodes.label: A-B Testing Design
nodes.excerpt: A/B testing uses randomized assignment to estimate whether a product or system change caused a metric difference. Good design specifies the unit of randomization, primary metric, guardrails, sample size, exposure rules, and stopping plan before launch.

nodes.label: Anomaly Detection Methods
nodes.excerpt: Anomaly detection flags observations that do not fit expected behavior, but the definition of expected behavior depends on seasonality, context, and false-alarm cost. Good systems combine statistical baselines, model scores, alert grouping, and human-readable context instead of treating every outlier as an incident.

nodes.label: Bayesian Statistics
nodes.excerpt: Bayesian statistics updates explicit prior beliefs with observed evidence to produce a posterior distribution. Its strength is decision-making under uncertainty; its risk is that priors, likelihood assumptions, and approximations can quietly drive the answer.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Causal Inference (Correlation vs Causation)
nodes.label: Causal Inference (Correlation vs Causation)
nodes.excerpt: Causal inference estimates what would happen under an intervention, not just which variables move together. It depends on design: randomized experiments when possible, or explicit assumptions through DAGs, matching, difference-in-differences, instrumental variables, regression discontinuity, and sensitivity analysis.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Clustering Algorithms (K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical)
nodes.label: Clustering Algorithms (K-Means, DBSCAN, Hierarchical)
nodes.excerpt: Clustering groups unlabeled data by a chosen similarity measure, so the result is only as meaningful as the representation and distance metric. K-means assumes compact centroid-shaped clusters, DBSCAN assumes density-separated regions, and hierarchical clustering exposes nested structure without committing to one k upfront.

nodes.label: Cohort Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Cohort analysis groups users or entities by a shared start condition and tracks behavior over age/time so aggregate growth does not hide retention quality. It is essential for activation, retention, lifecycle, subscription, and product-change analysis.

nodes.label: Confidence Intervals
nodes.excerpt: Confidence intervals put uncertainty around an estimate by describing what repeated sampling would capture with a chosen frequency. They are more decision-useful than naked point estimates because width, direction, and practical effect size are visible.

nodes.label: Dashboard Design Principles
nodes.excerpt: Dashboard design turns metrics into an operational decision surface for a specific audience. Good dashboards expose current status, trend, target, freshness, definitions, and drill-down paths; bad dashboards collect charts without a decision loop.

nodes.label: Data Cleaning Strategies
nodes.excerpt: Data cleaning makes raw data reliable by profiling, validating, standardizing, deduplicating, and documenting assumptions. The goal is not to make data look neat; it is to preserve meaning while preventing silent errors from contaminating dashboards, experiments, and models.

nodes.label: Data Lake Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Data lake architecture stores raw, semi-structured, and curated data in cheap object storage, then adds metadata, table formats, governance, and transformation layers so it remains queryable. Without catalogs, partitioning, quality checks, and ownership, a lake becomes a data swamp.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Data Normalization and Scaling
nodes.label: Data Normalization and Scaling
nodes.excerpt: Data normalization and scaling transform numeric features so units, skew, and magnitude do not dominate models for accidental reasons. Scaling is essential for distance-based methods, regularized regression, neural nets, and gradient optimization, but tree models often need it less.

nodes.label: Data Warehousing Concepts
nodes.excerpt: Data warehousing organizes operational data into consistent analytical models: facts, dimensions, star schemas, marts, lineage, tests, and governed definitions. Its value is shared truth: teams can ask product and business questions without rebuilding conflicting metrics from raw tables.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Dimensionality Reduction (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP)
nodes.label: Dimensionality Reduction (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP)
nodes.excerpt: Dimensionality reduction compresses high-dimensional data for visualization, denoising, speed, or modeling. PCA preserves linear variance, t-SNE emphasizes local neighborhoods for plots, and UMAP builds a neighborhood graph that often keeps more global structure while still being visualization-first.

nodes.label: ETL vs ELT
nodes.excerpt: ETL transforms data before loading it into the target store; ELT loads raw data first and transforms it inside the warehouse or lakehouse. The right choice depends on privacy, governance, cost, compute location, reprocessing needs, and who owns transformation logic.

nodes.label: Feature Engineering
nodes.excerpt: Feature engineering turns raw events, attributes, text, and timestamps into model inputs that expose useful signal without leaking the answer. It is where domain knowledge enters the model: recency, frequency, ratios, windows, encodings, interactions, and point-in-time correctness often matter more than algorithm choice.

nodes.label: Feature Selection Methods
nodes.excerpt: Feature selection removes irrelevant, redundant, expensive, unstable, or leaky variables so models generalize and remain explainable. The critical rule is that selection must happen inside validation folds; selecting on the full dataset leaks information and overstates performance.

nodes.label: Funnel Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Funnel analysis measures how users progress through an ordered sequence toward a goal, exposing step-level conversion, drop-off, and segment differences. Correct funnels require exact event definitions, identity resolution, ordering rules, time windows, and guardrails so conversion gains do not hide lower-quality users.

nodes.label: Handling Missing Data
nodes.excerpt: Handling missing data starts by asking why the value is absent: random loss, conditional missingness, collection bias, optional behavior, or system failure. Deletion, imputation, missingness indicators, and model-aware methods each encode assumptions that can bias metrics and models if used blindly.

nodes.label: Hypothesis Testing
nodes.excerpt: Hypothesis testing asks whether observed data would be unusually extreme if a null hypothesis were true. A valid test requires predeclared hypotheses, an appropriate test statistic, assumptions about sampling, and interpretation that separates statistical significance from practical importance.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Metrics and KPIs — How to Choose
nodes.label: Metrics and KPIs — How to Choose
nodes.excerpt: Choosing metrics and KPIs means choosing incentives: what the team will notice, optimize, and trade off. Strong metrics have precise definitions, clear ownership, decision relevance, guardrails, and resistance to gaming.

nodes.label: OLAP vs OLTP
nodes.excerpt: OLTP systems run live business transactions with low-latency correctness, while OLAP systems scan and aggregate historical data for analysis. The architecture boundary matters because analytical queries can lock, slow, or distort production systems if run against transactional databases directly.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/P-values and Statistical Significance
nodes.label: P-values and Statistical Significance
nodes.excerpt: A p-value is the probability of data at least this extreme assuming the null hypothesis and test assumptions are true. Statistical significance is a thresholding convention, not evidence that an effect is large, causal, replicable, or worth acting on.

nodes.label: Probability Theory Basics
nodes.excerpt: Probability theory is the grammar of uncertainty: events, random variables, distributions, expectation, conditioning, independence, and Bayes' rule. The practical skill is spotting which assumptions make a probability calculation valid, especially independence, stationarity, and base rates.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Regression Analysis (Linear, Logistic, Polynomial)
nodes.label: Regression Analysis (Linear, Logistic, Polynomial)
nodes.excerpt: Regression models an outcome as a function of input features, using coefficients to predict, explain, or estimate associations. Linear, logistic, and polynomial regression are powerful because they are interpretable, but their coefficients only mean what the data design and assumptions allow.

nodes.label: SQL Window Functions
nodes.excerpt: SQL window functions compute rankings, lag comparisons, cumulative totals, and rolling metrics across related rows while keeping row-level detail. The key mental model is partition, order, and frame: which rows are related, how they are sequenced, and which neighbors each calculation can see.

nodes.label: SQL for Analytics
nodes.excerpt: SQL for analytics turns relational tables into metric answers, but correctness depends on grain, joins, filters, time windows, and denominators. The practical skill is writing queries that match the metric definition and include sanity checks before anyone makes a decision from the result.

nodes.id: Resources/Data Science & Analytics/Statistics Fundamentals (Mean, Median, Mode, Std Dev)
nodes.label: Statistics Fundamentals (Mean, Median, Mode, Std Dev)
nodes.excerpt: Statistics fundamentals compress a dataset into center, spread, shape, and uncertainty. The useful move is not memorizing mean/median/std-dev; it is knowing what each statistic assumes, how outliers and skew distort it, and when percentiles or distribution plots tell the truth better.

nodes.label: Survival Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Survival analysis models time until an event while correctly handling censored observations that have not yet experienced the event. It is the right tool for churn, reliability, conversion delay, relapse, retention, and any question where timing matters as much as occurrence.

nodes.label: Time Series Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Time series analysis studies ordered observations where trend, seasonality, autocorrelation, lag, and regime shifts matter. The key discipline is respecting time: random splits, naive averages, and context-free anomaly rules often fail because observations are not independent.

nodes.label: Visualization Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: Visualization best practices make comparisons truthful and fast to read: choose the chart for the question, preserve honest scale, label units and definitions, show uncertainty when it matters, and use color intentionally. A chart is good when it reduces misinterpretation, not when it merely looks polished.

nodes.label: dbt (Data Build Tool)
nodes.excerpt: dbt turns warehouse SQL transformations into versioned, dependency-aware, tested models. It brings software-engineering discipline to analytics: modular SQL, lineage through ref(), source freshness, schema tests, documentation, environments, and CI.

nodes.label: Data Structures and Algorithms Patterns
nodes.excerpt: LeetCode/NeetCode pattern guide covering arrays, hash maps, two pointers, sliding windows, heaps, graphs, union find, backtracking, DP, and tradeoffs.

nodes.label: Database Internals Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: deeper database internals: pages, WAL, MVCC, vacuum/bloat, indexes, query planner, isolation anomalies, locks, replication, backups, partitioning, distributed SQL.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/Database Backups and Point-in-Time Recovery
nodes.label: Database Backups and Point-in-Time Recovery
nodes.excerpt: Backups are only real if restores work; point-in-time recovery combines a base backup with transaction logs to recover to a chosen moment before corruption, deletion, or outage. Design around RPO, RTO, restore drills, retention, encryption, and ownership.

nodes.label: Database Change Data Capture (CDC)
nodes.excerpt: Change Data Capture reads committed database changes, often from transaction logs, and publishes them to downstream systems such as Kafka, warehouses, caches, and search indexes. CDC reduces dual-write bugs but introduces ordering, schema evolution, replay, delete handling, and consumer idempotency responsibilities.

nodes.label: Database Connection Pooling (pgBouncer, SQLAlchemy)
nodes.excerpt: Connection pooling reuses a bounded set of database sessions so app traffic does not overwhelm the database with connection setup and idle backends. The sizing problem is global: app replicas, workers, ORMs, pgBouncer mode, query latency, and database max connections all multiply together.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/Database Indexing Strategies (B-Tree, Hash, GIN, GiST)
nodes.label: Database Indexing Strategies (B-Tree, Hash, GIN, GiST)
nodes.excerpt: Index strategy matches data structures to query predicates: B-tree for equality/range/order, hash for equality, GIN for containment and terms, GiST for generalized spatial/range searches, plus partial/expression/covering indexes for workload-specific shortcuts. Every index should pay rent by serving real queries.

nodes.label: Database Normalization (1NF through BCNF)
nodes.excerpt: Normalization decomposes relational tables so each fact depends on the right key and update anomalies do not leak into application code. The useful version is practical: identify functional dependencies, split mixed-grain tables, and denormalize only with documented maintenance rules.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/Full-Text Search (PostgreSQL FTS, Elasticsearch, Typesense)
nodes.label: Full-Text Search (PostgreSQL FTS, Elasticsearch, Typesense)
nodes.excerpt: Full-text search uses inverted indexes, tokenization, normalization, ranking, and filters so users can find relevant documents beyond exact matches. PostgreSQL FTS is good for integrated basic search, Typesense for simple product search UX, and Elasticsearch/OpenSearch for larger distributed search and analytics needs.

nodes.label: NoSQL — Document Stores (MongoDB)
nodes.excerpt: Document stores such as MongoDB store JSON-like documents and optimize for aggregate-oriented reads, flexible schema evolution, and horizontal scaling patterns. The design skill is choosing embed vs reference based on access patterns, document growth, consistency needs, and index shape.

nodes.label: NoSQL — Graph Databases (Neo4j)
nodes.excerpt: Graph databases such as Neo4j make relationships first-class with nodes, typed edges, properties, and traversal-oriented queries. They fit fraud rings, recommendations, permissions, dependency graphs, and knowledge graphs when relationship depth is the hard part.

nodes.label: NoSQL — Key-Value Stores (Redis)
nodes.excerpt: Key-value stores optimize for direct lookup by key; Redis extends that model with in-memory data structures, TTLs, atomic operations, persistence modes, and replication. The practical distinction is whether Redis holds derived/cacheable state or source-of-truth state that needs durability guarantees.

nodes.label: NoSQL — Wide-Column Stores (Cassandra)
nodes.excerpt: Wide-column stores like Cassandra distribute data by partition key and order rows by clustering columns, giving high write throughput and availability for known query patterns. The design rule is query-first modeling: duplicate data deliberately so each table answers one access pattern efficiently.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/ORM Patterns and Pitfalls (N+1 Problem, Lazy Loading)
nodes.label: ORM Patterns and Pitfalls (N+1 Problem, Lazy Loading)
nodes.excerpt: ORMs map tables and relationships into application objects, improving ergonomics while hiding generated SQL, query count, transaction boundaries, and loading behavior. The classic N+1 problem happens when lazy relationship access inside a loop emits one extra query per row.

nodes.label: Optimistic vs Pessimistic Locking
nodes.excerpt: Optimistic locking lets concurrent work proceed and rejects stale writes at commit time, while pessimistic locking blocks conflicting work earlier with database locks. Choose based on contention, conflict cost, user experience, and whether retries are safe.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/PostgreSQL — Advanced Features (JSONB, Arrays, Extensions)
nodes.label: PostgreSQL — Advanced Features (JSONB, Arrays, Extensions)
nodes.excerpt: PostgreSQL combines a strong relational core with JSONB, arrays, generated columns, partial indexes, full-text search, and extensions like PostGIS and pg_trgm. The pragmatic lesson is to use these features when they preserve one reliable system of record, but keep queryability, indexing, and constraints explicit.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/Redis Patterns (Caching, Pub Sub, Job Queues, Rate Limiting)
nodes.label: Redis Patterns (Caching, Pub Sub, Job Queues, Rate Limiting)
nodes.excerpt: Redis supports caching, pub/sub, streams, lightweight queues, counters, locks, and rate limiting through fast atomic data-structure operations. The core design question is durability: cache misses are fine, lost jobs or lost money are not.

nodes.label: Relational Database Design
nodes.excerpt: Relational database design models durable facts as tables, keys, relationships, and constraints so correctness is enforced by the database, not only by application code. The practical skill is choosing the right grain for each table and making invariants explicit with keys, foreign keys, uniqueness, checks, and transactions.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/SQL — Indexes and Query Plans (EXPLAIN)
nodes.label: SQL — Indexes and Query Plans (EXPLAIN)
nodes.excerpt: Indexes create alternate access paths, and EXPLAIN shows how the optimizer plans to use them. The practical skill is matching index shape to query predicates, sort order, joins, and selectivity, then comparing estimated rows to actual rows.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/SQL — Joins (Inner, Left, Right, Full, Cross)
nodes.label: SQL — Joins (Inner, Left, Right, Full, Cross)
nodes.excerpt: SQL joins combine rows by matching predicates, usually primary-key to foreign-key relationships. The main practical risks are accidental row multiplication, predicates in the wrong clause, and aggregating after joining at the wrong grain.

nodes.label: SQL — Subqueries and CTEs
nodes.excerpt: Subqueries and common table expressions express multi-step SQL logic inside one statement. Use them to name query stages, test existence, pre-aggregate at the right grain, and traverse hierarchies, but verify with EXPLAIN because readability can hide repeated work or materialization.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/SQL — Transactions and ACID Properties
nodes.label: SQL — Transactions and ACID Properties
nodes.excerpt: Transactions group database changes so coupled state moves atomically and concurrent work sees controlled versions of reality. ACID is practical engineering: atomic commits, constraint-preserving state, isolation levels with known anomalies, and durability through logs/checkpoints/backups.

nodes.id: Resources/Database Systems/SQLite — Use Cases and Limits
nodes.label: SQLite — Use Cases and Limits
nodes.excerpt: SQLite is an embedded relational database: the app links the engine and stores the database in a local file. It is excellent for local-first apps, mobile, CLIs, tests, edge deployments, and small services, but write concurrency, network access, and multi-process operations define its limits.

nodes.label: Time Series Databases (InfluxDB, TimescaleDB)
nodes.excerpt: Time series databases optimize timestamped measurements with high ingest, time-range queries, compression, retention, downsampling, and label/tag filtering. The modeling challenge is controlling cardinality while preserving the dimensions needed for debugging and analysis.

nodes.label: Databases
nodes.excerpt: deep dive: ACID, MVCC, indexes (B-tree/hash/composite/covering), query planning, N+1, connection pooling, SQL vs all 4 NoSQL types, PostgreSQL specifics.

nodes.label: Accessibility in Design (WCAG 2.1, 2.2)
nodes.excerpt: Accessibility in design makes products perceivable, operable, understandable, and robust for people with varied vision, hearing, motor, cognitive, and situational needs. Surface this when discussing WCAG 2.1/2.2, keyboard use, contrast, forms, focus, screen readers, motion sensitivity, touch targets, or why accessibility is product quality rather than a post-launch checklist.

nodes.label: Color Theory Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Color in product design communicates hierarchy, state, brand, affordance, and emotion through hue, saturation, value, contrast, and semantic meaning, but it must preserve accessibility and never carry meaning alone. Surface this when choosing UI palettes, status colors, contrast, data colors, or design-system tokens.

nodes.id: Resources/Design & UX/Dark Patterns — A Catalog of What to Avoid
nodes.label: Dark Patterns — A Catalog of What to Avoid
nodes.excerpt: Dark patterns are interface choices that manipulate, deceive, coerce, or exhaust users into decisions that serve the business against user intent. Surface this when metrics improve through confusion, friction asymmetry, hidden costs, forced continuity, privacy obstruction, or emotional pressure.

nodes.label: Design Handoff to Engineering
nodes.excerpt: Design handoff to engineering is a collaboration contract that captures intent, states, responsive behavior, tokens, assets, accessibility requirements, edge cases, and tradeoffs before implementation. Surface this when engineers are guessing from mockups, designs omit empty/error/loading states, or production diverges from Figma.

nodes.label: Design Systems Overview
nodes.excerpt: A design system is shared product infrastructure: tokens, components, patterns, content rules, accessibility guidance, and governance that keep design and engineering decisions coherent at scale. Surface this when teams are duplicating UI, arguing over one-off styles, drifting from accessibility, or treating a component library as the whole system.

nodes.label: Designing for Emotion
nodes.excerpt: Designing for emotion shapes how users feel through clarity, control, trust, momentum, safety, delight, identity, and recovery from mistakes; emotional design supports goals when incentives are aligned and becomes manipulation when they are not. Surface this when a product technically works but makes users anxious, stupid, unsafe, or unmotivated.

nodes.label: Figma Workflow and Components
nodes.excerpt: Figma workflow quality comes from organized files, frames, auto layout, components, variants, variables, libraries, naming, comments, and design-system alignment that make design maintainable and buildable. Surface this when design files become hard to review, component states drift, or engineering handoff depends on screenshots and guessing.

nodes.label: Gestalt Principles in Design
nodes.excerpt: Gestalt principles describe how people perceive visual elements as organized wholes: proximity, similarity, continuity, closure, figure-ground, common region, and connectedness create perceived relationships before users read labels. Surface this when layout feels visually noisy, grouped incorrectly, or hard to scan.

nodes.label: Grid Systems in Design
nodes.excerpt: Grid systems create layout order through columns, margins, gutters, spacing scales, baselines, and responsive breakpoints, making interfaces easier to scan and implement consistently. Surface this when layouts feel arbitrary, cards do not align, responsive behavior is unclear, or design handoff lacks structure.

nodes.label: Human-Centered Design Process
nodes.excerpt: Human-centered design is an iterative learning process that starts from real human needs, contexts, constraints, and behaviors, then moves through research, synthesis, ideation, prototyping, testing, and implementation. Surface this when a product conversation jumps straight to features, visuals, or engineering before validating the user's problem.

nodes.label: Information Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Information architecture organizes content, navigation, labels, search, hierarchy, and relationships so users can find and understand what they need. Surface this when a product feels confusing even though individual screens look polished, especially in dashboards, docs, settings, knowledge bases, and multi-step workflows.

nodes.id: Resources/Design & UX/Interaction Design Principles (Fitts's Law, Hick's Law, Jakob's Law)
nodes.label: Interaction Design Principles (Fitts's Law, Hick's Law, Jakob's Law)
nodes.excerpt: Interaction design principles explain how target size, distance, choice count, familiarity, feedback, constraints, and error recovery shape usability; Fitts's Law, Hick's Law, and Jakob's Law are practical heuristics, not magic formulas. Surface this when diagnosing tiny buttons, crowded menus, unfamiliar patterns, slow feedback, or preventable user errors.

nodes.label: Micro-Interactions Design
nodes.excerpt: Micro-interactions are small product moments made of a trigger, rules, feedback, and loops/modes; they clarify status, prevent errors, and build trust at the instant users act. Surface this when discussing toggles, saves, uploads, validation, hover states, loading, empty states, or why tiny UX details change perceived quality.

nodes.label: Mobile-First Design
nodes.excerpt: Mobile-first design starts from constrained screens, touch input, performance budgets, context switching, and core task priority, then progressively enhances for larger screens. Surface this when a product is cluttered, desktop-biased, hard to use one-handed, or missing responsive behavior.

nodes.label: Motion Design Principles
nodes.excerpt: Motion design uses time, easing, duration, choreography, and continuity to explain state changes, guide attention, provide feedback, and preserve spatial context. Surface this when animation needs to clarify what changed rather than add decorative movement or create accessibility problems.

nodes.label: The Aesthetic-Usability Effect
nodes.excerpt: The aesthetic-usability effect is the tendency for users to perceive attractive designs as easier to use, especially early, because visual harmony can increase trust and tolerance. Surface this when explaining why polish matters, while guarding against the mistake of using beauty to mask broken task flow or accessibility.

nodes.label: Typography Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Typography controls readability, hierarchy, tone, density, and scanability through typeface, size, weight, line length, line height, spacing, alignment, and contrast. Surface this when UI text feels cluttered, hard to scan, off-brand, inaccessible, or visually inconsistent.

nodes.label: UX Research Methods Overview
nodes.excerpt: UX research methods answer different kinds of uncertainty: interviews and field studies explain why, usability tests reveal task friction, surveys estimate prevalence, analytics show behavior at scale, and diary studies capture longitudinal context. Surface this when a team needs to choose a research method instead of defaulting to surveys or vibes.

nodes.label: User Personas
nodes.excerpt: User personas are evidence-based summaries of behavioral patterns, goals, contexts, constraints, and decision criteria, not fictional demographic posters. Surface this when a team needs shared user memory, but challenge personas that are invented, stale, or less actionable than jobs-to-be-done scenarios.

nodes.label: Wireframing vs Prototyping
nodes.excerpt: Wireframes answer structure and hierarchy questions, while prototypes answer flow, interaction, feedback, and realism questions; fidelity should match the risk being tested. Surface this when deciding whether to sketch, mock up, prototype, or build before the team wastes effort polishing the wrong artifact.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/AWS Core Services (EC2, S3, Lambda, RDS, VPC)
nodes.label: AWS Core Services (EC2, S3, Lambda, RDS, VPC)
nodes.excerpt: AWS core services form the vocabulary for most cloud architectures: EC2 (virtual machines with instance families for different workloads), S3 (durable object storage with storage classes, lifecycle rules, and presigned URLs), Lambda (serverless functions — see Lambda note for depth), RDS/Aurora (managed relational databases with Multi-AZ failover and read replicas), and VPC (private networking with public/private subnets, NAT Gateway, and Security Groups). IAM roles tie everything together by granting workload-specific permissions without static credentials.

nodes.label: Ansible Basics
nodes.excerpt: Ansible automates configuration management, server setup, and operational tasks over SSH (agentless). Core concepts: inventory (which hosts to target), playbooks (ordered YAML list of plays/tasks), modules (apt, copy, template, service, user), roles (packaged reusable task sets), and handlers (actions triggered by changes, run once at end of play). Ansible vs Terraform: Ansible configures machines after they exist; Terraform provisions the machines. Ansible Vault encrypts secrets within playbooks. Key idempotency rule: well-written Ansible can run multiple times safely — same result each run.

nodes.label: Bash Scripting
nodes.excerpt: Bash scripting automates shell workflows — CI steps, deployments, system administration, cron jobs, and glue code. The critical discipline: `set -euo pipefail` catches failures, always quote variables with double quotes (unquoted vars break on spaces and globs), use arrays not space-delimited strings. Bash is excellent glue for composing programs; it's a poor choice for complex logic, parsing, or data transformation — use Python when scripts exceed ~50 lines or need anything beyond simple conditionals and loops.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Blue-Green vs Canary vs Rolling Deployments
nodes.label: Blue-Green vs Canary vs Rolling Deployments
nodes.excerpt: Three main deployment strategies for reducing release risk: blue-green (maintain two identical environments, switch traffic instantly, fast rollback, expensive), canary (route small % to new version, validate, incrementally increase, best for risk reduction but requires observability), rolling (replace instances one-by-one, no extra infrastructure cost, slowest rollback). The critical constraint all three share: database migrations must be backward-compatible — the new and old code will run simultaneously during the transition, so schemas must support both. Argo Rollouts automates canary progression via Prometheus-queried golden signals.

nodes.label: CI-CD Pipeline Design
nodes.excerpt: CI/CD pipeline design automates the path from commit to production. The core principle: build the artifact once, test it repeatedly, promote the same binary through environments. A good pipeline catches failures fast (< 10 min for most feedback), enforces quality gates without slowing engineers, and produces an auditable record of every change. Stage ordering is deliberate: run cheap fast checks first (lint/typecheck/unit), expensive slow checks later (integration/e2e/security). Every stage output — test results, scan reports, artifact digests — is traceability data.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Cloud Providers Overview (AWS, GCP, Azure)
nodes.label: Cloud Providers Overview (AWS, GCP, Azure)
nodes.excerpt: AWS dominates on service breadth (200+ services) and ecosystem maturity. GCP leads on data analytics (BigQuery), Kubernetes heritage (GKE was the original managed k8s), and AI/ML infrastructure (TPUs, Vertex AI). Azure wins in Microsoft enterprise environments (Active Directory integration, Office 365 ecosystem, hybrid on-premises). In practice: if your company has no existing commitment, AWS is the default for startups; Azure if Microsoft-heavy enterprise; GCP if data/ML-primary. Multi-cloud is real but expensive in operational overhead — most teams are better served by using one provider well.

nodes.label: Cloudflare Services Overview
nodes.excerpt: Cloudflare is a global edge network (300+ PoPs) that proxies traffic between users and origins, adding CDN caching, DDoS protection, WAF, TLS termination, and edge compute (Workers). Key operational concepts: orange cloud (proxied, Cloudflare IP) vs gray cloud (DNS only, origin IP exposed); SSL modes (Full Strict required for real TLS security); Workers as V8-isolate edge compute (0ms cold start, 50ms CPU limit); Cloudflare Tunnel for exposing private services without opening firewall ports; R2 for S3-compatible storage with no egress fees.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Cron Jobs and Task Scheduling
nodes.label: Cron Jobs and Task Scheduling
nodes.excerpt: Cron runs scheduled work at defined intervals via a 5-field expression (minute/hour/day/month/weekday). The critical requirement: jobs must be idempotent (safe to run twice) and observable (failures must alert, not silently vanish). Two major footguns: overlap (two instances running simultaneously because the first didn't finish) and DST (jobs at 2am disappear or run twice in November). Modern alternatives: systemd timers (better logging), Kubernetes CronJob, AWS EventBridge, and Celery Beat for Python. Dead Man's Snitch pattern: job pings a URL on success; alert fires if ping doesn't arrive.

nodes.label: Database Migrations Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: Database migrations are schema or data changes applied to a live system — the risk is that the database is shared by multiple running application versions simultaneously. The expand-contract (parallel-change) pattern is the safe default: add the new thing, migrate data in batches, switch reads, remove the old thing — never in one atomic step. PostgreSQL DDL locks are the main footgun: ALTER TABLE holds an AccessExclusiveLock that blocks all reads and writes for the duration. CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY is the fix. Always run migrations in CI, always have rollback SQL ready.

nodes.label: Disk I-O Optimization
nodes.excerpt: Disk I/O bottlenecks manifest as high iowait (vmstat wa column), high device utilization (iostat %util), or long await times. Sequential I/O is ~100x faster than random on HDDs; SSDs narrow but don't eliminate this gap. The page cache is your first line of defense: reads from hot data cost nothing if it's in RAM. Database fsync is the main durability-vs-latency trade-off. Tools: iostat -x (device stats), iotop -o (per-process), fio (synthetic benchmarks). NFS and EBS add network latency on top of disk latency — never benchmark them as if they're local.

nodes.label: Error Budgets
nodes.excerpt: An error budget is the allowed unreliability derived from an SLO: budget = (1 - SLO) × window. A 99.9% SLO allows 43.8 minutes of downtime per month. Burn rate measures how fast the budget is consumed; multi-window alerts (1h fast burn + 6h slow burn) catch both sudden outages and slow degradations. When budget is exhausted, reliability work takes priority over features. The mechanism that makes reliability a product decision, not an engineering complaint.

nodes.label: GitHub Actions Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: GitHub Actions runs workflows on repository events using a jobs-and-steps model on hosted or self-hosted runners. Key skills: trigger design (push/PR/schedule/workflow_dispatch), matrix builds for multi-version testing, dependency caching, OIDC for secretless cloud auth, and security hardening (minimal permissions, pinned actions, fork PR isolation). Reusable workflows and composite actions reduce duplication across repositories. The most dangerous footgun: pull_request_target running attacker-controlled code with repo secrets.

nodes.label: GitOps Principles
nodes.excerpt: GitOps manages infrastructure and application delivery by storing desired state in Git and using controllers to reconcile live systems toward that state. The reconciliation is pull-based (controller watches Git, applies changes) rather than push-based (CI pipeline writes directly to cluster). Key implementations: Argo CD (app-centric, UI-first) and Flux (operator pattern, GitOps toolkit). Core problems: secret management (never commit raw secrets — use Sealed Secrets or SOPS), multi-environment management (separate repos or branches), and emergency changes that bypass Git (must be reconciled back).

nodes.label: Infrastructure as Code (IaC)
nodes.excerpt: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) defines cloud resources in versioned files instead of clicking through consoles. The core value is reproducibility, auditability, and review. The dominant tools: Terraform (declarative HCL, provider-agnostic, state-file-based), Pulumi (same model in Python/TypeScript/Go), AWS CDK (TypeScript/Python generating CloudFormation), CloudFormation (AWS-native, verbose YAML/JSON). The hardest IaC problems are not syntax — they're state management (remote state with locking), secrets handling (never in state or code), drift remediation (when real infra diverges), and import workflows (adding existing resources to IaC control).

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Kubernetes YAML — Deployment, Service, Ingress
nodes.label: Kubernetes YAML — Deployment, Service, Ingress
nodes.excerpt: Kubernetes desired-state manifests: Deployment (manages replicated pods, rolling update strategy, readiness/liveness probes), Service (stable network identity for pods via label selectors — ClusterIP for internal, LoadBalancer for external), Ingress (HTTP routing by host/path to Services via ingress controller). The critical mechanic connecting everything: label selectors. Deployment's spec.selector must match pod template labels; Service's selector must match pod labels. A mismatch silently routes to zero pods. Resource requests/limits affect scheduling and throttling.

nodes.label: Lambda Functions and FaaS
nodes.excerpt: Lambda is AWS's Function-as-a-Service platform. Each invocation runs in an isolated Firecracker micro-VM (execution environment). Key mechanics: cold vs warm starts (cold = new environment spin-up, 200ms–5s depending on runtime/package; warm = reuse existing environment), memory config controls CPU proportionally (1769 MB = 1 vCPU), and retry behavior varies by trigger type (SQS: at-least-once with DLQ; API GW: sync, no retry). Design rules: small single-purpose functions, idempotent handlers, connection pooling via RDS Proxy, and correlation IDs in every log line.

nodes.label: Linux Command Line Essentials
nodes.excerpt: Linux command line fluency is the highest-leverage infrastructure skill. Navigation (ls/find/fd), text processing (grep/awk/sed/cut/sort/uniq), process inspection (ps/top/lsof/ss), resource checking (df/du/free/vmstat), and redirection/pipes are the core toolkit. The composability model: small focused commands chained with pipes, each transforming text. Key survival commands in a production incident: dmesg (kernel events), journalctl -u (service logs), ss -tlnp (open ports), lsof -p (what files a process has open).

nodes.label: Log Aggregation (ELK Stack, Loki)
nodes.excerpt: Log aggregation centralizes logs from all services into a searchable store. ELK Stack (Elasticsearch + Logstash + Kibana): full-text indexed search, expensive but powerful for complex queries and log analytics. Loki (Grafana Labs): stores compressed streams indexed only by labels (not content), 10x cheaper, queries via LogQL. Key design decisions: structured JSON logs, correlation IDs in every line, retention windows, and sensitive data redaction before logs leave the application. Filebeat/Promtail ship logs from pods without sidecar overhead.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Memory Management in Operating Systems
nodes.label: Memory Management in Operating Systems
nodes.excerpt: OS memory management gives each process its own virtual address space backed by physical pages. Key concepts: page faults (minor = in cache, major = disk I/O), the page cache (free RAM used as disk cache — not wasted), swap (RAM overflow onto disk — causes severe latency), and OOM killer (kills processes when RAM is exhausted). In containers, cgroup memory limits OOM-kill pods even when the host has free RAM. Tools: free -m (overview), /proc/meminfo (detail), cat /proc/pid/status (per-process RSS), dmesg | grep -i 'killed' (OOM events).

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Observability — Logs, Metrics, Traces (The Three Pillars)
nodes.label: Observability — Logs, Metrics, Traces (The Three Pillars)
nodes.excerpt: Observability is the ability to understand a system's internal state from its external outputs — without deploying new code to answer every new question. Three signal types: logs (discrete events), metrics (numeric time-series), traces (request paths across services). The four golden signals (rate, errors, duration, saturation) are the minimum viable SLI set. Correlation IDs connect the pillars. High-cardinality labels are powerful but expensive — unbounded label values can blow up Prometheus memory.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/On-Call Culture and Incident Response
nodes.label: On-Call Culture and Incident Response
nodes.excerpt: On-call is the human operating layer for production systems. Healthy on-call has clear severity levels (P0–P3), defined roles (IC + comms lead + ops), fast alert-to-ack SLAs, a blameless culture, and measured toil. Unhealthy on-call is characterized by alert fatigue, absent runbooks, no incident commander role, and engineers too burned out to be effective. DORA research: on-call load is one of the top predictors of engineering team burnout.

nodes.label: OpenTelemetry
nodes.excerpt: OpenTelemetry (OTel) is the open-source standard for instrumenting distributed systems — it produces traces, metrics, and logs through a vendor-neutral API. Key components: API (instrument your code), SDK (configure exporters/samplers), Collector (pipeline — receive, process, export), semantic conventions (standardize attribute names). W3C traceparent header propagates trace context across service boundaries. Tail-based sampling catches slow/error traces that head-based sampling would discard. OTel auto-instrumentation covers 80% of coverage with zero code change.

nodes.label: Performance Profiling (CPU, Memory, I-O)
nodes.excerpt: Performance profiling finds where CPU, memory, I/O, or network time is actually spent — not where you guess. The discipline: measure first, then optimize. Linux tools: top/htop (overview), perf/py-spy (CPU flame graphs), strace (syscall trace), vmstat/iostat (system-level). Python: cProfile for function-level, memory_profiler for allocations. Flame graphs (Brendan Gregg) are the highest-ROI visualization — wide stacks show hot code. P99 latency matters more than average. EXPLAIN ANALYZE is the single most useful tool for database slowness.

nodes.label: Postmortem Writing
nodes.excerpt: A postmortem turns an incident into organizational learning. Structure: impact summary, timeline, contributing factors (not a single root cause), detection/response quality assessment, and concrete action items with owners and due dates. Blameless means the individual made a reasonable decision given their context — the system allowed a bad outcome. Good action items change the system; bad ones say 'be more careful.

nodes.label: Prometheus and Grafana
nodes.excerpt: Prometheus scrapes metrics from targets via HTTP pull (not push), stores labeled time-series, and queries them with PromQL. Grafana visualizes Prometheus data in dashboards and manages alerts. Key: four metric types (Counter/Gauge/Histogram/Summary), rate() for per-second rate from counters, histogram_quantile() for P99 latency, and the cardinality danger of unbounded label values. AlertManager routes alerts by severity/team. Recording rules pre-compute expensive queries for dashboard performance.

nodes.label: Reverse Proxies (Nginx, Caddy, Traefik)
nodes.excerpt: Reverse proxies receive client requests and forward to backends, adding TLS termination, load balancing, caching, header manipulation, and rate limiting. Nginx: mature, high-performance, complex config (server blocks, location blocks); best for fine-grained control. Caddy: automatic HTTPS with ACME, minimal config, ideal for simpler setups. Traefik: dynamic configuration from Docker labels or k8s CRDs — discovers services automatically; best in container environments. All three can terminate TLS, route by host/path, load balance, and forward headers.

nodes.label: Runbooks and Playbooks
nodes.excerpt: Runbooks are step-by-step operational procedures for specific symptoms or alerts. Playbooks are broader scenario flows (e.g., database failover, regional outage). Both reduce cognitive load during incidents by replacing improvisation with verified steps. A runbook is good when a junior engineer can follow it at 2am without asking for help. The main failure mode: stale runbooks that haven't been tested since they were written.

nodes.label: SLIs, SLOs, and SLAs
nodes.excerpt: SLIs measure service behavior (a PromQL expression), SLOs set reliability targets (99.9% over 30 days), SLAs are external contractual promises (weaker than SLO to leave headroom). The four golden signals map directly to SLI candidates: availability (error rate), latency (histogram_quantile), throughput (rate), saturation (resource %). Percentiles describe latency far better than averages — a P99 of 2s means 1% of users wait 2+ seconds. Time windows matter: 30-day rolling windows are more forgiving of short incidents than calendar-month windows.

nodes.label: SSH Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: SSH best practices: use ed25519 keys (not RSA-2048), protect private keys with passphrases, disable password auth in sshd_config, restrict to specific users/groups, use ProxyJump for bastion hosts. Modern alternative: AWS SSM Session Manager provides SSH-equivalent access without opening port 22 — no key management, full audit log in CloudTrail. For teams: SSH certificate authority (CA) is better than managing authorized_keys files — short-lived signed certs replace per-user key distribution.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/Secret Management (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
nodes.label: Secret Management (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager)
nodes.excerpt: Secret management stores credentials (DB passwords, API keys, TLS certs) centrally, controls access via policy, rotates automatically, and audits all reads. HashiCorp Vault: self-hosted, powerful dynamic secrets (generates short-lived DB credentials on demand), fine-grained policies, multiple auth backends. AWS Secrets Manager: managed, tight IAM integration, automatic rotation for RDS/Redshift/DocDB, cross-account access. AWS SSM Parameter Store: simpler, cheaper, good for config + non-sensitive parameters. The critical anti-patterns: secrets in environment variables (visible in process table), secrets in source code (git history is permanent), secrets in Docker image layers.

nodes.label: Serverless Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Serverless architecture builds systems from managed compute (Lambda/Cloud Run), managed data stores (DynamoDB, S3, SQS), and managed integrations (API Gateway, EventBridge) — eliminating server provisioning, capacity planning, and OS patching. The key tradeoffs: cold starts (100ms–2s depending on runtime and package size), execution limits (15 min max for Lambda), cost shape that's cheap for bursty workloads but expensive at sustained high throughput, and debugging complexity that increases as workflows span more managed services. Best fit: event-driven processing, API backends with spiky traffic, automation and glue logic, scheduled jobs.

nodes.label: Terraform Basics
nodes.excerpt: Terraform is HashiCorp's declarative IaC tool using HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language). Core concepts: providers (API adapters), resources (cloud objects), data sources (read existing infra), variables, outputs, locals, and modules. State is the critical operational concern — use S3+DynamoDB remote state with locking. Lifecycle rules (prevent_destroy, ignore_changes) guard against accidental destruction. count vs for_each for resource iteration. Common footguns: forgetting to review the plan before apply, state file leaking secrets, and renaming resources causing destroy+recreate.

nodes.label: VPN Architecture
nodes.excerpt: VPNs create encrypted network tunnels for two use cases: client VPN (remote employees → private network) and site-to-site VPN (offices/clouds connected). Protocol comparison: WireGuard is the modern default (fast, simple config, 4000 lines of code); OpenVPN is mature and audited but slower; IPsec/IKEv2 is used for site-to-site (AWS VPN, Cisco). Split tunnel routes only private traffic through VPN (better performance); full tunnel routes everything (better security control). The modern alternative: Cloudflare Access + Tunnel or Tailscale replace VPNs for web-based internal tools without routing all traffic.

nodes.id: Resources/DevOps & Infrastructure/systemd — Service and Process Management
nodes.label: systemd — Service and Process Management
nodes.excerpt: systemd is PID 1 on most Linux servers — it starts all processes, supervises services, manages dependencies, logs to journald, and runs timers. A service unit file defines the command, user, restart policy, resource limits, and security hardening for any long-running process. Key commands: systemctl start/stop/restart/status/enable/disable and journalctl -u service-name. Drop-in files (/etc/systemd/system/service.d/) let you override vendor unit files without modifying them — critical for packages you don't own.

nodes.label: Distributed Systems
nodes.excerpt: partial failures, CAP/PACELC, consistency models, replication (single-leader/multi-leader/leaderless), Raft consensus, 2PC vs Saga, vector clocks, gossip protocol.

nodes.label: Docker
nodes.excerpt: OverlayFS layer system, Dockerfile best practices (multi-stage builds), networking (bridge/host), volumes, container runtime architecture (dockerd→containerd→runc→kernel).

nodes.label: Dynamic Programming Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: focused DP guide covering state design, memoization vs tabulation, 1D/2D DP, knapsack, LIS, interval DP, tree DP, bitmask DP, common traps, and practice method.
