nodes.label: Behavioral Economics Overview
nodes.excerpt: Behavioral economics adds realistic psychology to economic choice: bounded rationality, loss aversion, defaults, framing, present bias, social norms, and choice architecture. Surface this when better information alone is not changing behavior, especially around habits, money, products, health, or policy nudges.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Bitcoin — Blockchain, Proof of Work, Mining
nodes.label: Bitcoin — Blockchain, Proof of Work, Mining
nodes.excerpt: Bitcoin is a decentralized settlement system that uses hash-linked blocks, proof-of-work mining, difficulty adjustment, cryptographic signatures, and economic incentives to maintain a ledger without a central operator. Surface this when discussing censorship resistance, mining energy, probabilistic finality, monetary scarcity, or why Bitcoin trades throughput for settlement assurance.

nodes.label: Cryptocurrency — Technical Overview
nodes.excerpt: Cryptocurrency combines public-key cryptography, distributed ledgers, consensus rules, wallets, incentives, and sometimes smart contracts to coordinate digital ownership without a traditional central operator. Surface this when separating the technical architecture from speculation, fraud, custody risk, governance, regulation, and real use cases.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/DeFi Basics (Protocols, Liquidity Pools, Yield)
nodes.label: DeFi Basics (Protocols, Liquidity Pools, Yield)
nodes.excerpt: DeFi uses smart contracts for trading, lending, borrowing, stablecoins, derivatives, and liquidity provision, but headline yield is compensation for smart-contract, oracle, liquidity, leverage, governance, and counterparty-like risks. Surface this when discussing AMMs, liquidity pools, stablecoins, yield farming, or why 'decentralized' does not mean risk-free.

nodes.label: Externalities and Market Failure
nodes.excerpt: Externalities occur when private decisions impose costs or benefits on third parties that prices do not reflect, creating market failure even when buyers and sellers act rationally. Surface this when discussing pollution, climate, vaccines, education, congestion, data privacy, or why market prices can miss social costs.

nodes.label: GDP and Economic Indicators
nodes.excerpt: GDP measures final production, but economic health needs a dashboard: real GDP, GDP per capita, unemployment, labor-force participation, inflation, productivity, wages, debt, inequality, and sentiment. Surface this when a conversation treats one number as a full verdict on how people are actually doing.

nodes.label: Game Theory Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Game theory studies strategic situations where each actor's best move depends on what others do, revealing why incentives, information, commitment, and repetition matter. Surface this when discussing competition, negotiation, pricing, arms races, platform behavior, cooperation, or why individual rationality can produce bad group outcomes.

nodes.label: Globalization — Benefits and Costs
nodes.excerpt: Globalization integrates economies through trade, capital, migration, supply chains, technology, and institutions, creating aggregate gains while concentrating adjustment costs. Surface this when discussing cheap goods, offshoring, supply-chain fragility, trade policy, inequality, industrial policy, or why efficiency and resilience can conflict.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Inflation — Types, Causes, and Effects
nodes.label: Inflation — Types, Causes, and Effects
nodes.excerpt: Inflation is a sustained rise in the general price level, caused by demand pressure, supply shocks, money and credit conditions, expectations, or sector bottlenecks. Surface this when discussing prices, wages, central banks, cost of living, or why inflation episodes need diagnosis before policy.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Interest Rates and Central Banks (The Fed, ECB)
nodes.label: Interest Rates and Central Banks (The Fed, ECB)
nodes.excerpt: Central banks influence the economy through policy rates, liquidity, balance sheets, bank reserves, credit conditions, and expectations, but they operate with delays and imperfect control. Surface this when discussing the Fed, ECB, inflation, recessions, mortgages, asset prices, or why rate changes affect more than borrowing costs.

nodes.label: Microeconomics vs Macroeconomics
nodes.excerpt: Microeconomics studies choices, prices, firms, incentives, and individual markets; macroeconomics studies aggregates like GDP, inflation, unemployment, growth, money, and policy. Surface this when an argument jumps from individual logic to economy-wide outcomes or confuses household budgeting with government policy.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Monetary Policy vs Fiscal Policy
nodes.label: Monetary Policy vs Fiscal Policy
nodes.excerpt: Monetary policy works through central-bank rates, liquidity, credit, and expectations; fiscal policy works through government spending, taxes, transfers, and borrowing. Surface this when policy debates mix up who has which tool, how fast each tool works, and whether the problem is demand, supply, distribution, or financial stability.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/NFTs — What They Are and Why They Matter (or Don't)
nodes.label: NFTs — What They Are and Why They Matter (or Don't)
nodes.excerpt: NFTs are unique blockchain tokens that can represent a record, claim, collectible, or access right, but token ownership is not the same as copyright, custody, permanence, or legal ownership. Surface this when discussing digital scarcity, creator markets, game assets, provenance, speculative bubbles, or why the token and the referenced asset must be separated.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Network Effects in Economics (Metcalfe's Law)
nodes.label: Network Effects in Economics (Metcalfe's Law)
nodes.excerpt: Network effects occur when a product becomes more valuable as adoption grows, creating self-reinforcing demand, defensibility, lock-in, and sometimes market concentration. Surface this when evaluating platforms, social products, marketplaces, protocols, developer ecosystems, or whether a company has a real moat rather than ordinary scale.

nodes.label: Platform Economics (Multi-Sided Markets)
nodes.excerpt: Platform economics studies businesses that coordinate multiple participant groups whose value depends on each other, such as buyers and sellers, riders and drivers, creators and audiences, or developers and users. Surface this when discussing marketplaces, app stores, social platforms, take rates, trust systems, subsidies, liquidity, and who captures platform value.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Prisoner's Dilemma and Nash Equilibrium
nodes.label: Prisoner's Dilemma and Nash Equilibrium
nodes.excerpt: The Prisoner's Dilemma shows how dominant individual incentives can create collectively worse outcomes, while Nash equilibrium names a strategy profile where no player wants to deviate alone. Surface this when discussing cooperation failures, arms races, climate policy, workplace incentives, platform competition, or why trust and enforcement change outcomes.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Public Goods and the Free Rider Problem
nodes.label: Public Goods and the Free Rider Problem
nodes.excerpt: Public goods are non-rival and non-excludable, so private markets tend to underfund them because people can benefit without paying. Surface this when discussing open source, infrastructure, research, clean air, national defense, public health, or why valuable systems often need institutions beyond voluntary payment.

nodes.label: Rent-Seeking vs Wealth Creation
nodes.excerpt: Wealth creation expands real surplus through productivity, usefulness, innovation, or capacity; rent-seeking captures existing value through privilege, gatekeeping, artificial scarcity, regulatory capture, or market power. Surface this when judging businesses, policies, careers, monopolies, platforms, or whether an activity improves the world or just improves someone's claim on it.

nodes.label: Supply and Demand Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Supply and demand explain how prices coordinate buyers and sellers, but the model only works when you track elasticity, curve shifts, market power, information, and externalities. Surface this when discussing prices, shortages, wages, housing, product demand, inflation claims, or why a market outcome is not automatically fair.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Technology and Labor Markets (Automation and Jobs)
nodes.label: Technology and Labor Markets (Automation and Jobs)
nodes.excerpt: Technology changes labor markets by automating tasks, complementing workers, creating new demand, shifting bargaining power, and changing who captures productivity gains. Surface this when discussing AI job disruption, reskilling, wages, automation anxiety, or why task-level analysis beats blanket doom or optimism.

nodes.label: The Economics of Attention
nodes.excerpt: The economics of attention treats human attention as a scarce, rivalrous input monetized by platforms, advertisers, creators, and products, creating incentives for engagement, interruption, personalization, and habit formation. Surface this when discussing social media, focus, feeds, ads, notification design, creator economies, or why willpower problems are often market-design problems.

nodes.id: Resources/Economics & Finance/Universal Basic Income — Arguments For and Against
nodes.label: Universal Basic Income — Arguments For and Against
nodes.excerpt: Universal Basic Income is an unconditional cash floor that trades targeting efficiency for simplicity, autonomy, bargaining power, and reduced benefit cliffs. Surface this when discussing automation policy, poverty, welfare bureaucracy, labor incentives, inflation concerns, or the difference between pilot evidence and national-scale design.

nodes.label: Winner-Take-All Markets
nodes.excerpt: Winner-take-all and winner-take-most markets concentrate rewards when small advantages compound through network effects, scale economies, distribution, ranking systems, superstar dynamics, or platform control. Surface this when discussing tech giants, creators, careers, marketplaces, AI model leaders, or why fair competition can still end in concentration.

nodes.label: Economics and Personal Finance
nodes.excerpt: practical economics and personal finance primer covering incentives, opportunity cost, supply/demand, compounding, emergency funds, debt, index funds, retirement accounts, taxes, credit, lifestyle inflation, and career finance.

nodes.label: Engineering Judgment and Tool Tradeoffs
nodes.excerpt: practical guide to choosing tools by fit, failure modes, accepted tradeoffs, monitoring signals, and simpler alternatives.

nodes.label: Building in Public
nodes.excerpt: Building in public shares progress, decisions, metrics, lessons, and failures while creating a product. It can create trust, feedback, and distribution, but only works when public output compounds real execution instead of replacing it.

nodes.label: Cap Table Basics
nodes.excerpt: A cap table is the source of truth for company ownership across founders, employees, investors, option pools, SAFEs, notes, and share classes. It should model current ownership, fully diluted ownership, future financing, and exit waterfalls.

nodes.label: Community-Led Growth
nodes.excerpt: Community-led growth uses shared identity, peer learning, trust, and participation to support acquisition, retention, feedback, and advocacy. It works when members receive value from each other, not just from the company broadcasting into a branded channel.

nodes.label: Customer Discovery
nodes.excerpt: Customer discovery tests whether a specific customer segment has a painful problem, current workaround, buying trigger, budget, and decision process. Good discovery asks about recent behavior and concrete situations, not whether someone likes your idea.

nodes.label: Distribution is Everything
nodes.excerpt: Distribution is the system for reaching, persuading, selling to, and retaining the right customers. Product quality matters, but without a repeatable channel and message-market fit, even strong products can stall.

nodes.label: Equity and Dilution
nodes.excerpt: Equity is ownership; dilution is the percentage reduction that happens when a company issues new shares for capital, hiring, advisors, or convertible instruments. Dilution is acceptable when the new money or talent increases expected company value more than the ownership percentage falls.

nodes.label: Finding Product-Market Fit
nodes.excerpt: Product-market fit is evidence that a specific customer segment urgently values a product enough to retain, pay, expand, and pull it forward. It is measured through retention, usage intensity, willingness to pay, referrals, sales-cycle compression, and customer disappointment if the product disappeared.

nodes.label: Founder-Market Fit
nodes.excerpt: Founder-market fit is the founder's unfair alignment with a market through lived experience, domain expertise, relationships, credibility, taste, or obsession. It improves access and judgment, but it must still be validated through customer evidence.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/How VC Funds Work (Management Fees, Carry, Fund Size)
nodes.label: How VC Funds Work (Management Fees, Carry, Fund Size)
nodes.excerpt: VC funds raise capital from limited partners, invest through general partners, charge management fees, and earn carry on profits. Fund size and power-law returns explain why VCs push for huge markets, fast growth, and outcomes large enough to return the fund.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/Jobs to Be Done Framework
nodes.label: Jobs to Be Done Framework
nodes.excerpt: Jobs to Be Done frames customers as hiring products to make progress in a specific situation, with functional, emotional, and social dimensions. The useful output is a switching story: trigger, old solution, anxieties, desired outcome, and competing alternatives.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/MVP Design — What Minimum Actually Means
nodes.label: MVP Design — What Minimum Actually Means
nodes.excerpt: An MVP is the smallest credible experiment that tests a risky product or business assumption. Minimum means minimum learning vehicle, not minimum quality: the artifact must be good enough that the result reflects customer demand rather than prototype brokenness.

nodes.label: Pricing Strategy (Value-Based, Cost-Plus, Freemium)
nodes.excerpt: Pricing strategy decides how a company captures value, segments customers, funds service costs, and signals positioning. The main approaches are value-based, cost-plus, competitive, usage-based, tiered, subscription, and freemium, each with different unit-economic and behavioral consequences.

nodes.label: Solopreneurship and Indie Hacking
nodes.excerpt: Solopreneurship and indie hacking build small, profitable, software-enabled businesses with low overhead and direct customer revenue rather than venture-scale expectations. The operating system is focus: narrow market, simple product, owned distribution, automation, and cash discipline.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/Startup Funding (Bootstrapping, Angel, Pre-Seed, Seed, Series A)
nodes.label: Startup Funding (Bootstrapping, Angel, Pre-Seed, Seed, Series A)
nodes.excerpt: Startup funding choices define growth expectations, ownership dilution, control, runway, and exit pressure. Bootstrapping, angels, pre-seed, seed, and Series A each finance a different level of evidence: problem, product, traction, repeatable go-to-market, and scale.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/Startup vs Small Business — Key Differences
nodes.label: Startup vs Small Business — Key Differences
nodes.excerpt: Startups search for a repeatable, scalable business model under extreme uncertainty; small businesses usually execute a more known model with cash-flow discipline. The distinction matters because funding, growth targets, hiring, risk tolerance, and success metrics are different games.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/Term Sheets — Key Terms to Negotiate
nodes.label: Term Sheets — Key Terms to Negotiate
nodes.excerpt: A term sheet defines the economics and control rights of a startup financing: valuation, investment amount, option pool, liquidation preference, pro rata rights, board composition, protective provisions, vesting, and information rights. Headline valuation is only one variable; preferences and control terms decide outcomes under stress.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/The Art of the Pivot
nodes.label: The Art of the Pivot
nodes.excerpt: A pivot is an evidence-driven change to customer, problem, product, channel, revenue model, or positioning that preserves validated learning while abandoning a failing assumption. Good pivots are disciplined; random restarts are just thrashing.

nodes.label: The Lean Startup Method (Build-Measure-Learn)
nodes.excerpt: Lean Startup treats a startup as a sequence of experiments that test risky assumptions about customers, problems, solutions, channels, and economics. Build-measure-learn only works when the team defines the hypothesis, success threshold, and decision rule before building.

nodes.label: Timing a Market
nodes.excerpt: Market timing is the fit between an idea and external readiness: enabling technology, urgent pain, budget, regulation, cultural behavior, and distribution channels. The same product can be too early, too late, or right on time depending on whether customers are ready to change.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/Unit Economics (CAC, LTV, Payback Period, Churn)
nodes.label: Unit Economics (CAC, LTV, Payback Period, Churn)
nodes.excerpt: Unit economics measure whether each customer relationship produces gross profit after acquisition and service costs. Core formulas include CAC, gross-margin LTV, payback period, logo churn, revenue churn, net revenue retention, and contribution margin.

nodes.id: Resources/Entrepreneurship & Startups/Y Combinator — Lessons and Application Advice
nodes.label: Y Combinator — Lessons and Application Advice
nodes.excerpt: Y Combinator's durable advice is operational: make something people want, talk to users, launch quickly, measure growth, and explain the company clearly. A strong YC-style application is specific about customer, problem, product, traction, insight, team, and why now.

nodes.label: Character Arc Design
nodes.excerpt: Explains character arcs as change or resistance under pressure: want versus need, flaw, wound, lie, value conflict, choice, consequence, and revelation. Covers positive, negative, flat, tragic, redemptive, and ambiguous arcs with craft-level failure modes.

nodes.label: Cinematography Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Explains cinematography as visual meaning through framing, lens choice, camera movement, lighting, exposure, color, composition, depth, and point of view. Covers how shots control attention and emotion rather than merely making images look pretty.

nodes.id: Resources/Film & Storytelling/Editing Principles (Continuity, Montage, Jump Cut)
nodes.label: Editing Principles (Continuity, Montage, Jump Cut)
nodes.excerpt: Explains film editing as control of time, rhythm, attention, causality, and emotion through shot order. Covers continuity editing, 180-degree rule, match cuts, cutting on action, montage, jump cuts, Kuleshov effect, pacing, and why cuts create meaning.

nodes.label: Genre Conventions
nodes.excerpt: Explains genre conventions as audience contracts around tone, stakes, pacing, plot patterns, character types, setting, and payoff. Covers familiarity versus surprise, trope use, hybrid genres, subversion, and why breaking a convention works only when the storyteller understands what the audience came for.

nodes.id: Resources/Film & Storytelling/Narrative Structure (3-Act, Hero's Journey, Story Circle, Save the Cat)
nodes.label: Narrative Structure (3-Act, Hero's Journey, Story Circle, Save the Cat)
nodes.excerpt: Explains narrative structure frameworks as tools for managing setup, desire, escalation, crisis, transformation, and payoff. Compares three-act structure, Hero's Journey, Dan Harmon's Story Circle, and Save the Cat while emphasizing causal momentum over formula.

nodes.label: Screenplay Writing Basics
nodes.excerpt: Explains screenplay writing as visual, performable storytelling through scenes, action lines, dialogue, subtext, conflict, formatting, page economy, and cinematic implication. Emphasizes that a screenplay is a production blueprint, not a novel with dialogue margins.

nodes.label: The Pixar Storytelling Formula
nodes.excerpt: Explains the Pixar storytelling formula as a compact causality scaffold: once there was, every day, until one day, because of that, until finally. Shows how it tests baseline, disruption, escalation, consequence, and resolution without reducing storytelling to a corporate template.

nodes.id: Resources/Film & Storytelling/The Role of Conflict in Story
nodes.label: The Role of Conflict in Story
nodes.excerpt: Explains conflict as meaningful resistance between desire and outcome, not merely fighting. Covers external, internal, interpersonal, social, moral, and existential conflict; stakes, escalation, choice, revelation, and the failure mode of conflict without consequence.

nodes.id: Resources/Film & Storytelling/Why Certain Stories Resonate (Evolutionary Psychology of Narrative)
nodes.label: Why Certain Stories Resonate (Evolutionary Psychology of Narrative)
nodes.excerpt: Explains story resonance through simulated social learning, threat detection, status, attachment, betrayal, morality, identity, transformation, and meaning-making. Uses evolutionary psychology cautiously: stories may rehearse human problems, but art cannot be reduced to one adaptive function.

nodes.label: Worldbuilding Principles
nodes.excerpt: Explains worldbuilding as designing coherent constraints: geography, history, institutions, economics, technology, magic, ecology, culture, daily life, and power. Emphasizes that strong worlds feel bigger than the plot while still pressuring character choice, and warns against lore dumping.

nodes.label: FlashAttention
nodes.excerpt: GPU memory hierarchy, why standard attention is HBM-bound, tiling algorithm, online softmax, FA-2 and FA-3.

nodes.label: Formal Methods Agent Safety
nodes.excerpt: temporal logic specs, type systems, runtime monitors, NN verification (and its limits), probabilistic verification, neurosymbolic approaches.

nodes.label: Competitive Gaming Psychology
nodes.excerpt: Explains competitive gaming psychology through attention control, tilt, arousal, confidence, deliberate practice, teamwork, communication, recovery, and decision-making under pressure. Connects sports-psych concepts to ranked ladders, tournaments, mechanics, VOD review, scrims, and why emotional regulation determines whether skill appears in match.

nodes.label: Difficulty Scaling in Games
nodes.excerpt: Explains difficulty scaling as tuning challenge over time through teaching, testing, enemy behavior, resource pressure, level design, optional mastery, adaptive systems, and player-selected modes. Focuses on fairness perception, readable failure, rubber-banding risks, difficulty curves, and why challenge should feel earned rather than arbitrary.

nodes.label: Esports Industry Overview
nodes.excerpt: Explains esports as a hybrid of competitive sport, media product, software platform, publisher-owned IP, sponsorship business, streaming ecosystem, and fandom. Covers teams, leagues, tournaments, publishers, patch control, revenue sources, player careers, sustainability problems, and why esports economics differ from traditional sports.

nodes.label: Game Balance Design
nodes.excerpt: Explains game balance as tuning incentives so choices, challenges, rewards, economies, strategies, and skill expression stay fair and interesting. Covers dominant strategies, perceived fairness, asymmetry, metagames, buffs/nerfs, economy sinks/sources, telemetry, playtesting, and why perfect numerical equality is not the same as good balance.

nodes.label: Game Design Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Explains game design as the craft of shaping player behavior through goals, rules, feedback, challenge, agency, progression, and reward. Uses the mechanics-dynamics-aesthetics lens, player motivation, failure modes like dominant strategies and unclear feedback, and concrete examples Bobby can use when discussing why a game actually feels good.

nodes.label: Game Narrative Design
nodes.excerpt: Explains game narrative design as interactive storytelling through mechanics, agency, quests, world state, environmental storytelling, dialogue, pacing, and emergent systems. Covers linear vs branching vs systemic narrative, ludonarrative dissonance, player-authored moments, and why game stories work best when play carries meaning.

nodes.label: Game Physics Engines Overview
nodes.excerpt: Explains game physics engines as systems for believable motion, collision detection, collision response, constraints, rigid bodies, triggers, raycasts, and stable simulation. Emphasizes that games optimize for feel, consistency, and performance rather than perfect realism, with examples like tunneling, fixed timesteps, and character-controller tradeoffs.

nodes.label: Gaming and Flow State
nodes.excerpt: Explains why games produce flow: clear goals, immediate feedback, challenge-skill balance, agency, deep attention, and recoverable failure. Covers boredom/anxiety tuning, progression curves, multiplayer disruption, compulsive engagement boundaries, and how flow differs from grind or variable-reward capture.

nodes.id: Resources/Gaming/Monetization Models in Games (Gacha, Battle Pass, DLC)
nodes.label: Monetization Models in Games (Gacha, Battle Pass, DLC)
nodes.excerpt: Explains game monetization models as design incentives, not just pricing: premium, DLC, subscriptions, cosmetics, battle passes, ads, loot boxes, and gacha all shape progression, retention, trust, fairness, and content cadence. Highlights pay-to-win boundaries, FOMO, whales, live-service economics, and ethical design tradeoffs.

nodes.label: Open World Design
nodes.excerpt: Explains open world design as creating meaningful freedom through spatial structure, landmarks, traversal, density, pacing, systemic interaction, discovery, and consequence. Emphasizes scale versus density, curiosity loops, traversal feel, player-authored goals, and the failure mode of large maps filled with low-meaning checklist content.

nodes.label: Pathfinding Algorithms (A*, NavMesh)
nodes.excerpt: Explains game pathfinding as converting space into graphs or navigation meshes, then finding believable routes with A*, heuristics, costs, steering, replanning, and local avoidance. Connects algorithm correctness to level design, AI readability, performance budgets, dynamic obstacles, and why valid paths still need to look intentional.

nodes.label: Procedural Generation Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Explains procedural generation as controlled possibility-space design, not just randomness. Covers seeds, noise, grammars, constraint solving, templates, cellular automata, roguelike maps, terrain, loot tables, validation filters, authored constraints, and why generated content must be curated for pacing, fairness, and readability.

nodes.label: The Game Loop (Input, Update, Render)
nodes.excerpt: Explains the game loop as the runtime heartbeat that repeatedly reads input, advances simulation, and renders frames. Covers fixed vs variable timesteps, delta time, physics stability, input latency, update ordering, interpolation, and why responsive feedback is the engineering foundation of game feel.

nodes.id: Resources/Gaming/The Psychology of Loot Boxes and Variable Rewards
nodes.label: The Psychology of Loot Boxes and Variable Rewards
nodes.excerpt: Explains loot boxes and variable rewards through variable-ratio reinforcement, anticipation, dopamine reward prediction, near misses, scarcity, sunk cost, loss aversion, social comparison, and monetization ethics. Distinguishes healthy surprise from gambling-adjacent manipulation, with practical warning signs like opaque odds, FOMO, whales, and frictionless spending.

nodes.label: Geospatial
nodes.excerpt: geohashing (8-neighbor fix), quadtrees, S2/Hilbert curve, Haversine formula, Yelp-like proximity design, Uber-like live driver matching with Redis GEOSEARCH, H3 hexagons, map tile systems.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/ARM Architecture and the Embedded Computing Ecosystem
nodes.label: ARM Architecture and the Embedded Computing Ecosystem
nodes.excerpt: ARM is a RISC ISA licensed by Arm Holdings, powering virtually all mobile devices and a large portion of embedded systems worldwide. ARM's business model is licensing IP rather than making chips, which means dozens of companies (Apple, Qualcomm, Samsung, NVIDIA) implement the ISA with wildly different microarchitectures. Understanding ARM explains the mobile performance revolution and Apple Silicon's dominance.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/ASICs vs FPGAs vs CPUs — Design Tradeoffs and When to Use Each
nodes.label: ASICs vs FPGAs vs CPUs — Design Tradeoffs and When to Use Each
nodes.excerpt: CPUs are general-purpose and flexible; FPGAs are reconfigurable hardware that can implement custom logic; ASICs are fixed-function chips optimized for exactly one task. The tradeoffs are cost, performance, power, development time, and volume — understanding the decision matrix explains why Bitcoin mines use ASICs, why network switches run ASICs, why ML inference is moving from GPUs to NPUs, and why FPGAs are used in data centers.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Arduino vs Raspberry Pi — Architecture, Use Cases, and What to Pick
nodes.label: Arduino vs Raspberry Pi — Architecture, Use Cases, and What to Pick
nodes.excerpt: Arduino is a microcontroller platform — it boots directly into your code with no OS, millisecond boot time, and μA sleep modes. Raspberry Pi is a Linux single-board computer with GHz CPU, gigabytes of RAM, and a full OS. They're not competing products — they solve different problems, and the right choice is usually obvious once you know which model each represents.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/CPU Architecture (RISC vs CISC, ARM vs x86)
nodes.label: CPU Architecture (RISC vs CISC, ARM vs x86)
nodes.excerpt: CPU architecture defines the instruction set and execution design exposed to software, with RISC emphasizing simpler fixed-style instructions and CISC emphasizing richer instructions. Modern ARM and x86 chips blur the old divide internally, but the distinction still helps explain compatibility, power efficiency, and ecosystem tradeoffs.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/CPU Microarchitecture — Pipelines, Branch Prediction, Out-of-Order Execution
nodes.label: CPU Microarchitecture — Pipelines, Branch Prediction, Out-of-Order Execution
nodes.excerpt: CPU microarchitecture defines how instructions are executed inside a processor. Pipelines allow overlapping stages of multiple instructions; out-of-order execution reorders independent instructions to fill execution units; branch prediction speculatively executes future instructions before knowing if a branch is taken. These techniques explain most of modern CPU performance — and its vulnerabilities.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Chip Packaging Technologies (BGA, Flip-Chip, Chiplets, 2.5D-3D Stacking)
nodes.label: Chip Packaging Technologies (BGA, Flip-Chip, Chiplets, 2.5D-3D Stacking)
nodes.excerpt: Chip packaging connects a bare die to the outside world. BGA (Ball Grid Array) is the standard PCB-mountable package. Flip-chip reverses the die orientation so bumps connect directly to the substrate, reducing parasitics. Chiplets decompose a large monolithic die into smaller dies assembled in one package using 2.5D (silicon interposer) or 3D (die stacking with TSVs) integration. These advanced packaging techniques are now the primary path for scaling performance when transistor density improvements alone aren't enough.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Clock Signals, Crystal Oscillators, PLLs, and Timing
nodes.label: Clock Signals, Crystal Oscillators, PLLs, and Timing
nodes.excerpt: Digital systems are synchronous machines driven by clock signals. Crystal oscillators provide accurate, stable frequencies from piezoelectric resonance. PLLs (Phase-Locked Loops) multiply and divide that reference to generate the many clock frequencies a SoC needs. Clock quality — frequency accuracy, jitter, skew — directly affects whether a system's timing margins hold or fail.

nodes.label: DRAM vs SRAM
nodes.excerpt: DRAM and SRAM are volatile memory technologies with different tradeoffs: DRAM is dense and cheap enough for main memory, while SRAM is faster and more expensive, making it suitable for CPU caches. The distinction explains why computers have separate RAM and cache layers instead of one universal memory.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Digital Logic — Gates, Flip-Flops, Registers, and Finite State Machines
nodes.label: Digital Logic — Gates, Flip-Flops, Registers, and Finite State Machines
nodes.excerpt: Digital logic is the foundation of all computing hardware: Boolean gates implement logic functions, flip-flops store a single bit of state synchronized to a clock, registers group flip-flops to store a word, and finite state machines combine registers with combinational logic to implement any sequential behavior. Everything from a CPU to a USB controller is built from these primitives.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/DisplayPort vs HDMI vs Thunderbolt
nodes.label: DisplayPort vs HDMI vs Thunderbolt
nodes.excerpt: DisplayPort, HDMI, and Thunderbolt all carry video in different ecosystems: HDMI dominates TVs and consoles, DisplayPort is common for PCs and high-refresh monitors, and Thunderbolt carries video plus high-speed data over USB-C. Choosing between them depends on resolution, refresh rate, VRR, HDR, docking, cable quality, and device support.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) — Sources, Shielding, and FCC Certification
nodes.label: Electromagnetic Interference (EMI) — Sources, Shielding, and FCC Certification
nodes.excerpt: EMI (Electromagnetic Interference) is unwanted electromagnetic energy that disrupts other devices. Every digital circuit emits EMI — clocks, switching regulators, and fast edges are dominant sources. FCC Part 15 / CE certification sets legal limits on emissions before a product can be sold. EMI mitigation starts in PCB layout and schematic, and is tested in an EMC chamber. Failing an EMC test is expensive: $5–20K for a test session and weeks of redesign.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Electronic Testing Tools — Multimeter, Oscilloscope, Logic Analyzer, Spectrum Analyzer
nodes.label: Electronic Testing Tools — Multimeter, Oscilloscope, Logic Analyzer, Spectrum Analyzer
nodes.excerpt: Electronic test tools are how you observe what a circuit is actually doing, not what you think it should be doing. A multimeter measures DC voltage, current, and resistance. An oscilloscope shows signal waveforms over time. A logic analyzer captures digital protocol data. A spectrum analyzer shows frequency-domain power distribution. Each tool answers a different class of question.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/FPGAs — Reprogrammable Hardware and How HDL Compiles to Gates
nodes.label: FPGAs — Reprogrammable Hardware and How HDL Compiles to Gates
nodes.excerpt: FPGAs are integrated circuits with programmable logic fabric — millions of configurable look-up tables, flip-flops, DSP blocks, and memory that can be wired together to implement any digital circuit. HDL (Verilog/VHDL) is synthesized into a netlist, then placed and routed onto the FPGA's programmable fabric via bitstream. FPGAs sit between CPUs (flexible, slow) and ASICs (fast, fixed) — chosen when you need custom hardware logic without the cost and time of a chip tape-out.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/GPIO and Embedded Interfaces (I2C, SPI, UART, CAN, PWM)
nodes.label: GPIO and Embedded Interfaces (I2C, SPI, UART, CAN, PWM)
nodes.excerpt: GPIO, I2C, SPI, UART, CAN, and PWM are the basic communication and control interfaces on every microcontroller and SBC. Each protocol has distinct speed, wiring, and addressing tradeoffs. Knowing which protocol a sensor or peripheral uses — and why — is the core skill for embedded hardware selection and debugging.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/HDMI and DisplayPort Signal Standards — How Video Is Transmitted Digitally
nodes.label: HDMI and DisplayPort Signal Standards — How Video Is Transmitted Digitally
nodes.excerpt: HDMI and DisplayPort are the two dominant digital video standards. HDMI is consumer-focused, carries video + audio + CEC over a single cable, and is royalty-bearing. DisplayPort is industry/PC-focused, royalty-free, and carries video at higher bandwidth with more flexible multi-stream support. Both transmit pixel data as high-speed differential serial streams. USB-C with DisplayPort Alt Mode carries DP signal over the USB-C physical layer, enabling a single cable for power + data + video.

nodes.label: Home Lab Setup Guide
nodes.excerpt: A home lab is a small personal infrastructure environment for learning networking, servers, storage, containers, automation, and observability. A good setup starts with clear goals, modest hardware, backups, network segmentation, documentation, and services that are useful enough to maintain.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How CPUs Work (Fetch, Decode, Execute, Write-Back)
nodes.label: How CPUs Work (Fetch, Decode, Execute, Write-Back)
nodes.excerpt: CPUs execute programs through an instruction cycle: fetch an instruction, decode it, execute the operation, and write results back to registers or memory. This simple model explains the baseline, while modern CPUs add pipelining, caches, branch prediction, and out-of-order execution to keep work flowing.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How GPUs Work and Why ML Uses Them
nodes.label: How GPUs Work and Why ML Uses Them
nodes.excerpt: GPUs are built for massive parallel throughput: many smaller cores execute similar operations across large batches of data. Machine learning uses them because neural network training and inference are dominated by matrix operations that map well to GPU parallelism and high memory bandwidth.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How NAND Flash Works (SLC-MLC-TLC-QLC Cells, Wear Leveling, FTL Controllers)
nodes.label: How NAND Flash Works (SLC-MLC-TLC-QLC Cells, Wear Leveling, FTL Controllers)
nodes.excerpt: NAND flash stores data in floating-gate transistors by trapping charge. SLC stores 1 bit per cell with long endurance; TLC stores 3 bits with lower endurance but much higher density and lower cost. An FTL (Flash Translation Layer) in the SSD controller handles wear leveling, garbage collection, and the logical-to-physical address mapping that hides NAND's erase-before-write constraint from the OS. These mechanisms explain write amplification, endurance ratings (TBW), and why SSDs slow down under sustained writes.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How NPUs Work — Neural Processing Units in Modern SoCs (Apple M-series, Snapdragon)
nodes.label: How NPUs Work — Neural Processing Units in Modern SoCs (Apple M-series, Snapdragon)
nodes.excerpt: NPUs (Neural Processing Units) are fixed-function hardware accelerators for matrix multiply and activation operations — the core operations of neural network inference. They outperform CPUs and GPUs by 10–100× per watt for supported model types because they eliminate the general-purpose overhead of both. Apple's Neural Engine, Qualcomm Hexagon NPU, and Google's Edge TPU are the dominant mobile implementations. Understanding NPUs explains on-device AI performance claims and which operations actually use them.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How Semiconductors Work (Silicon, Doping, P-N Junctions, Transistors)
nodes.label: How Semiconductors Work (Silicon, Doping, P-N Junctions, Transistors)
nodes.excerpt: Semiconductors are materials whose conductivity can be precisely controlled by doping and electric fields. Silicon transistors — billions per chip — are the physical switches that make all digital computation possible. Understanding them explains why chips run hot, why process nodes matter, and what limits clock speeds.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How USB Works Internally (Host Controller, Enumeration, Transfer Types)
nodes.label: How USB Works Internally (Host Controller, Enumeration, Transfer Types)
nodes.excerpt: USB is a host-centric, tiered serial bus. The host controller (EHCI, xHCI) manages all device communication — devices can't initiate transactions. On plug-in, the host enumerates the device: assigns address, reads descriptors, loads driver. USB supports four transfer types with different bandwidth/latency guarantees. Understanding enumeration and transfer types explains USB device class drivers, why some USB hubs cause issues, and how USB audio/video latency works.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How a CPU is Fabricated (Photolithography, EUV, TSMC vs Intel Fabs, Yield)
nodes.label: How a CPU is Fabricated (Photolithography, EUV, TSMC vs Intel Fabs, Yield)
nodes.excerpt: CPU fabrication uses photolithography to pattern billions of transistors onto silicon wafers. TSMC, Intel, and Samsung compete at 3nm and below using EUV light sources. Yield — the fraction of good dies per wafer — determines cost and explains why cutting-edge chips are expensive and take time to ramp.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/How a GPU is Designed (Shader Cores, SIMD Lanes, Tensor Cores vs CPU Architecture)
nodes.label: How a GPU is Designed (Shader Cores, SIMD Lanes, Tensor Cores vs CPU Architecture)
nodes.excerpt: GPUs trade deep per-thread pipelines for thousands of simple shader cores executing the same operation across massive datasets in parallel. This SIMD/SIMT design makes them ideal for graphics, ML training, and simulation — but poor for branchy, latency-sensitive code. Tensor Cores (matrix multiply accelerators) are the key hardware reason why modern AI training happens on GPUs.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Memory Hierarchy (L1-L2-L3 Cache, RAM, Disk)
nodes.label: Memory Hierarchy (L1-L2-L3 Cache, RAM, Disk)
nodes.excerpt: The memory hierarchy arranges storage by speed, size, and cost: registers and caches are tiny and fast, RAM is larger and slower, and disks are much larger and slower still. Performance often depends on keeping hot data close to the CPU and avoiding unnecessary movement through the hierarchy.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Memory Technologies — LPDDR vs DDR5 vs HBM and Why It Matters for AI
nodes.label: Memory Technologies — LPDDR vs DDR5 vs HBM and Why It Matters for AI
nodes.excerpt: DRAM memory comes in multiple packaging and interface variants optimized for different compute contexts. DDR5 is the current desktop/server standard. LPDDR5X is the mobile variant with lower power but narrower bus. HBM3 is 3D-stacked DRAM directly bonded to the GPU die — delivering 10–20× the bandwidth of DDR5 at a fraction of the energy per bit. HBM bandwidth is why LLM inference throughput scales with memory bandwidth, not compute.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Microcontrollers vs Microprocessors — Where Each Belongs
nodes.label: Microcontrollers vs Microprocessors — Where Each Belongs
nodes.excerpt: A microcontroller (MCU) integrates CPU, RAM, flash, and peripherals on one chip — it boots and runs directly, with no OS required. A microprocessor (MPU) is a CPU that needs external RAM, storage, and peripherals, typically running a full OS like Linux. The choice drives the entire development stack: bare-metal C vs Linux userspace, interrupt-driven vs process-based, μA sleep modes vs GHz throughput.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Motherboard and Bus Architecture (PCIe, DMI)
nodes.label: Motherboard and Bus Architecture (PCIe, DMI)
nodes.excerpt: A motherboard connects CPU, memory, storage, expansion cards, firmware, power delivery, and I/O through buses and chipset links. PCIe lanes, chipset bandwidth, VRMs, and physical slots determine what hardware can be installed and how much throughput it can actually use.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/NAS and Home Storage (ZFS, RAID Levels)
nodes.label: NAS and Home Storage (ZFS, RAID Levels)
nodes.excerpt: A NAS centralizes storage on the network, often using RAID or ZFS to improve availability, integrity, and manageability. The crucial caveat is that RAID is not backup: it can survive some drive failures, but it does not protect against deletion, corruption, theft, fire, or ransomware by itself.

nodes.label: Networking Hardware (Routers, Switches, NICs)
nodes.excerpt: Networking hardware moves packets between devices and networks: NICs connect hosts, switches connect local devices, and routers connect networks. Understanding these roles helps diagnose home lab, Wi-Fi, latency, throughput, and segmentation problems.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Ohm's Law and Basic Circuit Analysis (KVL, KCL, Voltage Dividers, Thevenin)
nodes.label: Ohm's Law and Basic Circuit Analysis (KVL, KCL, Voltage Dividers, Thevenin)
nodes.excerpt: Ohm's Law (V = IR) and Kirchhoff's Laws are the foundational tools for analyzing any electrical circuit. Voltage dividers, Thévenin equivalents, and nodal analysis are the practical techniques that follow. These let you predict currents, voltages, and power dissipation in any resistive circuit without simulation.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Open Hardware and the Maker Movement (KiCad, OSHW, Raspberry Pi Foundation)
nodes.label: Open Hardware and the Maker Movement (KiCad, OSHW, Raspberry Pi Foundation)
nodes.excerpt: The open hardware movement applies open-source principles to physical electronics: schematics, PCB layouts, and firmware are published under permissive licenses so anyone can study, build, and modify them. KiCad is the dominant open-source EDA tool. OSHW (Open Source Hardware Association) defines the certification standard. The Raspberry Pi Foundation democratized accessible computing hardware. Together they've lowered the barrier for hardware design from 'requires proprietary tools and NDA supplier relationships' to 'anyone with a laptop and $50 for a PCB run.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/PCB Design Basics (Schematic Capture, Layout, Gerbers, Design Rule Checks)
nodes.label: PCB Design Basics (Schematic Capture, Layout, Gerbers, Design Rule Checks)
nodes.excerpt: PCB design turns an electrical schematic into a physical board with copper traces, component pads, and layer stackups. The flow goes: schematic capture → netlist → layout → DRC → Gerber files → fab. Understanding this pipeline explains why hardware bugs are expensive and how board-level signal integrity problems arise.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/PCIe Architecture Deep Dive (Lanes, Gen 4 vs Gen 5, Bifurcation)
nodes.label: PCIe Architecture Deep Dive (Lanes, Gen 4 vs Gen 5, Bifurcation)
nodes.excerpt: PCIe (Peripheral Component Interconnect Express) is the universal high-speed bus connecting CPUs to GPUs, NVMe SSDs, NICs, and accelerators. Each lane is a pair of differential serial links; bandwidth scales with lane count and generation. Gen 5 doubles Gen 4 per-lane bandwidth to 32 GT/s, giving a ×16 slot 64 GB/s. Bifurcation, link training, and power delivery are the practical engineering details that affect whether high-speed PCIe works reliably.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Power Management ICs — LDOs vs Buck-Boost Converters
nodes.label: Power Management ICs — LDOs vs Buck-Boost Converters
nodes.excerpt: Power management ICs regulate voltage and current to supply stable, noise-tolerant power to every circuit on a board. LDOs (Low Dropout regulators) are simple, low-noise, but inefficient — they burn excess voltage as heat. Switching regulators (buck, boost, buck-boost) use inductors and switches to convert voltage with 85–95% efficiency but add switching noise. Choosing between them comes down to efficiency, noise, cost, and the voltage conversion ratio needed.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Power Supplies and 80+ Efficiency Ratings
nodes.label: Power Supplies and 80+ Efficiency Ratings
nodes.excerpt: A computer power supply converts wall AC into stable DC rails for components, and 80+ ratings describe efficiency at specified loads. Good PSU selection is about wattage headroom, build quality, protections, connectors, noise, and efficiency rather than chasing the highest advertised number.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/RISC-V — Open Source ISA and Why It Matters
nodes.label: RISC-V — Open Source ISA and Why It Matters
nodes.excerpt: RISC-V is a free and open ISA developed at UC Berkeley, designed for clean modularity and permissive licensing. Unlike ARM, no royalties are required, and the entire specification is public. It's seeing rapid adoption in embedded controllers, custom accelerators, and academic research — and is increasingly competing with ARM in commercial silicon.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Resistors, Capacitors, Inductors — What They Do and How They Behave
nodes.label: Resistors, Capacitors, Inductors — What They Do and How They Behave
nodes.excerpt: Resistors, capacitors, and inductors are the three passive components that all analog and mixed-signal circuits are built from. Resistors dissipate energy as heat, capacitors store energy in an electric field, and inductors store energy in a magnetic field. Their frequency-dependent behavior (impedance) is what makes filters, oscillators, power converters, and matching networks possible.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/SSD vs HDD — How They Work Internally
nodes.label: SSD vs HDD — How They Work Internally
nodes.excerpt: HDDs store data magnetically on spinning platters, while SSDs store data electronically in NAND flash. SSDs are faster for random access and shock resistance, but have write endurance and controller behavior that matter for reliability and performance.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Signal Integrity — Impedance Matching, Transmission Lines, Reflections, Crosstalk
nodes.label: Signal Integrity — Impedance Matching, Transmission Lines, Reflections, Crosstalk
nodes.excerpt: Signal integrity (SI) is the study of how electrical signals behave as they travel through conductors. At high frequencies (above ~100–300MHz), PCB traces behave as transmission lines — not simple wires. Impedance mismatches cause reflections that degrade signal edges; crosstalk causes adjacent traces to interfere; simultaneous switching noise stresses power planes. SI is why high-speed PCB design is a discipline, not just routing.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Single-Board Computers (SBCs) — Key Components and Design (CPU, PMIC, RAM, eMMC)
nodes.label: Single-Board Computers (SBCs) — Key Components and Design (CPU, PMIC, RAM, eMMC)
nodes.excerpt: A single-board computer (SBC) integrates an SoC, DRAM, storage, and I/O onto one PCB — ready to run Linux out of the box. Understanding the components (SoC, PMIC, eMMC, LPDDR) explains performance characteristics, boot failures, thermal limits, and why some SBCs are more reliable than others for production use.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Soldering and PCB Assembly Fundamentals (Through-Hole vs SMD)
nodes.label: Soldering and PCB Assembly Fundamentals (Through-Hole vs SMD)
nodes.excerpt: Soldering is the process of joining metal components with a tin-silver alloy that melts at 183–250°C. Through-hole components use leads passing through drilled holes; SMD components sit on pads on the board surface. Understanding the thermal, mechanical, and process differences between them determines component selection, assembly method, and rework strategies for any electronics project.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Thermal Design and CPU-GPU Cooling
nodes.label: Thermal Design and CPU-GPU Cooling
nodes.excerpt: Thermal design manages heat from CPUs, GPUs, VRMs, storage, and cases so components can sustain performance without throttling or reliability problems. Cooling depends on heat transfer, cooler capacity, airflow, ambient temperature, fan curves, and dust management.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/Thermal Design — TDP, Heat Sinks, Heat Pipes, Vapor Chambers, Liquid Cooling
nodes.label: Thermal Design — TDP, Heat Sinks, Heat Pipes, Vapor Chambers, Liquid Cooling
nodes.excerpt: Thermal design is the engineering discipline of moving heat from silicon die to ambient air fast enough that junction temperature stays below the safe limit. TDP defines the expected heat to dissipate. Heat sinks, heat pipes, vapor chambers, and liquid cooling are the progressively more capable technologies for moving that heat. Thermal throttling is the firmware response to exceeding limits — it's what caps CPU/GPU performance in thin laptops, hot rooms, and poorly cooled cases.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/USB Standards Timeline (USB 2.0 through USB4)
nodes.label: USB Standards Timeline (USB 2.0 through USB4)
nodes.excerpt: USB standards evolved from simple peripheral connectivity into high-speed data, charging, display tunneling, and docking. The confusing part is that connector shape, data speed, charging support, and display capability are separate properties that marketing names often blur.

nodes.id: Resources/Hardware & Computing/VLSI Design — From RTL to Tape-Out (Synthesis, Place & Route, Timing Closure)
nodes.label: VLSI Design — From RTL to Tape-Out (Synthesis, Place & Route, Timing Closure)
nodes.excerpt: VLSI design is the process of turning a hardware description (RTL) into a physical chip layout ready for fabrication. Key stages are synthesis (RTL → gate netlist), place & route (gates → physical locations and wire connections), and timing closure (verifying all signals meet clock deadlines). A tape-out is the final delivery of GDS files to the fab.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Back Health for Desk Workers
nodes.label: Back Health for Desk Workers
nodes.excerpt: Back health for desk workers is about movement variability and capacity, not fear of sitting. Ergonomics, walking, trunk/hip strength, sleep, stress management, and early attention to red flags matter more than holding one perfect posture.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Blood Work Markers Worth Tracking
nodes.label: Blood Work Markers Worth Tracking
nodes.excerpt: Blood work can catch health patterns that subjective feeling misses, but markers need clinical context and trend interpretation. The useful approach is tracking a small set of high-signal panels with a clinician: lipids, glucose regulation, CBC, CMP, thyroid when indicated, iron/ferritin, vitamin D, and inflammatory markers when there is a reason.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Breathing Techniques (Box Breathing, Physiological Sigh, Wim Hof)
nodes.label: Breathing Techniques (Box Breathing, Physiological Sigh, Wim Hof)
nodes.excerpt: Breathing techniques are state-regulation tools, not magic physiology hacks. Box breathing steadies attention, the physiological sigh quickly downshifts arousal through long exhalation, and Wim Hof-style hyperventilation/breath holds are intense enough to require strict safety boundaries.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Cardio Types — HIIT vs LISS vs Zone 2
nodes.label: Cardio Types — HIIT vs LISS vs Zone 2
nodes.excerpt: HIIT, LISS, and Zone 2 are different cardio tools, not competing religions. HIIT trains high-end capacity with high fatigue cost, LISS builds easy movement volume, and Zone 2 develops sustainable aerobic metabolism near the first lactate/ventilatory threshold.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Cold Exposure — Benefits and Protocols
nodes.label: Cold Exposure — Benefits and Protocols
nodes.excerpt: Cold exposure is an optional stress tool that can improve alertness, cold tolerance, and perceived soreness, but broad longevity and recovery claims are often overmarketed. Use conservative protocols, avoid breath holds in water, and do not place cold immersion immediately after hypertrophy-focused lifting if muscle growth is the goal.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Compound Lifts (Squat, Deadlift, Bench Press, OHP)
nodes.label: Compound Lifts (Squat, Deadlift, Bench Press, OHP)
nodes.excerpt: Compound lifts train coordinated force across multiple joints, which makes them efficient anchors for strength and muscle but also more technically sensitive than isolation work. Squat, deadlift, bench press, and overhead press are movement-pattern tools, not mandatory identity tests.

nodes.label: Deload Weeks
nodes.excerpt: A deload week deliberately reduces training stress so fatigue falls while skill and habit remain. It is most useful after hard blocks, repeated performance stalls, poor recovery signs, or schedule stress, and usually works by cutting volume more than movement practice.

nodes.label: Exercise Science Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Exercise science is the training version of systems engineering: apply a specific stress, recover from it, and the body adapts by changing muscle, tendons, heart, mitochondria, nervous system, and skill. The useful frame is stimulus-recovery-adaptation, with progressive overload and specificity constrained by fatigue, sleep, nutrition, injury history, and adherence.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Eye Strain and Screen Time (20-20-20 Rule)
nodes.label: Eye Strain and Screen Time (20-20-20 Rule)
nodes.excerpt: Digital eye strain usually comes from prolonged near focus, reduced blinking, glare, dry eyes, poor lighting, uncorrected vision issues, and long uninterrupted sessions. The 20-20-20 rule is a low-cost reset: every 20 minutes, look about 20 feet away for about 20 seconds.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Fasting Protocols for Health vs Performance
nodes.label: Fasting Protocols for Health vs Performance
nodes.excerpt: Fasting protocols can simplify calorie control or improve adherence for some people, but they are not automatically superior to calorie-matched eating and can conflict with training, protein distribution, medication timing, diabetes risk, pregnancy, or eating-disorder history. Choose fasting only when it serves the goal.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Heart Rate Variability (HRV) as a Recovery Metric
nodes.label: Heart Rate Variability (HRV) as a Recovery Metric
nodes.excerpt: Heart rate variability is beat-to-beat timing variation, used as a proxy for autonomic nervous system state and recovery. It is useful as a personal trend when measured consistently, but single-day wearable readiness scores should be interpreted alongside sleep, illness, alcohol, stress, soreness, and performance.

nodes.label: Hypertrophy vs Strength Programming
nodes.excerpt: Hypertrophy programming optimizes muscle growth; strength programming optimizes force output in specific movements. They overlap, but hypertrophy is driven mostly by hard volume and proximity to failure, while strength also depends heavily on neural skill, heavy-load practice, leverages, and specificity.

nodes.label: Mobility and Flexibility Training
nodes.excerpt: Flexibility is available range of motion; mobility is active control and strength inside that range. Useful mobility work is specific to a task, combines stretching with loaded control, and avoids chasing extreme range that does not improve movement or comfort.

nodes.label: NEAT (Non-Exercise Activity Thermogenesis)
nodes.excerpt: NEAT is the energy spent on everyday movement outside formal exercise: walking, standing, chores, errands, pacing, fidgeting, and posture changes. It can vary dramatically between people and often drops unconsciously during dieting, stress, or desk-heavy work.

nodes.label: Overtraining vs Overreaching
nodes.excerpt: Overreaching is short-term accumulated fatigue; overtraining syndrome is a longer maladaptive state where performance, mood, sleep, and health degrade because stress chronically exceeds recovery. The distinction matters because functional overreaching can be programmed, while nonfunctional overreaching and overtraining require backing off.

nodes.label: Posture and Desk Ergonomics
nodes.excerpt: Desk ergonomics reduces strain by fitting monitor, chair, keyboard, mouse, lighting, and break rhythm to the body. The best posture is not one perfect pose; it is a neutral-enough setup plus frequent variation before static loading becomes discomfort.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Recovery — Sleep, Nutrition, Active Recovery
nodes.label: Recovery — Sleep, Nutrition, Active Recovery
nodes.excerpt: Recovery is the process that converts training stress into adaptation through sleep, food, hydration, low-intensity movement, and fatigue management. It is not passive laziness; it is the biological budget that determines whether workouts become progress or accumulated wear.

nodes.label: Sauna — Evidence and Use
nodes.excerpt: Sauna is controlled heat stress: heart rate rises, sweating increases, and many people experience relaxation. Observational Finnish studies associate frequent sauna use with lower cardiovascular and all-cause mortality, but causality is not settled; safety, hydration, alcohol avoidance, and medical context matter.

nodes.label: Step Count and NEAT
nodes.excerpt: Step count is a practical proxy for daily movement and NEAT: imperfect, but easy to measure and improve. For sedentary technical work, increasing baseline steps supports energy expenditure, cardiometabolic health, back comfort, and mental resets without adding much recovery cost.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Strength Training Principles (Progressive Overload, Specificity)
nodes.label: Strength Training Principles (Progressive Overload, Specificity)
nodes.excerpt: Strength training improves force production by exposing muscles, tendons, and movement patterns to progressively harder but recoverable resistance. The key mechanisms are progressive overload, specificity, sufficient hard volume, technical practice, and fatigue management.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Stretching — Static vs Dynamic vs PNF
nodes.label: Stretching — Static vs Dynamic vs PNF
nodes.excerpt: Static, dynamic, and PNF stretching solve different problems: dynamic movement prepares for activity, static holds build passive range over time, and PNF uses contraction-relaxation to access range. Stretching helps range of motion, but active mobility and strength make that range usable.

nodes.label: Sunlight Exposure and Health
nodes.excerpt: Sunlight is useful for circadian timing, alertness, mood, and vitamin D synthesis, but UV exposure also increases skin aging, eye damage, and skin cancer risk. The practical balance is morning outdoor light for rhythm, sensible outdoor time, and sun protection when UV is meaningful.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/VO2 Max — What It Is and How to Improve It
nodes.label: VO2 Max — What It Is and How to Improve It
nodes.excerpt: VO2 max is the maximum rate the body can take in, deliver, and use oxygen during intense exercise. It reflects heart stroke volume, blood oxygen transport, muscle capillaries, mitochondrial use, genetics, and training history; it improves with both aerobic base work and high-intensity intervals.

nodes.label: Wrist Health for Programmers
nodes.excerpt: Wrist health for programmers is mostly load management: keep wrists near neutral, reduce long static/repetitive input bouts, vary devices and posture, and respond early to pain, numbness, or tingling. Ergonomic gear helps only when it changes the forces and positions that are causing symptoms.

nodes.id: Resources/Health & Fitness/Zone 2 Cardio — Science and Benefits
nodes.label: Zone 2 Cardio — Science and Benefits
nodes.excerpt: Zone 2 cardio is steady aerobic work below the point where lactate and breathing accelerate sharply. Its value is low-fatigue volume: better mitochondrial density, fat oxidation, capillary support, and endurance base without the recovery cost of frequent intervals.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/Bell Labs and Its Innovations
nodes.label: Bell Labs and Its Innovations
nodes.excerpt: Bell Labs produced the transistor, information theory, Unix, C, lasers, cellular concepts, and major physics discoveries because monopoly-era telecom funding supported long-horizon research tied to real engineering problems. Surface this when discussing why breakthrough technology often comes from institutions, not lone genius alone.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Artificial Intelligence (1950s-Present)
nodes.label: History of Artificial Intelligence (1950s-Present)
nodes.excerpt: AI history cycles through symbolic reasoning, expert systems, AI winters, statistical machine learning, deep learning, transformers, and foundation models, with progress driven by algorithms, data, compute, benchmarks, and commercial incentives. Surface this when grounding AI hype in the field's recurring pattern of ambition, disappointment, scaling, and reframing.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Cloud Computing
nodes.label: History of Cloud Computing
nodes.excerpt: Cloud computing evolved from time-sharing, virtualization, hosting, and utility computing into on-demand infrastructure, managed services, serverless, and global developer platforms. Surface this when explaining why startups can scale without buying servers, why architecture became a recurring cost decision, and why cloud providers became strategic infrastructure.

nodes.label: History of Cryptography
nodes.excerpt: Cryptography evolved from classical ciphers and wartime codebreaking into public-key systems, digital signatures, TLS, end-to-end encryption, cryptocurrencies, and zero-knowledge proofs, tracking a long tension between privacy, commerce, state power, and trust. Surface this when explaining why modern internet security depends on math, protocols, institutions, and key management.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Mobile Computing
nodes.label: History of Mobile Computing
nodes.excerpt: Mobile computing evolved from laptops and PDAs into cellular, BlackBerry, smartphones, app stores, sensors, cameras, maps, and always-connected personal devices. Surface this when explaining why modern software assumes touch, location, notifications, camera input, app distribution, and distracted one-handed use.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Open Source
nodes.label: History of Open Source
nodes.excerpt: Open source grew from academic and hacker sharing into a dominant software production model through free software ideals, permissive and copyleft licenses, Linux, Apache, Git, GitHub, package managers, and cloud-era commercialization. Surface this when explaining why modern software depends on unpaid maintainers, shared infrastructure, licenses, and supply-chain trust.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Personal Computing (Altair to iPhone)
nodes.label: History of Personal Computing (Altair to iPhone)
nodes.excerpt: Personal computing moved computation from institutions to individuals through microprocessors, hobbyist kits, Apple, IBM PCs, GUIs, Microsoft platforms, laptops, the web, and smartphones. Surface this when explaining why modern software assumes direct manipulation, personal apps, files, windows, mobile access, and user-level computing power.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Programming Languages
nodes.label: History of Programming Languages
nodes.excerpt: Programming languages evolved by raising abstraction while negotiating tradeoffs between machine control, safety, expressiveness, portability, performance, and developer speed. Surface this when explaining why C, Java, JavaScript, Python, Go, Rust, and TypeScript feel different: they were built for different eras, machines, organizations, and failure modes.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Search Engines
nodes.label: History of Search Engines
nodes.excerpt: Search engines evolved from human directories and early crawlers into ranking systems, ad platforms, semantic retrieval, vertical search, and AI-assisted answers; the key shift was ranking relevance and authority at web scale. Surface this when discussing retrieval, SEO, PageRank, ads, RAG, or why assistants inherit search's relevance problem.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Social Media
nodes.label: History of Social Media
nodes.excerpt: Social media evolved from forums, blogs, and social graphs into algorithmic feeds, creator economies, messaging networks, and attention markets, reshaping identity, news, politics, friendship, and advertising. Surface this when discussing social graphs, virality, moderation, recommendation algorithms, FOMO, or surveillance-ad incentives.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Video Game Consoles
nodes.label: History of Video Game Consoles
nodes.excerpt: Video game consoles are specialized consumer computing platforms shaped by hardware generations, controller design, exclusive software, licensing, media formats, online services, and living-room identity. Surface this when connecting gaming history to platform strategy, hardware constraints, and why console generations feel culturally distinct.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of Video Games
nodes.label: History of Video Games
nodes.excerpt: Video game history runs from lab experiments and arcades through consoles, PCs, 3D graphics, online play, mobile, esports, streaming, and live-service economies, shaped by hardware limits, input devices, distribution, and player culture. Surface this when connecting Kenny's gaming interest to technology, design, business models, and culture.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of the Internet (ARPANET to Web3)
nodes.label: History of the Internet (ARPANET to Web3)
nodes.excerpt: The internet grew from packet-switching research and ARPANET into global infrastructure because open internetworking protocols, academic/government institutions, commercialization, DNS, email, the web, broadband, mobile, and cloud made networks interoperable and economically valuable. Surface this when explaining why the internet is not one company, why protocols mattered, or how open infrastructure later produced platform power.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of the Transistor and Integrated Circuit
nodes.label: History of the Transistor and Integrated Circuit
nodes.excerpt: The transistor replaced fragile vacuum tubes with solid-state switching, and the integrated circuit packed many transistors onto chips, enabling Moore's Law, personal computing, mobile devices, cloud, AI, and modern electronics. Surface this when explaining the material foundation under all software.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/History of the Web (Web 1.0 to 2.0 to 3.0)
nodes.label: History of the Web (Web 1.0 to 2.0 to 3.0)
nodes.excerpt: The web evolved from linked documents into interactive applications, social platforms, cloud-backed SaaS, and contested Web3 decentralization narratives; it is an application layer on top of the internet, not the internet itself. Surface this when explaining browsers, frontend work, platform power, or why web history mixes documents, apps, media, and commerce.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/Moore's Law — History and Decline
nodes.label: Moore's Law — History and Decline
nodes.excerpt: Moore's Law began as Gordon Moore's observation that transistor counts on chips were doubling on a predictable cadence, then became an industry roadmap that drove cheaper, faster computing for decades. Surface this when explaining why software got used to free speedups, why that era is slowing, and why modern progress depends more on specialization, parallelism, chiplets, accelerators, and efficiency.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/Rise of the Smartphone (The iPhone Moment)
nodes.label: Rise of the Smartphone (The iPhone Moment)
nodes.excerpt: The iPhone did not invent the smartphone, but it crystallized the modern model: multitouch, a real browser, sensors, camera, app distribution, and pocket computing as everyday infrastructure. Surface this when explaining why mobile changed not just devices, but software distribution, attention, commerce, maps, media, social behavior, and platform power.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/The Age of Surveillance Capitalism
nodes.label: The Age of Surveillance Capitalism
nodes.excerpt: Surveillance capitalism, associated with Shoshana Zuboff, describes platform business models that collect behavioral data, predict behavior, and shape attention for advertising, personalization, and influence. Surface this when discussing social media incentives, privacy, recommendation systems, attention capture, or why free digital services often have hidden extraction economics.

nodes.label: The Browser Wars
nodes.excerpt: The browser wars were fights to control the web's main user interface, from Netscape vs Internet Explorer through Firefox, Chrome, Safari, and Edge, shaping standards, rendering engines, JavaScript performance, security, and platform power. Surface this when explaining why frontend compatibility, web standards, and browser engines still matter.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/The Dotcom Boom and Bust
nodes.label: The Dotcom Boom and Bust
nodes.excerpt: The dotcom boom and bust was a late-1990s internet investment bubble where real technological transformation was paired with unsustainable valuations, weak business models, and premature timing. Surface this when discussing hype cycles, AI bubbles, startup discipline, or why real technology can still produce irrational markets.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/The Homebrew Computer Club
nodes.label: The Homebrew Computer Club
nodes.excerpt: The Homebrew Computer Club was a 1970s Silicon Valley hobbyist community where affordable microprocessors, open sharing, demos, and entrepreneurial energy helped launch personal computing culture, including Apple's early path. Surface this when discussing how communities turn technical components into movements.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/The Open Source Movement (Linux, GNU, Apache)
nodes.label: The Open Source Movement (Linux, GNU, Apache)
nodes.excerpt: The open source movement blends GNU's free-software ideals, Linux's distributed kernel development, Apache's web infrastructure dominance, permissive and copyleft licensing, and corporate adoption into the foundation of modern software. Surface this when explaining why open source is simultaneously commons, ideology, production model, dependency supply chain, and platform strategy.

nodes.id: Resources/History of Technology/Xerox PARC — Ideas That Changed Computing
nodes.label: Xerox PARC — Ideas That Changed Computing
nodes.excerpt: Xerox PARC pioneered or refined graphical workstations, Ethernet, laser printing, Smalltalk, WYSIWYG editing, object-oriented environments, and networked office computing, but Xerox struggled to commercialize the whole vision. Surface this when explaining the gap between invention and market capture.

nodes.label: IaaS PaaS SaaS
nodes.excerpt: service model spectrum: IaaS (raw VMs), PaaS (managed runtime), SaaS (fully managed app), FaaS (functions), and where the lines blur.

nodes.label: Interview Communication
nodes.excerpt: interview communication guide covering project explanations, STAR stories, coding/system design structure, debugging out loud, clarifying questions, recovery, and resume bullet framing.

nodes.label: Kubernetes
nodes.excerpt: desired state reconciliation, control plane, worker nodes, all core objects (Pod/Deployment/Service/Ingress/StatefulSet/HPA/PV), scheduling, health probes, networking (CNI/kube-proxy/CoreDNS), RBAC.

nodes.label: LLM Capabilities and AI Ethics
nodes.excerpt: opinionated overview of what LLMs can and cannot do, their tradeoffs, whether they replace engineers/humans, and the philosophy, morality, and ethics of AI deployment.

nodes.label: LLM System Design
nodes.excerpt: tokens, context windows, prompt caching (KV cache, TTL, cost math), streaming, tool use/agentic loops, RAG (chunking, hybrid search, reranking), multi-agent orchestration, evals, fine-tuning vs prompting.

nodes.label: Effective Vocabulary Building
nodes.excerpt: Effective vocabulary building means learning words as usable distinctions, not isolated definitions. Durable vocabulary comes from context, examples, retrieval, morphology, and active use in speech or writing.

nodes.label: Etymology — How Words Evolve
nodes.excerpt: Etymology studies how words change through borrowing, sound shifts, metaphor, semantic drift, and cultural contact. It is useful because word histories reveal hidden connections, but current meaning still depends on present usage.

nodes.id: Resources/Language & Linguistics/How Language Works (Syntax, Semantics, Pragmatics)
nodes.label: How Language Works (Syntax, Semantics, Pragmatics)
nodes.excerpt: Language works through layered systems: syntax structures sentences, semantics carries meaning, and pragmatics explains how context changes what utterances actually do. This note gives Bobby a compact map for reasoning about communication, ambiguity, and human meaning-making.

nodes.id: Resources/Language & Linguistics/Language Acquisition — How Humans Learn Language
nodes.label: Language Acquisition — How Humans Learn Language
nodes.excerpt: Language acquisition is the human process of learning sound patterns, words, grammar, and social use from rich interaction. It combines biological readiness, statistical learning, feedback, imitation, and meaningful communication.

nodes.id: Resources/Language & Linguistics/Language Learning Methods (Immersion, Comprehensible Input, Krashen)
nodes.label: Language Learning Methods (Immersion, Comprehensible Input, Krashen)
nodes.excerpt: Strong language learning combines comprehensible input, meaningful output, spaced review, pronunciation practice, and real interaction. Krashen's input hypothesis is useful because learners need language they mostly understand, but fluency also benefits from deliberate output and feedback.

nodes.label: Natural Language Processing Overview
nodes.excerpt: Natural language processing is the field of making computers process, search, generate, classify, translate, and reason over human language. Modern NLP is dominated by embeddings and transformer language models, but classic tasks still depend on data quality, evaluation, and linguistic ambiguity.

nodes.label: Sapir-Whorf Hypothesis (Language Shapes Thought)
nodes.excerpt: The Sapir-Whorf hypothesis asks whether language shapes thought, with a strong deterministic version mostly rejected and a weaker influence version still useful. The practical takeaway is that words and categories can guide attention without fully trapping cognition.

nodes.id: Resources/Language & Linguistics/Writing Systems of the World
nodes.label: Writing Systems of the World
nodes.excerpt: Writing systems encode language in different ways: alphabets represent sounds, syllabaries represent syllables, abjads emphasize consonants, and logographic systems represent morphemes or words.
