Understanding writing systems helps explain literacy, typography, input methods, and why text is not language itself.

nodes.label: 1-on-1 Meeting Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: 1-on-1s are recurring private conversations for trust, context, feedback, coaching, and career development, not default status meetings. A strong 1-on-1 has a shared agenda, employee-owned topics, manager follow-through, and space for issues that dashboards will not reveal.

nodes.label: Async Communication Culture
nodes.excerpt: Async communication culture uses written context, durable decisions, clear ownership, and explicit response expectations so teams can coordinate without constant interruption. It works only when writing quality, norms, and escalation paths are strong.

nodes.label: Conway's Law
nodes.excerpt: Conway's Law says systems tend to mirror the communication structures of the organizations that build them. Architecture and org design are coupled: team boundaries, ownership, and coordination paths show up as APIs, services, dependencies, and failure modes.

nodes.label: Decision-Making Frameworks (DACI, RACI)
nodes.excerpt: DACI and RACI clarify decision and execution roles so work does not stall in ambiguity. DACI is strongest for decisions; RACI is strongest for recurring responsibilities and cross-functional execution.

nodes.label: Delegation — When and How
nodes.excerpt: Delegation transfers ownership with context, authority, expectations, support, and feedback loops. Good delegation creates leverage and growth; bad delegation is either dumping unclear work or taking it back before the person can learn.

nodes.label: Engineering Levels and Ladders
nodes.excerpt: Engineering levels and ladders define expectations for impact, scope, autonomy, ambiguity, technical judgment, collaboration, and leadership across IC and management tracks. They make hiring, feedback, promotion, and compensation more explicit, but must be calibrated with real examples.

nodes.id: Resources/Leadership & Management/Engineering Management vs IC Track
nodes.label: Engineering Management vs IC Track
nodes.excerpt: Engineering management and senior IC work are parallel leadership tracks with different leverage: managers shape teams, people systems, priorities, and execution; ICs shape technical direction, architecture, quality, and influence without direct authority. The right path depends on energy, strengths, and desired daily work, not status.

nodes.id: Resources/Leadership & Management/Giving Feedback (SBI Model — Situation, Behavior, Impact)
nodes.label: Giving Feedback (SBI Model — Situation, Behavior, Impact)
nodes.excerpt: The SBI model makes feedback specific by naming the situation, observable behavior, and impact. It avoids character judgments and mind-reading, making feedback easier to understand, discuss, and act on.

nodes.label: Hiring — Structured Interviews
nodes.excerpt: Structured interviews improve hiring by evaluating candidates against consistent role criteria, questions, rubrics, and written evidence. They reduce bias and increase signal by replacing improvised vibe-based conversations with job-relevant assessment.

nodes.label: Managing Up
nodes.excerpt: Managing up is proactive upward coordination: clarifying goals, surfacing risks early, adapting to a manager's decision style, bringing options, and asking for needed support. It is not manipulation; it is taking ownership of the manager-report interface.

nodes.id: Resources/Leadership & Management/Organizational Design (Functional vs Divisional vs Matrix)
nodes.label: Organizational Design (Functional vs Divisional vs Matrix)
nodes.excerpt: Organizational design chooses how authority, accountability, communication, and specialization are arranged. Functional, divisional, and matrix structures trade depth, speed, coordination, ownership clarity, and duplication; the right structure follows strategy and current bottlenecks.

nodes.label: Performance Reviews
nodes.excerpt: Performance reviews summarize impact, behavior, growth, and expectations over a defined period, often affecting compensation or promotion. Good reviews are evidence-based, level-calibrated, bias-aware, and contain no major surprises because feedback happened continuously.

nodes.id: Resources/Leadership & Management/Psychological Safety in Teams (Amy Edmondson)
nodes.label: Psychological Safety in Teams (Amy Edmondson)
nodes.excerpt: Psychological safety, from Amy Edmondson's research, is a shared belief that people can speak up, ask questions, admit mistakes, and challenge ideas without interpersonal punishment. It enables learning and risk detection; it is not comfort, consensus, or low standards.

nodes.label: Radical Candor (Kim Scott)
nodes.excerpt: Radical Candor combines caring personally with challenging directly. The useful managerial move is to give specific truth early while showing commitment to the person, avoiding both harshness without care and kindness that withholds needed feedback.

nodes.label: Remote Team Management
nodes.excerpt: Remote team management makes implicit office coordination explicit through documentation, rituals, outcomes, timezone norms, onboarding, feedback, and intentional connection. Remote work exposes weak management habits because visibility can no longer substitute for clarity.

nodes.label: Running Effective Meetings
nodes.excerpt: Effective meetings are expensive coordination tools with a clear purpose, prepared context, right participants, facilitation, explicit decisions, and follow-up. The default question should be whether synchronous time will resolve ambiguity faster than written async work.

nodes.label: Technical Interview Design
nodes.excerpt: Technical interview design should evaluate job-relevant engineering skill with consistent tasks, calibrated rubrics, and respectful candidate experience. A hard interview is not automatically high-signal; the question is whether it predicts the work the person will actually do.

nodes.label: The Principal-Agent Problem
nodes.excerpt: The principal-agent problem appears when one party delegates authority to another whose incentives, information, or risk exposure differ. In organizations, the practical fix is not just trust: align incentives, reduce information asymmetry, clarify accountability, and avoid metrics that reward proxy behavior over real outcomes.

nodes.label: The Staff Engineer's Path
nodes.excerpt: The Staff Engineer path is senior IC leadership through technical judgment, cross-team influence, architecture, execution strategy, mentorship, and organizational leverage without direct people management. Staff work is less about writing the hardest code personally and more about making important technical outcomes happen.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Active Recall vs Passive Review
nodes.label: Active Recall vs Passive Review
nodes.excerpt: The testing effect (Roediger & Karpicke, 2006) demonstrated that testing yourself on material produces 50% better retention at one week than restudying the same material — even though students in the restudy condition felt they were learning more. The mechanism is the 'retrieval practice effect': the neural effort of reconstructing a memory from partially-degraded cues strengthens the memory trace in ways that passive exposure does not. Passive review creates the fluency illusion — familiarity mistaken for recall.

nodes.label: Anki — Optimal Card Design
nodes.excerpt: Good Anki cards follow the minimum information principle: one question, one unambiguous answer, one clear fact — small enough to be graded honestly without partial credit. Michael Nielsen's rule: 'if you can't rate the card straightforwardly, it's probably testing multiple things.' The most common failure is adding cards for material not yet understood, creating decks that train frustration rather than memory.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Calibration — Knowing What You Don't Know
nodes.label: Calibration — Knowing What You Don't Know
nodes.excerpt: Calibration is the skill of matching confidence to reality. It improves through prediction, feedback, decision journaling, and noticing where understanding feels fluent but fails under retrieval or application.

nodes.label: Chunking Information
nodes.excerpt: Chunking is the cognitive process of grouping information into meaningful units so working memory can handle more with the same capacity. Expertise largely is chunking — experts don't think faster, they perceive at a higher level of abstraction. George Miller (1956) identified the ~7-item working memory limit; Chase & Simon (1973) showed chess masters recalled board positions not by pieces but by configurations of 5–7 pieces, which they'd seen thousands of times. The mechanism: familiar patterns collapse from many low-level items to one high-level chunk, freeing working memory for reasoning.

nodes.label: Deliberate Practice (Anders Ericsson)
nodes.excerpt: Deliberate practice (Anders Ericsson, 1993 paper; Peak, 2016) is the specific type of practice that produces expertise — not just time spent, but structured practice targeting a specific weakness, at the edge of current ability, with immediate feedback. Ericsson's most important finding: expert violinists practiced ~4 hours of deliberate practice per day, not 8 or 10, because deliberate practice is maximally effortful and requires real recovery. The 10,000-hour rule (Gladwell's popularization) missed the key qualifier: it's 10,000 hours of deliberate practice, not just time in the domain.

nodes.label: Desirable Difficulties in Learning
nodes.excerpt: Desirable difficulties (Robert Bjork, 1994) are learning conditions that feel harder and slower in the short term but consistently produce better long-term retention and transfer. The 'desirable' qualifier is critical: the difficulties must still allow success with effort and must provide feedback for error correction — otherwise they become just difficult, not productively difficult. The core counterintuitive finding: the ease with which learning feels to occur during a session is inversely correlated with how much is actually retained.

nodes.label: First Principles Thinking
nodes.excerpt: First principles thinking decomposes a problem to its fundamental truths — constraints that are physically, logically, or economically real — and rebuilds solutions from there, bypassing inherited assumptions and analogical thinking. The method has roots in Aristotle's 'first principles' as the basis of scientific demonstration and Descartes' methodological doubt. Musk's battery example is the canonical modern application: the market price of battery packs (~$600/kWh) was treated as a ceiling; decomposed to commodity material costs (~$80/kWh), it became a floor — revealing that manufacturing efficiency was the real constraint, not material cost.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Growth Mindset vs Fixed Mindset
nodes.label: Growth Mindset vs Fixed Mindset
nodes.excerpt: Growth mindset frames ability as improvable through effort, strategy, and feedback, while fixed mindset treats ability as static. The useful version is not empty positivity; it is choosing better practice after failure.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/How Memory Works (Encoding, Storage, Retrieval)
nodes.label: How Memory Works (Encoding, Storage, Retrieval)
nodes.excerpt: Memory is an active reconstruction process, not a recording — Bartlett (1932) showed that retrieved memories are rebuilt from gist plus schemas, which explains why retrieval practice (not re-reading) is the primary mechanism for strengthening memory traces. The Ebbinghaus forgetting curve, sleep consolidation via hippocampal sharp-wave ripples, and the testing effect together define the optimal learning system: encode with depth, retrieve with spacing, sleep after learning.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/How to Read a Technical Paper
nodes.label: How to Read a Technical Paper
nodes.excerpt: Reading a technical paper effectively requires a structured multi-pass approach, not linear reading from title to references. Srinivasan Keshav's three-pass method (2007, ACM SIGCOMM) is the standard: pass 1 in 5–10 minutes extracts the claim and decides whether the paper is worth reading; pass 2 in ~1 hour maps the evidence to the claim without getting lost in proofs; pass 3 (only for papers you'll implement or critique) reconstructs the paper from first principles. The critical reader's questions — What is the claim? What evidence supports it? What assumptions does it rest on? What would falsify it? — are what distinguish understanding from passive exposure.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/How to Retain What You Read
nodes.label: How to Retain What You Read
nodes.excerpt: Retaining what you read requires active processing: previewing, questioning, summarizing, recalling, connecting, and applying. Highlighting alone creates familiarity but weak long-term recall.

nodes.label: Interleaving Practice
nodes.excerpt: Interleaving practice (Shea & Morgan, 1979; Robert Bjork) mixes related problem types within sessions instead of drilling one type exhaustively before moving to the next. Despite feeling harder and less productive during practice, it consistently produces 20-50% better retention and transfer on delayed tests because it forces discrimination — learners must retrieve the relevant strategy with each problem rather than keeping the same approach loaded in working memory. The most important warning: interleaving is only beneficial after basic exposure; premature interleaving of unlearned material creates confusion, not desirable difficulty.

nodes.label: Inversion Thinking
nodes.excerpt: Inversion thinking solves problems by asking 'what would guarantee failure?' and then avoiding or counteracting those paths. The mathematician Jacobi's principle — 'man muss immer umkehren' (you must always invert) — and Charlie Munger's systematic application made it a core mental model in modern decision-making. The key insight is asymmetry: failure modes are often easier to enumerate precisely than success conditions, especially in complex systems where success requires many things to go right but failure only requires one thing to go wrong. Gary Klein's premortem (1989) is the structured organizational version.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Learning a New Domain Fast
nodes.label: Learning a New Domain Fast
nodes.excerpt: Learning a new domain fast means building a map of core concepts, vocabulary, constraints, experts, tools, and feedback loops. The fastest path combines overview, targeted projects, active recall, and conversations with knowledgeable people.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Learning a New Programming Language Fast
nodes.label: Learning a New Programming Language Fast
nodes.excerpt: Learning a new programming language fast requires understanding its mental model — the runtime model, type system, memory model, concurrency model, and error model — not memorizing syntax. Josh Kaufman (The First 20 Hours) showed that focused, deliberate practice on the core concepts produces ~80% functional proficiency in far less time than assumed. For someone with existing languages (Kenny: Go, Python, TypeScript), the fastest path is comparative: what does this language assume differently? Syntax is learned incidentally; the mental model must be acquired deliberately. Reading idiomatic code in the stdlib or well-maintained open source projects is more effective than tutorials for internalizing idioms.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Mental Models — What They Are and How to Build Them
nodes.label: Mental Models — What They Are and How to Build Them
nodes.excerpt: Mental models are simplified internal representations of how systems work — used to predict outcomes, notice tradeoffs, and make decisions under uncertainty. Charlie Munger argues a 'latticework of mental models' from multiple disciplines is more powerful than deep expertise in one domain, because real-world problems are interdisciplinary. The failure mode: having a model vs. using it. A model you know abstractly but can't deploy in a live situation is a label, not a tool. Models must be tested against reality, updated when wrong, and actually applied in decision-making to develop into genuine thinking tools.

nodes.label: Metacognition
nodes.excerpt: Metacognition is thinking about one's own thinking: planning, monitoring, and evaluating learning or decision processes. It helps learners choose strategies intentionally instead of confusing effort with progress.

nodes.label: Note-Taking Methods (Cornell, Outline, Zettelkasten)
nodes.excerpt: Note-taking methods differ in how they structure attention, recall, and synthesis. Cornell supports study review, outlines support hierarchy, and Zettelkasten supports connected long-term thinking.

nodes.label: Productive Disagreement
nodes.excerpt: Productive disagreement turns conflict into better understanding by separating claims, evidence, assumptions, and values. It works when participants try to improve the model rather than win the exchange.

nodes.label: Second-Order Thinking
nodes.excerpt: Second-order thinking traces consequences beyond the immediate first effect of a decision, accounting for how people, systems, and incentives adapt in response. Howard Marks (Oaktree Capital) frames it as the essential edge in decision-making: first-order thinking is obvious and shared, so it can't produce above-average outcomes. The canonical engineering example is Goodhart's Law — when a metric becomes a target, optimization pressure changes behavior until the metric no longer measures what it was intended to measure. The cobra effect (British colonial India paid bounties for dead cobras; farmers bred cobras for the bounty) is the cleanest historical illustration.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Spaced Repetition — Theory and Implementation
nodes.label: Spaced Repetition — Theory and Implementation
nodes.excerpt: Spaced repetition exploits the Ebbinghaus forgetting curve by scheduling reviews just before a memory would fully decay — the effortful retrieval of a partially-faded memory strengthens it far more than reviewing a still-fresh one. The SM-2 algorithm (Wozniak, 1987, still used by Anki) calculates optimal intervals by tracking per-card difficulty, expanding intervals for easy cards and contracting them for hard ones. The system is high-leverage for durable factual retention but fails for understanding, rapidly-changing knowledge, and decks too large to maintain.

nodes.label: Systems Thinking
nodes.excerpt: Systems thinking (Donella Meadows, 'Thinking in Systems,' 2008) analyzes complex phenomena through stocks, flows, feedback loops, delays, and leverage points rather than linear cause-and-effect chains. The central insight: most problems in complex systems aren't caused by bad actors or obvious causes — they're caused by the structure of the system itself. Balancing and reinforcing feedback loops, combined with delays that make cause and effect invisible, produce counterintuitive behavior like oscillations, collapse, and fixes that make things worse. The policy-resistance archetype — where every intervention triggers adaptive responses that undermine the goal — is why many 'solutions' fail.

nodes.id: Resources/Learning & Memory/Teaching as a Learning Tool
nodes.label: Teaching as a Learning Tool
nodes.excerpt: Teaching strengthens learning because it forces retrieval, organization, simplification, and feedback. Explaining a topic clearly reveals missing links that passive review can hide.

nodes.label: The Feynman Technique
nodes.excerpt: The Feynman Technique tests understanding by forcing explanation in plain language without jargon — and treating every point where the explanation becomes vague or hand-wavy as a precise diagnosis of where understanding is incomplete. Named for Richard Feynman (Nobel Physics 1965), who famously claimed he could explain any physics concept to a freshman because genuinely understanding something and being able to explain it simply are the same thing.

nodes.label: The Learning Pyramid
nodes.excerpt: The Learning Pyramid is a popular but oversimplified model claiming different retention rates by activity. Its useful takeaway is that active methods often beat passive review, but the exact percentages should not be treated as evidence.

nodes.label: Transfer of Learning
nodes.excerpt: Transfer of learning is applying knowledge or skill from one context to another. It improves when learners understand underlying principles, practice varied examples, and compare cases instead of memorizing surface patterns.

nodes.label: MLOps and ML Engineering
nodes.excerpt: ML lifecycle from data and labels through training, experiment tracking, model registry, deployment, monitoring, drift, retraining, and LLMOps for Bobby-like systems.

nodes.label: Bayes' Theorem
nodes.excerpt: Explains Bayes' theorem as disciplined belief updating: posterior probability equals prior times likelihood divided by evidence. Includes the formula, medical-test base-rate example, odds form, repeated updating, ML applications, and common mistakes around base rates and causal interpretation.

nodes.label: Big-O Notation and Complexity Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Explains Big-O notation as asymptotic growth reasoning for time and space complexity, including common classes, examples, worst/average/amortized cases, lower bounds, and practical caveats. Emphasizes that Big-O is a scalability filter, not a substitute for benchmarking or understanding constants, cache behavior, and input shape.

nodes.label: Boolean Algebra and Logic Gates
nodes.excerpt: Explains Boolean algebra as the math of true/false values and logic gates as hardware implementations of AND, OR, NOT, XOR, NAND, and NOR. Covers truth tables, De Morgan's laws, functional completeness, bit masks, condition simplification, circuit intuition, and policy-logic failure modes.

nodes.label: Calculus — Derivatives and Integrals
nodes.excerpt: Explains calculus as the math of local change and accumulated quantity: derivatives measure instant slope or sensitivity, while integrals add infinitesimal pieces into totals. Connects limits, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, optimization, motion, probability density, and gradient-based machine learning.

nodes.label: Central Limit Theorem
nodes.excerpt: Explains the Central Limit Theorem as the reason standardized sums and sample means often become approximately normal even when raw data is not. Covers the formula, standard error, confidence-interval intuition, sample-size effects, heavy-tail/dependence caveats, and how CLT differs from the Law of Large Numbers.

nodes.label: Chain Rule (in Context of Backprop)
nodes.excerpt: Explains the chain rule as multiplication of local derivative effects through composed functions, then maps that directly onto neural-network backpropagation. Covers computation graphs, upstream gradients, local gradients, forward/backward passes, automatic differentiation, and vanishing/exploding gradient failure modes.

nodes.label: Combinatorics Basics (Permutations, Combinations)
nodes.excerpt: Explains combinatorics as disciplined counting: permutations count ordered arrangements, combinations count unordered selections, and inclusion-exclusion fixes overcounting. Connects factorial growth, search spaces, probability denominators, backtracking, brute-force limits, and common order/repetition traps.

nodes.label: Discrete Mathematics for CS
nodes.excerpt: Explains discrete mathematics as the math of countable structures that computer science manipulates directly: bits, sets, logic, graphs, relations, functions, strings, integers, proofs, and finite states. Connects each area to algorithms, databases, type systems, cryptography, automata, verification, and engineering reasoning.

nodes.label: Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
nodes.excerpt: Explains eigenvectors as directions a transformation does not rotate, and eigenvalues as the scale factors along those directions. Connects the equation A v = lambda v to PCA, graph ranking, stability analysis, differential equations, and why dominant eigenvectors reveal hidden structure.

nodes.label: Expected Value and Variance
nodes.excerpt: Explains expected value as a probability-weighted average and variance as squared spread around that average. Includes formulas, dice and risk examples, linearity of expectation, standard deviation, covariance caveats, decision-making tradeoffs, and why averages alone can hide tail risk.

nodes.label: Fourier Transform Intuition
nodes.excerpt: Builds intuition for the Fourier transform as changing a signal from time/space values into frequency components. Explains sine/cosine decomposition, low vs high frequencies, DFT/FFT, filtering, convolution, spectrograms, compression, aliasing, and why frequency-domain thinking helps audio, images, ML, and signal processing.

nodes.label: Gradient and Partial Derivatives
nodes.excerpt: Explains partial derivatives as one-variable-at-a-time sensitivities and gradients as the vector of all local sensitivities. Covers steepest ascent/descent, directional derivatives, feature scaling, saddle points, gradient magnitude, and why gradients are the training signal for modern ML.

nodes.label: Graph Theory Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Explains graph theory as the math of entities and relationships, covering vertices, edges, directed/weighted graphs, paths, cycles, connectivity, trees, DAGs, adjacency representations, traversal, shortest paths, and graph failure modes. Connects the abstraction to dependencies, routing, recommendations, social networks, state spaces, and distributed systems.

nodes.id: Resources/Mathematics/Information Theory — Entropy, Cross-Entropy, KL Divergence
nodes.label: Information Theory — Entropy, Cross-Entropy, KL Divergence
nodes.excerpt: Explains entropy, cross-entropy, and KL divergence as measures of uncertainty, coding cost, and mismatch between probability distributions. Connects Shannon information to compression, language-model next-token loss, log loss, perplexity, VAEs, and why KL direction matters.

nodes.label: Lagrange Multipliers
nodes.excerpt: Explains Lagrange multipliers as constrained-optimization tools: at an equality-constrained optimum, the objective gradient aligns with the constraint gradient. Includes a worked circle constraint example, Lagrangian setup, shadow-price intuition, regularization links, and caveats around constraint qualifications and local optima.

nodes.label: Law of Large Numbers
nodes.excerpt: Explains the Law of Large Numbers as convergence of sample averages toward expected value as valid independent data accumulates. Covers weak/strong intuition, coin-flip examples, Monte Carlo dependence, random error versus bias, and why large samples can still be confidently wrong.

nodes.label: Linear Algebra — Vectors and Matrices
nodes.excerpt: Builds intuition for vectors as quantities with direction or feature coordinates, and matrices as linear transformations, datasets, or systems of equations. Connects span, basis, rank, independence, projections, and matrix shape to ML embeddings, graphics, optimization, and data pipelines.

nodes.label: Markov Chains
nodes.excerpt: Explains Markov chains as probabilistic state-transition systems where the next state depends only on the current state. Covers transition matrices, memorylessness, random walks, stationary distributions, absorbing states, PageRank intuition, MCMC links, and when the Markov assumption breaks.

nodes.label: Matrix Multiplication and Dot Products
nodes.excerpt: Explains dot products as alignment-weighted sums and matrix multiplication as composing linear transformations or batching many dot products. Includes dimensions, worked examples, projection intuition, neural-network layers, attention scores, and common shape/order mistakes.

nodes.label: Monte Carlo Methods
nodes.excerpt: Explains Monte Carlo methods as random-sampling estimators for expectations, probabilities, integrals, and simulations that are hard to compute exactly. Covers pi estimation, error scaling, variance reduction, bootstrap-style uncertainty, MCMC, and the tradeoff between flexible approximation and slow convergence.

nodes.label: Multivariable Calculus Basics
nodes.excerpt: Extends single-variable calculus to functions with many inputs and outputs, covering partial derivatives, gradients, Jacobians, Hessians, level sets, constrained optimization, and directional derivatives. Frames ML loss landscapes as high-dimensional surfaces where local slope and curvature guide parameter updates.

nodes.label: Number Theory Basics (Primes, Modular Arithmetic)
nodes.excerpt: Explains number theory for computing: divisibility, primes, GCD, modular arithmetic, congruence, modular inverses, fast exponentiation, sieves, hashing, cyclic buffers, checksums, and public-key crypto intuition. Emphasizes how remainder arithmetic creates wraparound systems and why integer structure matters in algorithms.

nodes.label: Optimization — Convex vs Non-Convex
nodes.excerpt: Explains optimization as choosing variables to minimize or maximize an objective, with convex problems offering global guarantees and non-convex problems requiring local-search pragmatism. Covers objectives, constraints, gradients, curvature, regularization, initialization, saddle points, learning rates, and ML loss-landscape expectations.

nodes.label: Probability Distributions (Normal, Binomial, Poisson, Exponential)
nodes.excerpt: Explains probability distributions as data-generating models, comparing normal, binomial, Poisson, and exponential distributions by support, parameters, formulas, assumptions, examples, and failure modes. Emphasizes choosing distributions by mechanism, not by superficial histogram shape.

nodes.label: Set Theory
nodes.excerpt: Explains set theory as the language of membership, containment, union, intersection, difference, complement, cardinality, power sets, Cartesian products, and relations. Connects set operations to SQL, permissions, filters, probability events, type systems, and common boundary/universe mistakes.

nodes.label: Mechanistic Interpretability
nodes.excerpt: reverse-engineering transformers: circuit hypothesis, induction heads, IOI circuit, superposition, sparse autoencoders, activation patching.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/ADHD — Understanding and Managing It
nodes.label: ADHD — Understanding and Managing It
nodes.excerpt: ADHD is a neurodevelopmental condition driven by dopamine/norepinephrine dysregulation in the prefrontal cortex and striatum — primarily an executive function disorder, not an attention deficit. Russell Barkley's model reframes it as impaired self-regulation and time blindness. Understanding the actual mechanism changes what management strategies are worth trying.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Anxiety — Types and Coping Mechanisms
nodes.label: Anxiety — Types and Coping Mechanisms
nodes.excerpt: Anxiety is the brain's threat-detection system running past its useful range — anticipating danger that either doesn't exist or isn't proportionate. Different anxiety disorders have distinct triggers and mechanisms, but all are maintained by the same core loop: avoidance prevents extinction and keeps fear alive. The most effective coping tools target this avoidance directly.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Attachment Styles (Secure, Anxious, Avoidant, Fearful)
nodes.label: Attachment Styles (Secure, Anxious, Avoidant, Fearful)
nodes.excerpt: Attachment theory (Bowlby, Ainsworth) describes how early caregiver relationships shape internal working models of closeness, safety, and self-worth in relationships. Four adult attachment styles — secure, anxious, avoidant, fearful — predict how people behave under relational stress, seek or reject support, and regulate emotions in close relationships. Styles are patterns, not fixed identities, and can shift with experience and therapy.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Burnout — Signs, Stages, and Recovery
nodes.label: Burnout — Signs, Stages, and Recovery
nodes.excerpt: Burnout is a syndrome of emotional exhaustion, cynicism/depersonalization, and reduced personal accomplishment resulting from chronic unmanaged workplace stress. Christina Maslach's 40+ years of research identifies six organizational misfits that cause it. Recovery requires structural change, not just rest — and often takes 6–12 months for full recovery.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Chronic Stress — Long-Term Physical and Mental Effects
nodes.label: Chronic Stress — Long-Term Physical and Mental Effects
nodes.excerpt: Chronic stress is the physiological and psychological state when the stress response activates more often than the body can recover from it. The long-term effects are systemic: cognitive degradation, cardiovascular damage, immune dysfunction, metabolic changes, and structural brain changes. Understanding the mechanism helps separate 'feeling stressed' from the actual biological load accumulating.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) Basics
nodes.label: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) Basics
nodes.excerpt: CBT is the most evidence-supported psychotherapy for anxiety, depression, and several other conditions. It works by interrupting the feedback loops between thoughts, emotions, body sensations, and behaviors — and teaching skills to respond differently. The core insight is that changing how you interpret an event changes how you feel about it.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Cognitive Distortions — Complete Reference
nodes.label: Cognitive Distortions — Complete Reference
nodes.excerpt: Cognitive distortions are habitual, automatic thought patterns that skew interpretation toward threat, failure, blame, or hopelessness. They originate from Aaron Beck's cognitive model and are the primary intervention target in CBT. Naming the pattern creates enough distance from the thought to examine it rather than automatically act on it.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Depression — Biology and Treatment
nodes.label: Depression — Biology and Treatment
nodes.excerpt: Major depressive disorder involves persistent low mood or anhedonia plus at least 4 additional symptoms for 2+ weeks, causing significant impairment. The biology is more complex than the 'chemical imbalance' framing: neuroinflammation, HPA dysregulation, and default mode network overactivity are all implicated. Treatment evidence supports CBT, behavioral activation, SSRIs/SNRIs, and exercise — with newer options (ketamine, TMS) for treatment-resistant cases.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Dopamine System — Rewards and Motivation
nodes.label: Dopamine System — Rewards and Motivation
nodes.excerpt: Dopamine is a neuromodulator involved in reward prediction error, motivation, and learning — not pleasure itself. Wolfram Schultz's Nobel-adjacent work showed dopamine neurons fire on prediction errors, not rewards per se. The wanting/liking distinction (Kent Berridge) is critical: dopamine drives wanting and pursuit, while opioid systems drive liking and enjoyment. This explains compulsive behavior without proportionate satisfaction.

nodes.label: Emotional Regulation Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Emotional regulation is the ability to influence which emotions arise, when, how intensely, and how long they last — and then to respond from choice rather than automaticity. James Gross's process model identifies five intervention points. DBT's skills hierarchy (TIPP, STOP, PLEASE) gives a practical ladder. The key distinction: regulation is not suppression — it changes the emotion, not just the expression.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/FOMO and Digital Anxiety
nodes.label: FOMO and Digital Anxiety
nodes.excerpt: FOMO and digital anxiety arise when feeds, notifications, social comparison, and constant availability make missing information feel costly. The fix is not knowing everything, but setting boundaries around inputs and values.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Grief and Loss — Stages and Modern Research
nodes.label: Grief and Loss — Stages and Modern Research
nodes.excerpt: Grief is a varied response to loss, not a fixed five-stage checklist. Modern grief research emphasizes oscillation, meaning, continuing bonds, individual differences, and support rather than one universal timeline.

nodes.label: Impostor Syndrome
nodes.excerpt: Impostor syndrome (Clance & Imes, 1978) is the persistent internal experience of intellectual fraudulence despite objective evidence of competence. It is driven by a self-perpetuating cognitive cycle that attributes success to external factors and treats mistakes as evidence of true inadequacy. Highly prevalent in software engineering — especially among high performers, in new roles, or in underrepresented groups.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Journaling for Mental Health
nodes.label: Journaling for Mental Health
nodes.excerpt: Journaling has solid research support (Pennebaker's expressive writing paradigm) for processing difficult experiences and reducing their ongoing physiological impact. Different journaling formats serve different purposes — expressive writing, gratitude, CBT thought records, decision journaling. The key distinction: reflection that moves toward insight is different from venting that reinforces rumination.

nodes.label: Learned Helplessness
nodes.excerpt: Learned helplessness (Seligman, 1967) is the reduced motivation, cognitive impairment, and passive acceptance that results from repeated exposure to uncontrollable outcomes. The key insight: the animal/person doesn't learn 'I can't escape this' — they learn 'I can't control anything,' which generalizes beyond the original situation. The reformulated model (Abramson 1978) adds attribution style as the individual difference that determines who develops helplessness vs. who doesn't.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Locus of Control (Internal vs External)
nodes.label: Locus of Control (Internal vs External)
nodes.excerpt: Locus of control (Julian Rotter, 1966) describes the generalized expectancy about whether outcomes are determined by one's own behavior (internal) vs external forces (luck, others, fate). Internal LOC correlates with better health outcomes, academic achievement, and resilience. The nuance: healthy internality is specific and calibrated — not self-blame for genuinely uncontrollable events, but persistent agency over what can actually be influenced.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Loneliness and Connection — The Health Impact
nodes.label: Loneliness and Connection — The Health Impact
nodes.excerpt: Loneliness is the felt gap between desired and actual connection, and it is associated with worse mental and physical health. Connection is a health behavior, not just a nice extra.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Meditation Types and Benefits
nodes.label: Meditation Types and Benefits
nodes.excerpt: Meditation is a family of distinct practices that train different cognitive and emotional capacities. Focused attention trains sustained concentration. Open monitoring trains metacognitive awareness. Loving-kindness trains compassion and social warmth. Body scan trains interoception. Choosing the right type depends on what the person actually needs, not a generic 'try meditation' recommendation.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Mindfulness — Evidence and Practice
nodes.label: Mindfulness — Evidence and Practice
nodes.excerpt: Mindfulness is the practice of deliberately attending to present-moment experience — thoughts, sensations, emotions — with less automatic reactivity. Jon Kabat-Zinn operationalized it in 1979 as MBSR and launched three decades of neuroscience research showing measurable structural and functional brain changes. It is evidence-supported for stress, anxiety, depression relapse, and pain — but not a universal treatment and requires care around trauma.

nodes.label: Neuroplasticity
nodes.excerpt: Neuroplasticity is the nervous system's capacity to change its structure and function through experience. It includes synaptic strengthening/weakening, structural growth, and adult neurogenesis in the hippocampus. The key principles: change is specific (practice what you want to change), change requires attention and salience, sleep consolidates it, and BDNF is the primary molecular signal. Chronic stress impairs plasticity; exercise, learning, and sleep promote it.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Perfectionism — Causes and Fixes
nodes.label: Perfectionism — Causes and Fixes
nodes.excerpt: Perfectionism is not high standards — it is a pattern where self-worth becomes contingent on achievement quality, mistakes feel threatening rather than informative, and avoidance becomes preferable to imperfect output. Frost's Multidimensional Perfectionism Scale identifies six components. Maladaptive perfectionism is a primary driver of procrastination, burnout, and chronic underperformance despite high ability.

nodes.label: Post-Traumatic Growth
nodes.excerpt: Post-traumatic growth (PTG) is positive psychological change that some people report following a struggle with highly challenging life circumstances. Tedeschi and Calhoun (1996) identified five domains: personal strength, relating to others, new possibilities, appreciation of life, and spiritual/existential change. The mechanism is shattered-assumptions rebuilding — not that trauma is good, but that rebuilding core belief structures after their destruction sometimes creates more robust ones.

nodes.label: Psychological Safety
nodes.excerpt: Psychological safety (Amy Edmondson, Harvard) is the shared belief that a team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking — speaking up, admitting mistakes, asking questions, and challenging ideas without fear of punishment or humiliation. Google's Project Aristotle identified it as the single strongest predictor of team performance. It is created primarily through leader behavior, especially how leaders respond to bad news.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Resilience — How to Build It
nodes.label: Resilience — How to Build It
nodes.excerpt: Resilience is the capacity to absorb adversity, adapt, and maintain or regain function — not the absence of distress. Research (Bonanno, APA, van der Kolk) shows resilience is not a fixed trait but a dynamic resource built through specific factors: relationships, recovery, meaning, flexible thinking, and graduated exposure to manageable challenges. It can be built deliberately.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Self-Compassion vs Self-Esteem (Kristin Neff)
nodes.label: Self-Compassion vs Self-Esteem (Kristin Neff)
nodes.excerpt: Self-compassion (Kristin Neff) means treating yourself during difficulty with the same kindness you'd offer a good friend — not excusing poor performance, but responding to failure and pain without harsh self-attack. Unlike self-esteem, it doesn't require positive self-evaluation to maintain. Research shows it predicts lower shame, less fear of failure, more motivation to improve, and better resilience than high self-esteem.

nodes.label: Serotonin System
nodes.excerpt: Serotonin (5-HT) is a neurotransmitter and signaling molecule synthesized from tryptophan, with 14+ receptor subtypes and roles spanning mood, sleep, appetite, pain, gut motility, social behavior, and cognition. ~90% of the body's serotonin is in the gut. The serotonin-depression link is more complex than 'low serotonin = depression' — a 2022 umbrella review found no consistent evidence for the serotonin deficiency theory of depression.

nodes.label: Social Comparison Theory
nodes.excerpt: Leon Festinger's 1954 social comparison theory holds that people evaluate their opinions and abilities by comparing themselves to others, especially under uncertainty. Upward comparison motivates but can produce envy/inadequacy; downward comparison boosts mood but can produce complacency. Social media amplifies harmful comparison by providing curated upward comparisons at scale. Alternative comparison targets (past self, future self) are often more productive.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Stoicism Applied to Modern Life
nodes.label: Stoicism Applied to Modern Life
nodes.excerpt: Applied Stoicism focuses on what is within one's control, responding to events with virtue and judgment, and practicing perspective before reaction. Its modern value is emotional discipline without emotional suppression.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/Stress Response — HPA Axis (Cortisol Cascade)
nodes.label: Stress Response — HPA Axis (Cortisol Cascade)
nodes.excerpt: The stress response involves two cascading systems: the fast SAM axis (seconds, adrenaline) and the slower HPA axis (minutes to hours, cortisol). Both are adaptive short-term but damaging when chronically activated. Understanding the HPA axis explains why sleep, mood, immunity, memory, and metabolism all degrade under sustained stress.

nodes.id: Resources/Mental Health & Psychology/The Role of Play in Adult Life
nodes.label: The Role of Play in Adult Life
nodes.excerpt: Play is voluntary, intrinsically motivated activity where the process is the point — not performed for external reward. Stuart Brown's research identifies seven distinct play types. The neuroscience: play activates dopaminergic circuits, promotes prefrontal plasticity, and reduces cortisol. Adults who maintain play capacity show better creativity, resilience, and emotional regulation. The anti-optimization framing: not every activity needs to produce output.

nodes.label: Message Queues
nodes.excerpt: delivery guarantees (at-most/at-least/exactly-once), Kafka internals (partitions, offsets, consumer groups, zero-copy, EOS), SQS, RabbitMQ, outbox pattern, event sourcing, backpressure.

nodes.label: Calibration and Epistemic Humility
nodes.excerpt: Calibration means your confidence matches reality over time: 70 percent confidence should be right about 70 percent of the time. Epistemic humility is the discipline of treating beliefs as improvable models rather than identity claims.

nodes.label: Fermi Estimation Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Fermi estimation is a way to approximate unknown quantities by decomposing them into smaller guesses and orders of magnitude. It is useful for sanity-checking claims, sizing systems, making decisions with incomplete data, and catching impossible numbers early.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/How to Build a Mental Model
nodes.label: How to Build a Mental Model
nodes.excerpt: A mental model is a compressed representation of how something works, useful because it lets you predict, explain, and act without memorizing every detail. Building one means identifying the system boundaries, core variables, causal relationships, feedback loops, failure modes, and places where the model breaks.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/How to Find the Crux of a Disagreement
nodes.label: How to Find the Crux of a Disagreement
nodes.excerpt: The crux of a disagreement is the belief that would actually change someone's conclusion if it changed. Finding it requires separating definitions, values, evidence, predictions, and trust judgments instead of arguing around surface claims.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/How to Read Primary Research (Not Just Summaries)
nodes.label: How to Read Primary Research (Not Just Summaries)
nodes.excerpt: Reading primary research means inspecting the actual question, methods, data, results, limitations, and incentives rather than relying only on abstracts or popular summaries. The goal is to understand what the study really supports, what it does not, and how much confidence it deserves.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/How to Update Beliefs Gracefully (Bayesian Updating)
nodes.label: How to Update Beliefs Gracefully (Bayesian Updating)
nodes.excerpt: Bayesian updating is the habit of changing confidence when new evidence arrives, rather than flipping between certainty and rejection. Updating gracefully means treating belief changes as maintenance on your model, not as a personal defeat.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/How to Write an Evergreen Note
nodes.label: How to Write an Evergreen Note
nodes.excerpt: An evergreen note is a durable, self-contained idea written so it can be reused, linked, and improved over time. Good evergreen notes have a clear claim, context, reasoning, examples, and connections instead of being raw highlights or vague summaries.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/The Difference Between Information and Knowledge
nodes.label: The Difference Between Information and Knowledge
nodes.excerpt: Information is available content; knowledge is organized, interpreted, connected, and usable understanding. The difference matters because collecting more notes, tabs, or facts does not automatically improve judgment unless the material changes what you can explain, predict, or do.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/The Map is Not the Territory
nodes.label: The Map is Not the Territory
nodes.excerpt: `The map is not the territory` means models, labels, metrics, notes, and abstractions are representations, not reality itself. The principle is a warning to use models as tools while checking them against lived evidence, edge cases, and changing conditions.

nodes.id: Resources/Meta-Learning & Epistemics/When to Trust Experts (and When Not To)
nodes.label: When to Trust Experts (and When Not To)
nodes.excerpt: Trusting experts is usually rational when the domain has real expertise, feedback loops, transparent methods, and consensus, but expert claims still need scope boundaries. Be more cautious when incentives are distorted, predictions are unfalsifiable, track records are hidden, or the expert is speaking outside their domain.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/Changelog and Release Notes Writing
nodes.label: Changelog and Release Notes Writing
nodes.excerpt: Changelogs and release notes translate project changes into useful history for users, teammates, and future maintainers. Good entries group changes by impact, explain migration needs, and distinguish features, fixes, breaking changes, security updates, and known issues.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/Code as Communication (Writing for Readers)
nodes.label: Code as Communication (Writing for Readers)
nodes.excerpt: Code is communication with future readers as much as instruction for machines. Good code makes intent, structure, constraints, and failure behavior legible so maintainers can change it without reconstructing the author's entire mental state.

nodes.label: Decision-Making Under Uncertainty
nodes.excerpt: Decision-making under uncertainty means choosing without complete information by separating known facts, assumptions, probabilities, stakes, reversibility, and feedback loops. Good decisions are judged by process quality and expected value, not only by whether the outcome happened to work.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Ask for Help Effectively (XY Problem)
nodes.label: How to Ask for Help Effectively (XY Problem)
nodes.excerpt: Asking for help effectively means sharing the real goal, current approach, observed behavior, constraints, and what has already been tried. This avoids the XY problem, where someone asks about a chosen solution instead of the underlying problem.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Do a Brag Document
nodes.label: How to Do a Brag Document
nodes.excerpt: A brag document is a running record of accomplishments, impact, feedback, responsibilities, metrics, and lessons. It helps with performance reviews, resumes, interviews, promotion packets, and personal confidence because evidence is captured before memory fades.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Give a Good Demo
nodes.label: How to Give a Good Demo
nodes.excerpt: A good demo makes value visible quickly by showing a real workflow, not by reciting implementation details. It needs a clear audience, tight setup, realistic data, a rehearsed path, backup plan, and explicit takeaway.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Onboard onto a New Codebase
nodes.label: How to Onboard onto a New Codebase
nodes.excerpt: Onboarding onto a new codebase requires building a map of the system before trying to change it: setup, tests, entry points, data flow, ownership boundaries, conventions, and deployment path. The fastest path is to make small verified changes while documenting discoveries.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Run a Design Review
nodes.label: How to Run a Design Review
nodes.excerpt: A design review is a structured critique of a proposed technical approach before implementation. Good reviews test assumptions, alternatives, interfaces, failure modes, operational risk, and maintainability without turning into taste debates.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Run a Good Retrospective
nodes.label: How to Run a Good Retrospective
nodes.excerpt: A good retrospective turns recent work into specific learning and action without becoming blame, venting, or vague process theater. It asks what happened, what helped, what hurt, what should change, and who owns the follow-up.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Say No Without Burning Bridges
nodes.label: How to Say No Without Burning Bridges
nodes.excerpt: Saying no well means protecting priorities while preserving respect, clarity, and future relationship. The best refusals are timely, direct, brief, grounded in constraints, and sometimes paired with a smaller alternative.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/How to Write a Good Technical Spec
nodes.label: How to Write a Good Technical Spec
nodes.excerpt: A good technical spec clarifies the problem, goals, non-goals, proposed design, alternatives, tradeoffs, risks, rollout, and validation plan before implementation. Its purpose is to make decisions explicit enough that reviewers can improve the plan while changes are still cheap.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/Negotiating Job Offers — Full Playbook
nodes.label: Negotiating Job Offers — Full Playbook
nodes.excerpt: Negotiating job offers means improving compensation, role fit, start date, location, title, learning opportunity, and risk while preserving the relationship. A strong negotiation uses market data, alternatives, clear priorities, gratitude, and written confirmation.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/Reading Other People's Code
nodes.label: Reading Other People's Code
nodes.excerpt: Reading other people's code is a deliberate skill: start from behavior, trace execution paths, inspect tests, map data flow, identify invariants, and resist judging style before understanding constraints. The goal is to reconstruct intent accurately enough to change the code safely.

nodes.label: Risk Management Frameworks
nodes.excerpt: Risk management frameworks help identify, assess, prioritize, mitigate, transfer, accept, and monitor things that could go wrong. The practical value is forcing risks into explicit language before they become surprises.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/Root Cause Analysis (5 Whys, Fishbone)
nodes.label: Root Cause Analysis (5 Whys, Fishbone)
nodes.excerpt: Root cause analysis looks past the visible symptom to the system conditions that allowed a failure to happen. 5 Whys and fishbone diagrams are simple tools for exploring causal chains, but they need evidence and systems thinking to avoid shallow blame.

nodes.label: Rubber Duck Debugging
nodes.excerpt: Rubber duck debugging is the practice of explaining a problem step by step to reveal hidden assumptions, missing details, and contradictions. It works because forcing a clear explanation often exposes the bug before another person needs to intervene.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/Technical Interview Preparation Guide
nodes.label: Technical Interview Preparation Guide
nodes.excerpt: Technical interview preparation should balance algorithms, system design, project storytelling, behavioral examples, communication practice, and company-specific research. The goal is not memorization alone, but reliable problem-solving under time and social pressure.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/The Art of the README
nodes.label: The Art of the README
nodes.excerpt: A README is the front door of a project: it should explain what the project is, why it exists, how to run it, how to use it, and where to go next. The best READMEs optimize for quick orientation and successful first execution.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/The Importance of Slack (Buffer Time in Schedules)
nodes.label: The Importance of Slack (Buffer Time in Schedules)
nodes.excerpt: Slack is unused capacity intentionally left in a schedule or system so it can absorb surprises, recovery, thinking, coordination, and change. Without slack, systems look efficient right up until small disruptions cascade into stress, lateness, and brittle decisions.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/The Maker's Schedule vs Manager's Schedule (Paul Graham)
nodes.label: The Maker's Schedule vs Manager's Schedule (Paul Graham)
nodes.excerpt: Paul Graham's maker-versus-manager schedule distinction explains why meetings can be cheap for managers but expensive for builders. Makers need long, uninterrupted blocks for deep work, while managers can often operate in smaller calendar slots.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/The Programmer's Brain (Felienne Hermans)
nodes.label: The Programmer's Brain (Felienne Hermans)
nodes.excerpt: The Programmer's Brain explains programming through cognition: working memory limits, long-term memory, reading strategies, chunking, naming, misconceptions, and learning transfer. Its practical takeaway is that code comprehension is a human cognitive task, not just a technical one.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/The STAR Interview Method
nodes.label: The STAR Interview Method
nodes.excerpt: The STAR method structures interview stories as Situation, Task, Action, and Result. It is useful because it turns vague claims about skill into concrete evidence of context, responsibility, decisions, and impact.

nodes.label: The XY Problem
nodes.excerpt: The XY problem happens when someone needs help with goal X but asks about attempted solution Y, causing helpers to optimize the wrong thing. The fix is to surface the original goal, constraints, and why Y seemed necessary.

nodes.id: Resources/Miscellaneous High-Value Topics/Why Meetings Are Expensive (Cost of Interruption)
nodes.label: Why Meetings Are Expensive (Cost of Interruption)
nodes.excerpt: Meetings are expensive because their cost includes every participant's time, context switching, preparation, recovery, and opportunity cost. A meeting is worth it when shared understanding or decision quality improves enough to justify that cost.

nodes.label: Writing Good Documentation
nodes.excerpt: Good documentation helps the right reader complete a real task or understand a real system with less friction. It should make audience, purpose, prerequisites, examples, edge cases, and maintenance ownership explicit.

nodes.label: Multi-Agent RL
nodes.excerpt: MARL: non-stationarity, CTDE, QMIX monotonicity, MAPPO, self-play, league play, emergent communication, credit assignment.

nodes.label: Multi-Agent Systems
nodes.excerpt: multi-agent architectures (orchestrator-worker, blackboard, peer-to-peer), emergent behaviors, failure modes, safety and control.

nodes.label: Audio Production Workflow (DAWs, Plugins)
nodes.excerpt: Explains modern audio production as a pipeline from idea capture to arrangement, recording/programming, editing, sound design, mixing, mastering/export, and revision inside a DAW. Covers MIDI, audio tracks, plugins, buses, automation, stems, reference tracks, and workflow failure modes.

nodes.label: Harmony and Chord Progressions
nodes.excerpt: Explains harmony as the movement of chords through stability, tension, color, and resolution. Covers tonic/subdominant/dominant function, roman numerals, voice leading, borrowed chords, common progressions, and why the same melody changes meaning over different chords.

nodes.label: How Streaming Royalties Work
nodes.excerpt: Explains streaming royalties as rights-flow economics, not a simple per-stream wage. Distinguishes master recording royalties from publishing royalties, labels from distributors, PROs from publishers, pro-rata pools from user-centric models, contract recoupment, territory effects, and why artist take-home varies wildly.

nodes.id: Resources/Music/Lo-Fi Music — Why It Aids Focus
nodes.label: Lo-Fi Music — Why It Aids Focus
nodes.excerpt: Explains why lo-fi can support focus through steady tempo, low lyrical load, predictable loops, soft timbres, mild novelty, noise masking, and moderate arousal. Distinguishes useful background music from distraction, and notes when silence, ambient sound, or higher-energy music works better.

nodes.label: Mixing Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Explains mixing as arranging perception through level, panning, EQ, compression, saturation, reverb, delay, automation, frequency masking, stereo placement, and reference listening. Emphasizes that the goal is emotional clarity, not making every track loud or impressive in isolation.

nodes.label: Music Genres and Their Origins
nodes.excerpt: Explains music genres as cultural, sonic, technological, and commercial categories rather than fixed boxes. Covers blues, jazz, rock, hip-hop, electronic, pop, migration, recording technology, scenes, identity, industry labeling, hybridization, and why genre history carries power and memory.

nodes.label: Music Theory Fundamentals (Scales, Chords, Intervals)
nodes.excerpt: Explains music theory as a naming system for pitch relationships: intervals, scales, chords, keys, consonance, dissonance, tension, and resolution. Focuses on why patterns feel stable or unstable and how theory helps Bobby talk about music without turning creativity into rules.

nodes.label: Rhythm and Time Signatures
nodes.excerpt: Explains rhythm as music's organization of time: beat, tempo, meter, subdivision, syncopation, groove, swing, and time signatures. Emphasizes that rhythm feels bodily because it shapes expectation, movement, and attention, not just notation.

nodes.label: Sound Design Basics (Oscillators, Filters, Envelopes)
nodes.excerpt: Explains sound design as sculpting timbre over time with oscillators, filters, envelopes, LFOs, modulation, sampling, layering, and effects. Connects waveform shape, ADSR envelopes, filter cutoff/resonance, and spatial effects to why one note can feel warm, aggressive, eerie, or futuristic.

nodes.id: Resources/Music/The Psychology of Music (Why We Like What We Like)
nodes.label: The Psychology of Music (Why We Like What We Like)
nodes.excerpt: Explains music preference through prediction, repetition, surprise, rhythm, movement, timbre, memory, identity, culture, reward, and context. Emphasizes that taste is personal but not random: brains like patterns they can partly predict and partly be surprised by.

nodes.label: Networking
nodes.excerpt: OSI model, IP/NAT/CIDR, TCP internals (handshake, sliding window, congestion control, BBR), UDP, DNS resolution, TLS 1.3 (Diffie-Hellman, forward secrecy, mTLS), HTTP/1.1 vs HTTP/2 vs HTTP/3 (QUIC).

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Anycast Routing — How CDNs and Public DNS (1.1.1.1, 8.8.8.8) Scale Globally
nodes.label: Anycast Routing — How CDNs and Public DNS (1.1.1.1, 8.8.8.8) Scale Globally
nodes.excerpt: Anycast advertises the same IP prefix from multiple locations so routing sends users to a nearby or preferred instance, commonly used by CDNs, DNS resolvers, DDoS platforms, and edge services. It scales globally because BGP path selection steers clients without requiring them to know which site will answer.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/BGP Deep Dive — How the Internet Routes Between Autonomous Systems
nodes.label: BGP Deep Dive — How the Internet Routes Between Autonomous Systems
nodes.excerpt: BGP is the interdomain routing protocol that lets autonomous systems advertise IP prefixes and choose paths across the internet. Its power comes from policy: operators influence routing with AS paths, local preference, MED, communities, filtering, and peering relationships rather than only shortest path.

nodes.label: BGP — Internet Routing
nodes.excerpt: BGP is the inter-domain routing protocol that lets autonomous systems announce which IP prefixes they can reach. It makes the internet scalable and decentralized, but its trust model also enables route leaks, hijacks, outages, and policy-driven routing surprises.

nodes.label: Bluetooth Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Bluetooth is a short-range wireless architecture for peripherals, audio, sensors, and device-to-device communication. Classic Bluetooth and Bluetooth Low Energy serve different needs, which explains why headphones, controllers, trackers, keyboards, and IoT devices behave differently.

nodes.label: DHCP
nodes.excerpt: DHCP automatically assigns IP configuration to devices on a network, including address, subnet mask, gateway, DNS servers, and lease time. It explains why devices usually connect without manual setup and why IP conflicts, stale leases, or wrong gateways can break local networking.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/DNS — How It Works (Recursive Resolution, TTL, Caching)
nodes.label: DNS — How It Works (Recursive Resolution, TTL, Caching)
nodes.excerpt: DNS turns human-readable names into network addresses through a distributed hierarchy of resolvers, root servers, TLD servers, and authoritative name servers. TTLs and caching make DNS fast, but they also explain propagation delays and stale answers.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/DOCSIS — How Cable Internet Delivers Bandwidth Over Coax
nodes.label: DOCSIS — How Cable Internet Delivers Bandwidth Over Coax
nodes.excerpt: DOCSIS is the cable-internet standard that lets cable modems carry IP traffic over coaxial networks originally built for TV. It uses downstream and upstream channels, a cable modem termination system, signal levels, modulation, and provisioning to share neighborhood capacity among many subscribers.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Ethernet Physical Layer — Cables Explained (Cat5e-6-6a-8, SFP, Fiber vs Copper)
nodes.label: Ethernet Physical Layer — Cables Explained (Cat5e-6-6a-8, SFP, Fiber vs Copper)
nodes.excerpt: Ethernet physical-layer troubleshooting covers copper and fiber media, cable categories, SFP modules, link negotiation, duplex, PoE, optics, and error counters. Many network problems that look like routing or application issues start as bad physical signaling.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Firewall Types (Stateful vs Stateless vs NGFW)
nodes.label: Firewall Types (Stateful vs Stateless vs NGFW)
nodes.excerpt: Firewalls enforce network traffic policy, from simple stateless rules to stateful connection tracking and next-generation inspection. Good firewall design starts with explicit trust boundaries, least privilege, logging, and rules that are understandable enough to maintain.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/GPON and XGS-PON — How Fiber-to-the-Home (FTTH) Works
nodes.label: GPON and XGS-PON — How Fiber-to-the-Home (FTTH) Works
nodes.excerpt: GPON and XGS-PON are passive optical network technologies that deliver fiber-to-the-home by sharing one provider optical line terminal across many customer ONTs through passive splitters. GPON is typically asymmetric lower-capacity service, while XGS-PON supports higher symmetric capacity on newer optics.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/HTTP-1.1 vs HTTP-2 vs HTTP-3 (QUIC)
nodes.label: HTTP-1.1 vs HTTP-2 vs HTTP-3 (QUIC)
nodes.excerpt: HTTP evolved from simple per-request patterns toward multiplexed, lower-latency transports: HTTP/1.1 uses TCP connections, HTTP/2 multiplexes streams over TCP, and HTTP/3 runs over QUIC on UDP. The main tradeoff is reducing latency and head-of-line blocking while preserving web compatibility.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/How 5G Networks Work (RAN, Core Network, mmWave vs Sub-6GHz, Network Slicing)
nodes.label: How 5G Networks Work (RAN, Core Network, mmWave vs Sub-6GHz, Network Slicing)
nodes.excerpt: 5G networks combine radio access, spectrum bands, backhaul, a cloud-native core, subscriber identity, mobility management, and policy control to deliver mobile broadband and specialized services. The practical tradeoff is that mmWave offers high capacity over short ranges while sub-6GHz provides broader coverage with less peak throughput.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/How Ethernet Frames Work (MAC Addresses, Frame Structure, CSMA-CD, Full Duplex)
nodes.label: How Ethernet Frames Work (MAC Addresses, Frame Structure, CSMA-CD, Full Duplex)
nodes.excerpt: Ethernet frames are Layer 2 envelopes that carry payloads across a local link using source and destination MAC addresses, EtherType, optional VLAN tags, payload data, and a frame check sequence. Understanding frames explains how switches forward traffic, why broadcasts stay local to a VLAN, and why physical errors appear as CRC/FCS counters.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/How a Network Engineer Works with an ISP (Escalation, NOC, BGP Communities)
nodes.label: How a Network Engineer Works with an ISP (Escalation, NOC, BGP Communities)
nodes.excerpt: Working with an ISP is evidence-driven escalation: prove the customer edge, circuit, packet loss, routing, DNS, and BGP symptoms before asking the provider NOC to act. Good escalation includes timestamps, circuit IDs, source and destination IPs, traceroutes, interface counters, BGP session state, and requested policy changes such as communities.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/How a Network Switch Learns and Forwards (MAC Table, ARP, Flooding, Aging)
nodes.label: How a Network Switch Learns and Forwards (MAC Table, ARP, Flooding, Aging)
nodes.excerpt: An Ethernet switch learns source MAC addresses per VLAN, stores them in a MAC/CAM table, forwards known unicast frames to the right port, and floods broadcasts or unknown unicasts. Most switching mysteries come from stale MAC entries, loops, VLAN mismatches, ARP confusion, or a device moving between ports.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/How an ISP Works — Tiers, Transit Agreements, and Peering
nodes.label: How an ISP Works — Tiers, Transit Agreements, and Peering
nodes.excerpt: An ISP connects customers to the internet by combining last-mile access, aggregation networks, core routing, DNS/resolver services, transit, private peering, public peering, and operational support. The business relationships behind transit and peering directly affect latency, reachability, redundancy, and escalation paths.

nodes.label: IPv4 vs IPv6
nodes.excerpt: IPv4 and IPv6 are internet-layer addressing systems, with IPv4 limited to 32-bit addresses and IPv6 using a much larger 128-bit space. IPv6 reduces the need for NAT and improves address availability, but coexistence with IPv4 creates transition and compatibility complexity.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/IPv6 Transition Strategies (Dual Stack, NAT64, 6to4, Tunneling)
nodes.label: IPv6 Transition Strategies (Dual Stack, NAT64, 6to4, Tunneling)
nodes.excerpt: IPv6 transition strategies let networks adopt IPv6 while still reaching IPv4-only systems, commonly through dual stack, NAT64/DNS64, tunnels, or older mechanisms such as 6to4. The operational challenge is keeping DNS, routing, firewall policy, observability, and application behavior correct across two protocol families.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Internet Exchange Points (IXPs) — Where ISPs Physically Connect
nodes.label: Internet Exchange Points (IXPs) — Where ISPs Physically Connect
nodes.excerpt: An internet exchange point is a shared switching fabric where networks physically connect and exchange traffic through public peering. IXPs reduce cost and latency by letting ISPs, CDNs, cloud providers, and enterprises exchange traffic directly instead of sending it through transit providers.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/MPLS — How Enterprise WANs Used to Work
nodes.label: MPLS — How Enterprise WANs Used to Work
nodes.excerpt: MPLS forwards packets through provider networks using labels rather than normal destination-IP lookups at every hop, enabling carrier-managed VPNs, traffic engineering, and QoS classes. It powered many enterprise WANs before SD-WAN and internet-first architectures became common.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Multicast Networking — IGMP, PIM, and How IPTV Works
nodes.label: Multicast Networking — IGMP, PIM, and How IPTV Works
nodes.excerpt: Multicast sends one stream to many receivers without duplicating traffic for every client, using IGMP on local networks and PIM across routed networks. It is useful for IPTV, market data, conferencing, and discovery, but it requires explicit control-plane support so unwanted streams do not flood the LAN.

nodes.label: NAT and Port Forwarding
nodes.excerpt: NAT lets many private devices share one public IPv4 address by translating internal addresses and ports. Port forwarding creates explicit inbound mappings, which is useful for hosting services but needs careful firewalling and security.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Network Address Translation (NAT) — How It Actually Works, and Why It's a Hack
nodes.label: Network Address Translation (NAT) — How It Actually Works, and Why It's a Hack
nodes.excerpt: Network Address Translation rewrites packet addresses and often ports so many private hosts can share limited public IPv4 addresses. It works through stateful translation tables, but it complicates inbound connectivity, peer-to-peer protocols, logging, geolocation, and troubleshooting.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Network Engineer's Toolkit — ping, traceroute, mtr, nslookup, dig, netstat, ss
nodes.label: Network Engineer's Toolkit — ping, traceroute, mtr, nslookup, dig, netstat, ss
nodes.excerpt: The network engineer's CLI toolkit maps symptoms to evidence: reachability with ping, path behavior with traceroute and mtr, DNS answers with nslookup and dig, and socket state with netstat or ss. These tools are useful because each tests a different layer instead of vaguely asking whether the internet works.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Network Monitoring — SNMP, NetFlow, sFlow, and Prometheus Exporters
nodes.label: Network Monitoring — SNMP, NetFlow, sFlow, and Prometheus Exporters
nodes.excerpt: Network monitoring combines device health, interface counters, flow records, logs, synthetic checks, and alerts to show what the network is doing before users complain. SNMP, NetFlow, sFlow, and Prometheus exporters answer different questions, so good monitoring uses them together rather than treating one metric as the whole truth.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Network Segmentation and DMZ Architecture
nodes.label: Network Segmentation and DMZ Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Network segmentation divides infrastructure into zones with different trust levels, while a DMZ exposes public-facing services without giving the internet direct access to the internal LAN. The real security value comes from enforced routing, firewall rules, identity controls, logging, and documented exceptions.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/OSI Model — All 7 Layers with Examples
nodes.label: OSI Model — All 7 Layers with Examples
nodes.excerpt: The OSI model is a conceptual seven-layer map for network communication, from physical signals up through applications. Real protocols do not always fit perfectly, but the model is useful for debugging by asking which layer a failure belongs to.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/OSPF — How Routers Discover and Share Route Information (Link-State)
nodes.label: OSPF — How Routers Discover and Share Route Information (Link-State)
nodes.excerpt: OSPF is a link-state interior gateway protocol where routers form adjacencies, flood link-state advertisements, build a shared topology database, and run shortest-path calculations. It is used inside organizations because it converges faster and models topology more explicitly than simple static routing.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/QoS and Traffic Shaping — How Networks Prioritize VoIP, Video, and Bulk Data
nodes.label: QoS and Traffic Shaping — How Networks Prioritize VoIP, Video, and Bulk Data
nodes.excerpt: QoS and traffic shaping manage congestion by classifying traffic, marking priority, queuing packets, limiting rates, and protecting latency-sensitive flows like voice and video. QoS cannot create bandwidth, but it can decide which packets suffer first when a link is full.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Real Scenario - DHCP Failure — Why IPs Stop Being Assigned and How to Fix It
nodes.label: Real Scenario - DHCP Failure — Why IPs Stop Being Assigned and How to Fix It
nodes.excerpt: DHCP failure means clients cannot complete the Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledge exchange that gives them an IP address, gateway, DNS servers, and lease options. Diagnosis focuses on broadcast reachability, DHCP relay, scope health, VLAN placement, and whether replies make it back to the client.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Real Scenario - DNS Failure — Why a Site Stops Resolving and the Diagnostic Chain
nodes.label: Real Scenario - DNS Failure — Why a Site Stops Resolving and the Diagnostic Chain
nodes.excerpt: DNS failure diagnosis separates local resolver settings, cache behavior, recursive resolver failure, authoritative DNS problems, split-horizon DNS, and application-layer confusion. A useful DNS check proves whether the name, resolver, network path, or record data is wrong.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Real Scenario - Diagnosing Slow Network — Bandwidth, Latency, Jitter, Packet Loss
nodes.label: Real Scenario - Diagnosing Slow Network — Bandwidth, Latency, Jitter, Packet Loss
nodes.excerpt: Slow-network diagnosis separates bandwidth saturation, latency, jitter, packet loss, retransmissions, DNS delay, Wi-Fi quality, duplex/link problems, and application slowness. The goal is to identify which performance dimension is bad instead of treating all slowness as the same problem.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Real Scenario - Setting Up a Small Business Network (Router, Switch, AP, VLAN Plan)
nodes.label: Real Scenario - Setting Up a Small Business Network (Router, Switch, AP, VLAN Plan)
nodes.excerpt: A small business network should define ISP handoff, router/firewall, managed switch, access points, VLANs, DHCP scopes, DNS, guest isolation, management access, backups, and monitoring before devices are plugged in. The goal is a simple design that can be troubleshot and expanded without becoming a flat, undocumented LAN.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Real Scenario - You Have Power but No Internet — Full Diagnostic Walkthrough
nodes.label: Real Scenario - You Have Power but No Internet — Full Diagnostic Walkthrough
