nodes.excerpt: A full no-internet diagnostic starts by separating physical link, local IP configuration, gateway reachability, DNS, routing, firewall/NAT, ISP, and application-layer problems. The point is to avoid vague reboot-and-hope troubleshooting by proving exactly where packets stop.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Routing Tables and Longest-Prefix Match — How a Packet Finds Its Way
nodes.label: Routing Tables and Longest-Prefix Match — How a Packet Finds Its Way
nodes.excerpt: Routing tables map destination prefixes to next hops or interfaces, and longest-prefix match chooses the most specific matching route for each packet. This explains why a /32 host route beats a /24 subnet route, why default routes work, and why overlapping routes can create surprising paths.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/SD-WAN — Software-Defined Enterprise WAN and Why It Replaced MPLS
nodes.label: SD-WAN — Software-Defined Enterprise WAN and Why It Replaced MPLS
nodes.excerpt: SD-WAN builds encrypted, policy-driven overlays across multiple underlays such as broadband, LTE/5G, DIA, and MPLS so applications can use the best available path. It displaced many MPLS-only designs by adding central policy, path measurement, faster failover, and cheaper internet links.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Software-Defined Networking (SDN) — Separating Control Plane from Data Plane
nodes.label: Software-Defined Networking (SDN) — Separating Control Plane from Data Plane
nodes.excerpt: Software-defined networking separates network control decisions from packet forwarding so policy, topology, and automation can be managed centrally while devices handle the data plane. SDN shows up in data centers, cloud networks, campus fabrics, SD-WAN, and network virtualization.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Spanning Tree Protocol (STP - RSTP - MSTP) — Loop Prevention in Switched Networks
nodes.label: Spanning Tree Protocol (STP - RSTP - MSTP) — Loop Prevention in Switched Networks
nodes.excerpt: Spanning Tree Protocol prevents Layer 2 loops by electing a root bridge, choosing forwarding and blocking paths, and disabling redundant links until they are needed. STP matters because Ethernet loops can create broadcast storms, MAC flapping, and network-wide outages in seconds.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Subnetting and CIDR — How IP Ranges Are Carved Up (with Examples)
nodes.label: Subnetting and CIDR — How IP Ranges Are Carved Up (with Examples)
nodes.excerpt: Subnetting and CIDR describe how IP address space is divided into prefixes such as /24, /26, or /30 so networks can be routed, segmented, and documented. The practical skill is knowing which addresses belong to a subnet, which addresses are usable by hosts, and how prefix length changes capacity and routing behavior.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/TCP vs UDP — When to Use Each
nodes.label: TCP vs UDP — When to Use Each
nodes.excerpt: TCP provides reliable, ordered byte streams, while UDP provides lightweight datagrams without built-in delivery guarantees. The choice depends on whether the application values reliability and ordering more than latency, control, and tolerance for packet loss.

nodes.label: TLS Handshake Step-by-Step
nodes.excerpt: A TLS handshake lets a client and server agree on encryption keys, authenticate the server certificate, and create a secure channel. Understanding the steps helps debug HTTPS failures, certificate errors, protocol mismatches, and latency during connection setup.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/The Last Mile — How Internet Gets to Your Home (DSL, Cable, GPON Fiber)
nodes.label: The Last Mile — How Internet Gets to Your Home (DSL, Cable, GPON Fiber)
nodes.excerpt: The last mile is the access link between a customer site and the provider network, using technologies such as DSL over copper pairs, DOCSIS over coax, GPON/XGS-PON over fiber, fixed wireless, or cellular. Many internet outages start here because the physical medium, signal levels, provisioning, and neighborhood aggregation all affect reliability.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/VLANs — Why Networks Are Segmented and How Trunking Works (802.1Q)
nodes.label: VLANs — Why Networks Are Segmented and How Trunking Works (802.1Q)
nodes.excerpt: VLANs split one physical switching fabric into multiple Layer 2 broadcast domains, usually so users, servers, guests, voice, cameras, and management traffic can be separated. 802.1Q trunking carries multiple VLANs over one link by tagging Ethernet frames with VLAN IDs.

nodes.label: VPN Protocols (WireGuard, OpenVPN, IPsec)
nodes.excerpt: VPN protocols create encrypted tunnels across untrusted networks, but they differ in complexity, performance, compatibility, and operational model. WireGuard is modern and compact, OpenVPN is flexible and widely supported, and IPsec is deeply integrated into many enterprise and site-to-site setups.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/VXLAN — Stretching L2 Networks Over L3 (Used in Data Centers and VMware)
nodes.label: VXLAN — Stretching L2 Networks Over L3 (Used in Data Centers and VMware)
nodes.excerpt: VXLAN is an overlay technology that encapsulates Layer 2 Ethernet frames inside UDP/IP packets so virtual networks can span a Layer 3 underlay. It is common in data centers because VNIs provide far more tenant segments than VLAN IDs and allow workloads to move across routed fabrics.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Wi-Fi Authentication Deep Dive (WPA2-WPA3, PSK, 802.1X - EAP - RADIUS)
nodes.label: Wi-Fi Authentication Deep Dive (WPA2-WPA3, PSK, 802.1X - EAP - RADIUS)
nodes.excerpt: Wi-Fi authentication controls who can join a wireless network using methods such as WPA2/WPA3 personal pre-shared keys or enterprise 802.1X with EAP and RADIUS. Authentication problems must be separated from RF association, VLAN assignment, DHCP, DNS, and captive portal behavior.

nodes.label: Wi-Fi Standards (802.11 a-b-g-n-ac-ax)
nodes.excerpt: Wi-Fi standards describe generations of wireless networking, from early 802.11 variants through Wi-Fi 4, 5, and 6. Real-world performance depends less on the headline standard and more on spectrum, channel width, interference, antenna design, distance, walls, and client support.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/Wireshark and Packet Analysis — Reading a Full TCP Handshake
nodes.label: Wireshark and Packet Analysis — Reading a Full TCP Handshake
nodes.excerpt: Wireshark makes packet captures readable by decoding frames, IP packets, TCP state, DNS, TLS, and application protocols. Reading a TCP handshake means verifying SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK, options, timing, retransmissions, and resets before blaming higher layers.

nodes.label: Zero Trust Networking
nodes.excerpt: Zero trust networking assumes network location is not enough to prove trust, so access decisions should depend on identity, device posture, policy, and continuous verification. The goal is not adding friction everywhere, but reducing implicit trust after a user or device gets onto a network.

nodes.id: Resources/Networking & Protocols/tcpdump — Capturing and Reading Raw Packets from the CLI
nodes.label: tcpdump — Capturing and Reading Raw Packets from the CLI
nodes.excerpt: tcpdump captures packets from the command line so a network engineer can prove what actually left, arrived, retransmitted, or never appeared. It is most useful when higher-level tools disagree or when DHCP, DNS, TCP, TLS, or firewall behavior needs packet-level evidence.

nodes.label: Amygdala and Emotional Processing
nodes.excerpt: The amygdala is not the 'fear center' — it's the brain's relevance detector, flagging emotionally significant information (threats, rewards, novelty) and coordinating responses across memory, attention, hormones, and body. Fear conditioning happens in the lateral nucleus via NMDA-dependent LTP; extinction creates new safety learning in vmPFC (not erasure of fear); PTSD involves impaired vmPFC→amygdala inhibition.

nodes.label: Attention Networks in the Brain
nodes.excerpt: Attention depends on interacting brain networks that support alertness, orienting, executive control, salience detection, and internally focused thought. Focus is not one switch; it is a dynamic allocation system.

nodes.label: Brain Plasticity in Adults
nodes.excerpt: Adult brains remain plastic through learning, practice, therapy, injury recovery, and environment, but plasticity is constrained by biology, attention, repetition, sleep, and context. Change is possible without being effortless.

nodes.label: Brain Regions and Functions (Simplified Map)
nodes.excerpt: The brain is organized into lobes, subcortical structures, and networks — each with primary functional contributions, but no complex behavior belongs to one region alone. Key regions include the prefrontal cortex (executive control), amygdala (emotional salience), hippocampus (episodic memory), basal ganglia (action selection/habit), thalamus (relay), and cerebellum (timing/prediction). Understanding which region disruptions produce which symptoms is the practical payoff.

nodes.label: Default Mode Network
nodes.excerpt: The Default Mode Network (DMN) is the brain's self-simulation system — active during rest, mind-wandering, self-referential thought, social cognition, and future planning. It anti-correlates with the Task Positive Network (TPN); poor DMN suppression during tasks is associated with ADHD and mind-wandering. In depression, the DMN hyperconnects into rumination loops. Meditation and psychedelics both significantly alter DMN activity.

nodes.label: Hippocampus and Memory Formation
nodes.excerpt: The hippocampus is the brain's memory indexer — it binds the scattered cortical traces of an experience into a retrievable record and replays them to the cortex during sleep for long-term consolidation. The DG performs pattern separation; CA3 enables pattern completion; CA1 integrates and outputs. Patient H.M.'s bilateral hippocampal resection proved this structure is essential for new declarative memory but not procedural learning or working memory.

nodes.id: Resources/Neuroscience/How Exercise Affects the Brain (BDNF, Neurogenesis)
nodes.label: How Exercise Affects the Brain (BDNF, Neurogenesis)
nodes.excerpt: Exercise affects the brain through blood flow, neurotrophic factors like BDNF, mood regulation, sleep, insulin sensitivity, inflammation, and possibly hippocampal neurogenesis. Human claims should be careful because mechanisms are easier to show in animals than in people.

nodes.label: How Sleep Consolidates Memory
nodes.excerpt: Sleep supports memory consolidation by replaying, stabilizing, and integrating learning across neural systems. Deep NREM and REM appear to support different aspects of memory, emotion, and abstraction.

nodes.label: Interoception — Body Awareness and Emotions
nodes.excerpt: Interoception is the brain's sensing and interpretation of internal body signals such as heartbeat, breath, hunger, temperature, pain, and gut sensations. It shapes emotion, anxiety, self-awareness, and regulation.

nodes.label: Long-Term Potentiation (LTP) and Memory Formation
nodes.excerpt: Long-term potentiation (LTP) is the lasting strengthening of synaptic connections following coordinated activity — the leading cellular model for how learning physically changes the brain. The NMDA receptor acts as a coincidence detector (requiring both pre and postsynaptic activity to open), triggering CaMKII signaling and AMPA receptor insertion. Early LTP lasts hours; late LTP requires protein synthesis and can last years.

nodes.id: Resources/Neuroscience/Neurochemistry Overview (Dopamine, Serotonin, GABA, Glutamate, Norepinephrine)
nodes.label: Neurochemistry Overview (Dopamine, Serotonin, GABA, Glutamate, Norepinephrine)
nodes.excerpt: Neurotransmitters are context-dependent modulators, not simple 'happy chemicals' — the same molecule produces different effects depending on receptor type, brain region, and circuit state. This note covers synthesis, release, receptor families, and behavioral roles for glutamate, GABA, dopamine, serotonin, norepinephrine, acetylcholine, and endocannabinoids — plus why the 'chemical imbalance' model of mental illness is an oversimplification.

nodes.label: Neuron Structure and Electrochemical Signaling
nodes.excerpt: Neurons signal through action potentials — electrical pulses generated by ion channel dynamics — and communicate across synapses via neurotransmitter release. Understanding the mechanism (resting potential, threshold, saltatory conduction, vesicle release) grounds all higher neuroscience: plasticity, drugs, psychiatric conditions, and brain computation.

nodes.label: Prefrontal Cortex and Decision-Making
nodes.excerpt: The prefrontal cortex (PFC) is the seat of executive function — working memory, inhibitory control, planning, goal maintenance, and value-based decision-making. Different PFC subdivisions do different jobs (dlPFC = working memory, OFC = reward value, vmPFC = somatic markers, ACC = error monitoring). Stress and sleep deprivation selectively impair PFC function, pushing behavior toward reactive amygdala-driven choices.

nodes.label: Psychedelics and Neuroplasticity (Current Research)
nodes.excerpt: Current psychedelic research suggests compounds like psilocybin can acutely alter brain network dynamics and may promote plasticity-related processes, often through serotonin 5-HT2A pathways. The field is promising but early, regulated, and not suitable for casual self-treatment framing.

nodes.id: Resources/Neuroscience/Synaptic Plasticity (Hebbian Learning — Neurons that Fire Together)
nodes.label: Synaptic Plasticity (Hebbian Learning — Neurons that Fire Together)
nodes.excerpt: Synaptic plasticity is the ability of synapses to strengthen (LTP) or weaken (LTD) based on activity patterns. Hebbian learning — 'neurons that fire together wire together' — is the intuitive principle; STDP, CaMKII signaling, and AMPA receptor trafficking are the actual mechanisms. Plasticity explains why practice, attention, and emotional salience all physically change the brain.

nodes.label: The Gut-Brain Connection (Enteric Nervous System)
nodes.excerpt: The gut-brain connection involves the enteric nervous system, vagus nerve, immune signals, hormones, microbial metabolites, and nutrient sensing. It is real biology, but many consumer claims outrun what can be personalized today.

nodes.label: Notification System Design
nodes.excerpt: End-to-end notification system design covering aggregation, two-tier pipelines, reliable delivery, the outbox pattern, and a comparison against Meta's production system.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Alcohol and Health — What the Research Says
nodes.label: Alcohol and Health — What the Research Says
nodes.excerpt: Alcohol is a psychoactive calorie source linked to injuries, dependence, liver disease, sleep disruption, cancer risk, and poorer training recovery. Current risk framing is simple: less alcohol generally means lower health risk, and any claimed benefit is not a reason to start drinking.

nodes.label: Anti-Inflammatory Foods
nodes.excerpt: Anti-inflammatory eating is a dietary pattern, not a list of miracle ingredients: high-fiber plants, legumes, fish or omega-3 sources, olive oil, nuts, adequate protein, and fewer ultra-processed foods. It matters because chronic low-grade inflammation tracks with metabolic, cardiovascular, and gut health, but food cannot cancel out poor sleep, inactivity, smoking, or excess alcohol.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Caffeine Timing and Adenosine Clearance
nodes.label: Caffeine Timing and Adenosine Clearance
nodes.excerpt: Caffeine timing matters because caffeine blocks adenosine receptors for hours without removing the underlying sleep pressure. The practical rule is to use caffeine early and intentionally, then verify with sleep quality rather than assuming 'I can fall asleep' means it had no cost.

nodes.label: Caffeine — Pharmacology and Optimization
nodes.excerpt: Caffeine improves alertness and performance mainly by blocking adenosine receptors, but its half-life, tolerance, anxiety effects, and sleep cost make timing more important than dose escalation. A practical range is often 1-3 mg/kg for focus and 3-6 mg/kg for performance, with late-day use treated as borrowing from sleep.

nodes.label: Caloric Deficit and Surplus
nodes.excerpt: A caloric deficit drives weight loss and a surplus drives weight gain, but body composition depends on protein, resistance training, sleep, and the size of the energy gap. The practical skill is choosing a deficit or surplus small enough to repeat and large enough to measure.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Creatine — Benefits, Safety, and Protocol
nodes.label: Creatine — Benefits, Safety, and Protocol
nodes.excerpt: Creatine monohydrate is one of the best-supported supplements for repeated high-intensity performance, strength training volume, and lean-mass support. The practical protocol is simple: 3-5 g/day consistently, with optional loading if faster saturation matters.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Glycemic Index and Blood Sugar Management
nodes.label: Glycemic Index and Blood Sugar Management
nodes.excerpt: Glycemic index ranks carbohydrate foods by blood-glucose response in isolation, while glycemic load adds portion size and is usually more practical. Real blood sugar depends on fiber, protein, fat, food form, sleep, stress, exercise, insulin sensitivity, and diabetes status.

nodes.label: Gut Microbiome Basics
nodes.excerpt: The gut microbiome is the ecosystem of bacteria, fungi, viruses, and other microbes that metabolize fiber, produce short-chain fatty acids, train immune responses, and interact with digestion and mood. The actionable basics are dietary diversity, fiber, fermented foods if tolerated, sleep, stress management, and caution with unnecessary antibiotics.

nodes.label: Gut-Brain Axis
nodes.excerpt: The gut-brain axis is bidirectional signaling between the gut, microbiome, immune system, endocrine system, vagus nerve, and brain. It explains why stress affects digestion and diet can affect physiology, while also setting a boundary: most mood claims from probiotics and gut products outrun the evidence.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Hydration — How Much, When, and Electrolytes
nodes.label: Hydration — How Much, When, and Electrolytes
nodes.excerpt: Hydration is fluid balance, not a universal gallon-a-day rule. Total water needs depend on body size, sweat rate, climate, food intake, sodium, exercise, illness, and medications; electrolytes matter most with heavy sweat, heat, long endurance, sauna, vomiting, diarrhea, or low-sodium diets.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Intermittent Fasting — Methods and Evidence
nodes.label: Intermittent Fasting — Methods and Evidence
nodes.excerpt: Intermittent fasting restricts eating windows or fasting days and often works by reducing opportunities to eat, not by bypassing energy balance. It can be a useful adherence tool, but it is not universally superior and can conflict with training, protein targets, medications, or eating-disorder risk.

nodes.label: Macronutrients — Protein, Fat, Carbohydrates
nodes.excerpt: Macronutrients are the calorie-bearing levers of diet design: protein provides amino acids for tissue repair, fat supports cell membranes and hormones, and carbohydrates replenish glycogen for high-output work. The useful question is not which macro is morally best, but which mix supports Kenny's goal, training, satiety, labs, and adherence.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Magnesium — Uses, Forms, and Deficiency
nodes.label: Magnesium — Uses, Forms, and Deficiency
nodes.excerpt: Magnesium is an essential mineral for ATP handling, muscle and nerve function, blood pressure regulation, glucose metabolism, and hundreds of enzyme reactions. Food-first intake is safest; supplements differ by form, and the adult upper limit for supplemental magnesium is 350 mg/day unless medically supervised.

nodes.label: Meal Timing and Performance
nodes.excerpt: Meal timing affects training energy, recovery, digestion, and sleep, while total daily calories and protein still dominate body composition. Timing matters most for hard sessions, long sessions, two-a-day training, and people whose digestion or sleep is sensitive.

nodes.label: Metabolic Rate and TDEE
nodes.excerpt: Metabolic rate and TDEE estimate daily energy expenditure from resting metabolism, food digestion, exercise, and non-exercise movement. Calculators are useful starting guesses, but body-weight trends and step/activity data are the calibration system.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Micronutrients — Key Vitamins and Minerals
nodes.label: Micronutrients — Key Vitamins and Minerals
nodes.excerpt: Micronutrients are vitamins and minerals required in small amounts for metabolism, blood, bone, thyroid, immunity, and nervous system function. Deficiency and excess both matter, so the serious approach is diet diversity plus targeted labs or supplementation when risk is real.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Omega-3 Fatty Acids — EPA, DHA, and Inflammation
nodes.label: Omega-3 Fatty Acids — EPA, DHA, and Inflammation
nodes.excerpt: Omega-3s matter most in their long-chain EPA and DHA forms, which come mainly from fatty fish or algae and influence cell membranes, triglycerides, and inflammatory signaling. Supplement value depends on fish intake, dose, product quality, bleeding/medication context, and the specific outcome Kenny cares about.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Processed vs Whole Foods — What Matters
nodes.label: Processed vs Whole Foods — What Matters
nodes.excerpt: Processing exists on a spectrum: frozen vegetables, yogurt, olive oil, bread, protein powder, and candy are processed in very different ways. The meaningful question is whether a food pattern improves satiety, protein, fiber, micronutrients, and energy control, not whether every food is perfectly 'natural.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Protein Intake for Muscle Building and Recovery
nodes.label: Protein Intake for Muscle Building and Recovery
nodes.excerpt: Protein intake supports hypertrophy, recovery, satiety, and lean-mass retention, with daily total intake doing most of the work. For active adults, roughly 1.6-2.2 g/kg/day split across 3-5 protein-rich meals is a practical evidence-based target.

nodes.label: Supplements Worth Taking (Evidence-Based)
nodes.excerpt: Evidence-based supplements solve specific problems: deficiency, convenience, performance, or clinician-directed treatment. The boring short list is usually creatine, caffeine, protein powder, vitamin D when low, omega-3 when fish intake is low, electrolytes for heavy sweat, and targeted micronutrients based on diet or labs.

nodes.id: Resources/Nutrition & Diet/Vitamin D — Importance and Dosing
nodes.label: Vitamin D — Importance and Dosing
nodes.excerpt: Vitamin D supports calcium absorption, bone mineralization, muscle function, and immune signaling, with deficiency risk shaped by sun exposure, skin tone, latitude, season, age, body size, and malabsorption. Dosing should be risk- and lab-aware because both deficiency and chronic high-dose supplementation can cause harm.

nodes.label: OS Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: processes (fork/exec/CoW), virtual memory (page tables, MMU, TLB), file descriptors, I/O models (epoll, io_uring), concurrency primitives, CFS scheduling, signals, namespaces, cgroups.

nodes.label: Observability
nodes.excerpt: monitoring vs observability, structured logging, log levels, metrics (RED/USE methods, Prometheus, PromQL), distributed tracing (spans, context propagation, sampling), OpenTelemetry, SLOs/error budgets.

nodes.label: Observability Case Studies
nodes.excerpt: production debugging guide using logs, metrics, traces, SLOs, alerts, postmortems, and case studies for latency, queues, databases, and error spikes.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Finance/Asset Allocation by Age and Risk Tolerance
nodes.label: Asset Allocation by Age and Risk Tolerance
nodes.excerpt: Asset allocation divides money across stocks, bonds, cash, and other assets based on time horizon, risk capacity, risk tolerance, liquidity needs, and failure consequences; age is only a rough proxy. Surface this when discussing portfolio risk, rebalancing, target-date funds, or why investment choices should follow goals rather than vibes.

nodes.label: Budgeting Methods (50-30-20, Zero-Based, Envelope)
nodes.excerpt: Budgeting methods are behavior systems for directing cash flow, reducing surprise, and aligning spending with priorities; the right method depends on income volatility, cognitive load, debt, goals, and emotional friction. Surface this when someone needs a practical money system rather than moralizing about discipline.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Finance/Compound Interest — The Eighth Wonder of the World
nodes.label: Compound Interest — The Eighth Wonder of the World
nodes.excerpt: Compound interest is growth on prior growth: time, rate, contribution consistency, fees, taxes, and drawdowns determine whether compounding works for or against you. Surface this when explaining why starting early, avoiding high-interest debt, minimizing fees, and staying invested matter more than clever financial moves.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Finance/Credit Scores — How They Work and How to Improve Them
nodes.label: Credit Scores — How They Work and How to Improve Them
nodes.excerpt: Credit scores estimate repayment risk from credit-report data, with payment history and utilization usually dominating, but models, lenders, and use cases differ. Surface this when discussing loans, apartments, credit cards, report errors, utilization, or why credit improvement is mostly boring consistency.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Finance/Debt — Types and Payoff Strategies (Avalanche vs Snowball)
nodes.label: Debt — Types and Payoff Strategies (Avalanche vs Snowball)
nodes.excerpt: Debt should be evaluated by interest rate, collateral, tax treatment, amortization, flexibility, default consequences, and psychology; avalanche and snowball strategies trade mathematical efficiency against motivation. Surface this when prioritizing payoff, comparing debt types, or explaining why high-interest debt is negative compounding.

nodes.label: Dollar Cost Averaging
nodes.excerpt: Dollar cost averaging invests fixed amounts on a schedule, reducing timing regret and turning investing into a habit, while lump-sum investing often has higher expected return when markets trend upward. Surface this when discussing paycheck investing, deploying cash, market-timing anxiety, or the difference between mathematical expectation and behavioral adherence.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Finance/Emergency Fund — Size and Structure
nodes.label: Emergency Fund — Size and Structure
nodes.excerpt: An emergency fund is liquid, safe cash reserved for income disruption, urgent repairs, medical bills, moves, deductibles, or other shocks, sized by essential expenses and risk exposure rather than gross income. Surface this when discussing financial resilience, avoiding forced debt, or why cash drag can be rational.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Finance/Estate Planning Basics (Will, Beneficiaries, Power of Attorney)
nodes.label: Estate Planning Basics (Will, Beneficiaries, Power of Attorney)
nodes.excerpt: Estate planning defines who can act for you, who receives assets, and how dependents and medical decisions are handled if you die or become incapacitated. Surface this when discussing wills, beneficiary designations, powers of attorney, healthcare directives, trusts, account inventories, or reducing burden on family during emergencies.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Finance/FIRE Movement — Financial Independence, Retire Early
nodes.label: FIRE Movement — Financial Independence, Retire Early
nodes.excerpt: FIRE is the pursuit of financial independence through high savings rates, controlled spending, diversified investing, and withdrawal planning; the strongest lesson is autonomy, not quitting work as an identity. Surface this when discussing savings rate, optionality, burnout, retirement targets, or how spending affects both current surplus and the required portfolio.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Finance/Index Funds vs Active Management — The Data
nodes.label: Index Funds vs Active Management — The Data
nodes.excerpt: Index funds are the hard-to-beat baseline because they deliver broad market exposure at low cost, while active management must overcome fees, taxes, benchmark risk, survivorship bias, and inconsistent skill. Surface this when discussing investing defaults, fund selection, fee drag, or why simple diversified portfolios often beat complex ones.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Finance/Insurance Types You Actually Need
nodes.label: Insurance Types You Actually Need
nodes.excerpt: Insurance is for low-probability, high-severity risks that would break the financial plan, not for every small predictable expense. Surface this when discussing health, auto, renters/homeowners, disability, term life, umbrella liability, deductibles, or why complex insurance-investment hybrids deserve skepticism.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Finance/Negotiating Salary — Scripts and Tactics
nodes.label: Negotiating Salary — Scripts and Tactics
nodes.excerpt: Salary negotiation improves lifetime earnings by combining market data, alternatives, demonstrated impact, timing, scope, and total-compensation framing. Surface this when preparing offers, raises, promotions, recruiter calls, or scripts that are firm without being adversarial.

nodes.label: Net Worth Tracking
nodes.excerpt: Net worth tracking measures assets minus liabilities over time, but the useful interpretation depends on liquidity, allocation, debt quality, cash flow, and life context. Surface this when building a financial dashboard, checking progress, or separating a useful metric from identity and comparison.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Finance/Real Estate vs Stocks — Long-Term Comparison
nodes.label: Real Estate vs Stocks — Long-Term Comparison
nodes.excerpt: Real estate and stocks build wealth through different mechanisms: real estate often combines leverage, local market exposure, cash flow, tax treatment, and operational work, while stocks offer liquid diversified business ownership. Surface this when comparing investment paths, homeownership, rentals, leverage, liquidity, or why raw return charts hide effort and risk.

nodes.label: Sequence of Returns Risk
nodes.excerpt: Sequence of returns risk is the danger that poor returns early in a withdrawal period permanently impair a portfolio even when average returns look acceptable. Surface this when discussing retirement, FIRE, withdrawal rates, cash buffers, flexible spending, or why path matters more once money is being spent down.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Finance/Side Income and Tax Implications
nodes.label: Side Income and Tax Implications
nodes.excerpt: Side income is taxable business activity once it moves beyond casual one-offs, bringing self-employment tax, estimated payments, deductible expenses, bookkeeping, entity, insurance, and retirement-account questions. Surface this when discussing freelancing, indie hacking, creator income, consulting, 1099 work, or why side money needs an operating system.

nodes.label: Tax Loss Harvesting
nodes.excerpt: Tax loss harvesting realizes losses in taxable accounts to offset capital gains and limited ordinary income while maintaining similar market exposure, but wash-sale rules, replacement assets, recordkeeping, future tax rates, and transaction costs matter. Surface this when discussing taxable investing, market downturns, after-tax returns, or why tax deferral is useful but not magic.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Finance/Tax-Advantaged Accounts (401k, Roth IRA, HSA, SEP-IRA)
nodes.label: Tax-Advantaged Accounts (401k, Roth IRA, HSA, SEP-IRA)
nodes.excerpt: Tax-advantaged accounts are wrappers that change when and how savings are taxed, including traditional pre-tax accounts, Roth accounts, HSAs, 401(k)s, IRAs, and self-employed plans. Surface this when explaining retirement account order, employer matches, Roth-vs-traditional tradeoffs, HSA triple tax advantage, or why current limits and eligibility must be verified.

nodes.label: The 4% Rule (Safe Withdrawal Rate)
nodes.excerpt: The 4% rule is a retirement withdrawal heuristic from historical portfolio simulations: withdraw 4% initially, then adjust that dollar amount for inflation, but success depends on time horizon, valuation, asset allocation, fees, taxes, flexibility, and sequence risk. Surface this when doing FIRE math or explaining why a withdrawal rate is not a guarantee.

nodes.label: Annual Review Template
nodes.excerpt: An annual review is the only review with enough temporal distance to see identity patterns, life-direction drift, and multi-year themes that weekly and quarterly reviews miss. A high-quality annual review has four phases: backward-looking narrative (what happened and why), domain scan (health/career/relationships/finances/creative/learning), pattern identification (recurring themes and energy peaks/drains), and forward-looking planning (commitments, experiments, a guiding word or theme for the year). It's only useful if it produces real decisions, not just reflection.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Atomic Habits — Key Ideas (James Clear)
nodes.label: Atomic Habits — Key Ideas (James Clear)
nodes.excerpt: Atomic Habits (James Clear, 2018) argues that tiny consistent behaviors compound dramatically over time and that sustainable behavior change comes from identity, environment design, and systems — not willpower or goal-setting. The Four Laws of Behavior Change (make it obvious, attractive, easy, satisfying) provide a systematic design framework for any habit, applied in reverse to break bad ones.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Building a Second Brain (BASB — Tiago Forte)
nodes.label: Building a Second Brain (BASB — Tiago Forte)
nodes.excerpt: BASB (Tiago Forte, 2022) is a PKM framework centered on CODE — Capture, Organize, Distill, Express — designed to make accumulated knowledge useful for projects and creative output. Its key contribution is the Express step (most PKM systems stop at storage) and Progressive Summarization (multi-layer distillation so notes are fast to re-use). The failure mode is obsessing over Capture and Organize while never Expressing anything.

nodes.label: Capture Systems (Ubiquitous Capture)
nodes.excerpt: Ubiquitous capture (David Allen's GTD principle) means having a fast, friction-free way to externalize thoughts, tasks, ideas, and commitments the moment they arise — so the brain can stop holding them in working memory. The Zeigarnik effect explains why uncaptured open loops persistently interrupt attention; capture resolves this by creating a trusted external store the brain can offload to.

nodes.label: Commonplace Book Tradition
nodes.excerpt: The commonplace book tradition collects quotations, observations, excerpts, and ideas for later reflection. Its modern value is not hoarding references, but giving interesting material a place to be revisited and connected.

nodes.label: Daily Note Template
nodes.excerpt: A daily note template gives each day a lightweight place for captures, priorities, reflections, and session logs. The best template is short enough to use consistently and structured enough to review later.

nodes.label: Decision Journaling
nodes.excerpt: Decision journaling records the reasoning, assumptions, and expected outcomes of important decisions before the outcome is known — making it possible to evaluate decision quality separately from outcome luck. The practice addresses 'resulting' (Annie Duke's term for judging decisions by outcomes rather than by reasoning quality), builds calibration by revealing systematic overconfidence or underconfidence, and creates a corpus of past reasoning that surfaces patterns in how you think.

nodes.label: Deep Work (Cal Newport)
nodes.excerpt: Deep Work (Cal Newport, 2016) argues that cognitively demanding, distraction-free work is becoming simultaneously rarer and more valuable — creating an opportunity for those who cultivate it. Newport's practical system includes four scheduling philosophies (monastic to journalistic), the attention residue problem (why even brief context-switching contaminates focus), the shutdown ritual, and fixed-schedule productivity as a forcing function for eliminating shallow work.

nodes.label: Digital Minimalism
nodes.excerpt: Digital minimalism (Cal Newport, 2019) is a philosophy of technology use built on values rather than defaults: adopt a tool only if it directly supports something you deeply care about, and reject tools that offer marginal benefits at the cost of fragmented attention. The key insight is that the attention economy deliberately designs apps for maximum engagement, not user wellbeing — and that passive consumption crowds out the solitude, boredom, and creative reflection that generate original thinking.

nodes.label: Distraction Management
nodes.excerpt: Distraction management works through environment design rather than willpower — removing triggers before they activate reduces the need for self-control entirely. External distractions (notifications, colleague interruptions, open browser tabs) and internal distractions (boredom, anxiety, checking impulse) require different interventions, but both are better addressed through pre-commitment and system design than in-the-moment resistance.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Energy Management vs Time Management
nodes.label: Energy Management vs Time Management
nodes.excerpt: Energy management (Schwartz & Loehr, The Power of Full Engagement, 2003) frames productivity as managing four energy types — physical, emotional, mental, and purposive — rather than allocating hours. An empty calendar block is only useful if the right type of energy exists to fill it. The key insight is that high performance requires oscillation between stress and recovery: chronic work without recovery produces burnout; recovery without challenge produces atrophy. For most knowledge workers, scheduling by energy type is more impactful than scheduling by time.

nodes.label: Evening Wind-Down Routines
nodes.excerpt: Evening wind-down routines work through two mechanisms: closing open loops (resolving the Zeigarnik effect that causes work rumination during sleep) and aligning with sleep physiology (reducing blue light to allow melatonin release, lowering core body temperature to initiate sleep onset). The evening routine's primary output is sleep quality — which is the direct determinant of tomorrow's cognitive performance, mood, and impulse control.

nodes.label: Evergreen Notes
nodes.excerpt: Evergreen notes (Andy Matuschak) are permanent, atomic, concept-oriented notes designed to remain useful indefinitely — distinct from highlights, book summaries, and meeting notes. The key disciplines: titles as assertable claims, written in your own words, densely linked with explanatory connections, and actively refactored as understanding deepens. The goal is a thinking network that generates new ideas when browsed, not just a knowledge repository that reminds you of old ones.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Flow State — How to Trigger It
nodes.label: Flow State — How to Trigger It
nodes.excerpt: Flow (Csikszentmihalyi, 1990) is a state of complete absorption in a challenging task where time distorts, self-consciousness fades, and action feels effortless. Neurologically, it involves transient hypofrontality (reduced PFC activity) and a cocktail of dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin, anandamide, and endorphins. Flow cannot be forced directly, but four conditions reliably increase its probability: challenge slightly exceeding current skill, clear goals with immediate feedback, distraction-free time blocks, and at least 15–20 minutes of focused entry time.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Focus Modes and Context Switching Costs
nodes.label: Focus Modes and Context Switching Costs
nodes.excerpt: Context switching imposes a hidden cognitive tax: when you move from one type of work to another, attention residue from the previous task lingers for 15–20 minutes and degrades performance on the new one. Focus modes reduce this tax by grouping cognitively compatible work and protecting distinct mental states from contamination. For engineers, the most costly switches are between deep implementation and communication tasks.

nodes.label: Getting Things Done (GTD) Method
nodes.excerpt: GTD (David Allen, 2001) is a complete workflow for managing commitments externally so the mind stays clear. The core loop — Capture, Clarify, Organize, Reflect, Engage — is only trustworthy when the weekly review is done consistently; without it, the system decays. The practical genius is the two-minute rule and the next physical action discipline, which eliminate the decision paralysis that stalls most to-do lists.

nodes.label: Goal Frameworks (OKRs, SMART, 12-Week Year)
nodes.excerpt: OKRs (Grove/Doerr), SMART goals, and the 12-Week Year address the same problem — converting vague ambition into measurable commitments — with different emphases: OKRs are for aspirational alignment across teams with quarterly-to-annual horizons; SMART goals are for well-defined individual execution; the 12-Week Year injects urgency into annual planning by collapsing the year into quarterly sprints. The shared failure mode across all three is too many goals, which dilutes attention and makes everything a priority, which means nothing is.

nodes.label: Habit Stacking
nodes.excerpt: Habit stacking (James Clear, popularizing B.J. Fogg's anchor concept) piggybacks a new behavior onto an existing reliable routine using the formula 'After I [CURRENT HABIT], I will [NEW HABIT].' The mechanism is neurological: existing habits are encoded in the basal ganglia as chunked sequences, and their completion provides a natural trigger for a new behavior without requiring willpower or deliberate decision.

nodes.label: Inbox Zero Strategy
nodes.excerpt: Inbox Zero (Merlin Mann, 2007) is not about having zero emails permanently — it is about using the inbox as a decision queue, not a storage system. The method requires processing each item to a decision (delete/archive/reply/delegate/task) rather than repeatedly re-reading the same unresolved pile, which creates a persistent low-grade anxiety tax even when you're not actively reading email.

nodes.label: Intention Setting
nodes.excerpt: Intention setting is the practice of explicitly deciding how to approach a situation before entering it — not what to produce, but what mode, quality of attention, or behavior to bring. Gollwitzer's implementation intention research shows that specifying when, where, and how you will act more than doubles follow-through versus simply deciding what to do. Intentions bridge the gap between goals (outcomes) and presence (moment-to-moment engagement quality).

nodes.label: Keystone Habits
nodes.excerpt: Keystone habits (Charles Duhigg, The Power of Habit, 2012) are behaviors with spillover effects — when they take hold, they trigger a cascade of other positive changes across identity, energy, schedule, and environment without deliberate effort. Exercise is the most documented example: people who start exercising spontaneously improve diet, sleep, work productivity, and financial behavior even without being asked. The value for habit design is finding the one leverage point that makes everything else easier.

nodes.label: Managing Information Overwhelm
nodes.excerpt: Information overwhelm has two distinct failure modes: too much input (following too many sources) and insufficient filtering (no system for deciding depth of engagement per item). The fix is not faster consumption but a triage hierarchy — ignore, skim, read once, capture and process, note-worthy — and the 'just-in-time vs just-in-case' distinction that reveals most information is consumed speculatively and never used. For knowledge workers in fast-moving technical domains, the leverage is in curation (finding high-signal-to-noise sources) not comprehensiveness.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Morning Routines — Research and Design
nodes.label: Morning Routines — Research and Design
nodes.excerpt: Morning routines work by exploiting the Cortisol Awakening Response (a natural alertness spike in the first 30-45 minutes after waking), protecting the highest-quality cognitive window from reactive inputs before it can be colonized by others' demands, and establishing a consistent first-block transition into meaningful work. The research supports: consistent wake time, morning light exposure, movement, and protecting the first 30-60 minutes from phone/email. It does not support elaborate multi-hour routines that collapse under real-world constraints.

nodes.label: Motivation — Intrinsic vs Extrinsic
nodes.excerpt: Self-Determination Theory (Deci & Ryan, 1985) identifies three core psychological needs that underpin intrinsic motivation: autonomy (acting from free will), competence (growing in skill), and relatedness (mattering to others). The overjustification effect — where adding external rewards to intrinsically motivated activities reduces intrinsic interest — explains why tying financial incentives to creative or intellectual work can backfire, and why the most durable motivation systems internalize external reasons rather than relying on continuous external pressure.

nodes.label: Notification Hygiene
nodes.excerpt: Notifications are asymmetric: they cost milliseconds to send and 15–23 minutes of full attention to recover from. Gloria Mark (UC Irvine) found that knowledge workers are interrupted on average every 11 minutes and take 23 minutes to fully return to a task. Good notification hygiene is not a cosmetic preference — it is the first-line defense against involuntary context switching, and the default settings on every platform are wrong by design.

nodes.label: Obsidian Workflow Design
nodes.excerpt: Obsidian is a local-first markdown note-taking app that treats your vault as a folder of plain text files with bidirectional wikilinks. Effective workflow design has four stages: Capture (low-friction, immediate), Process (move to correct folder, add frontmatter), Connect (meaningful links, VAULT_INDEX update), and Review (daily notes + weekly pass). The failure mode is over-engineering — complex plugin stacks and beautiful graph views that substitute for actual note-making.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/PARA Method (Projects, Areas, Resources, Archives)
nodes.label: PARA Method (Projects, Areas, Resources, Archives)
nodes.excerpt: PARA (Tiago Forte) organizes all information into four categories based on actionability: Projects (active goals with deadlines), Areas (ongoing standards to maintain), Resources (reference material by topic), and Archives (inactive items). The key discipline is maintaining the Projects list as a live, committed list of outcomes — not a wishlist — and moving notes just-in-time rather than filing everything upfront.

nodes.label: Personal Knowledge Management (PKM) Overview
nodes.excerpt: PKM is the deliberate practice of capturing, processing, connecting, and expressing knowledge so it compounds over time rather than staying siloed in individual notes. The core challenge is that most PKM systems optimize for input (capture) and neglect output (expression) — notes accumulate but never produce thinking. The only metric that matters: does the system make you smarter and more productive when you actually need it?

nodes.label: Pomodoro Technique
nodes.excerpt: The Pomodoro Technique (Francesco Cirillo, late 1980s) uses 25-minute focused intervals with 5-minute breaks to solve two distinct problems: the activation energy barrier to starting work, and the attention fatigue that builds from working without structured recovery. It is most effective as an anti-procrastination tool and a time estimation method, but it can interrupt flow if applied rigidly during deep work.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Procrastination — Root Causes and Fixes
nodes.label: Procrastination — Root Causes and Fixes
nodes.excerpt: Procrastination is fundamentally emotion regulation, not time management (Pychyl & Sirois, 2013) — people avoid tasks to escape aversive feelings (anxiety, self-doubt, boredom), and the avoidance reliably provides immediate mood relief. Because the root cause varies (emotional avoidance, task ambiguity, perfectionism, low energy, overwhelm, low interest), effective fixes require diagnosing the actual cause first. Blunt interventions like 'just start' often miss the mechanism.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Reward Systems and Dopamine Loops
nodes.label: Reward Systems and Dopamine Loops
nodes.excerpt: Wolfram Schultz's dopamine prediction error research (1990s) showed that dopamine neurons fire not to reward itself but to cues that predict reward — and fire more strongly when rewards are unexpected. This explains why variable reward schedules (social media, slot machines) are more compelling than fixed ones, why anticipation feels more exciting than the actual reward, and how to design productive habit loops that exploit these mechanisms rather than being exploited by them.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/Task Management Philosophies (GTD, Autofocus, ZTD)
nodes.label: Task Management Philosophies (GTD, Autofocus, ZTD)
nodes.excerpt: Task management systems are opinionated answers to the same problem: how to reliably capture, prioritize, and execute commitments without mental overhead. GTD (Allen) is comprehensive and powerful for complex multi-project knowledge work; Autofocus (Forster) is lightweight and intuition-driven; Zen To Done (Babauta) adapts GTD into sequential habit formation; Personal Kanban (Benson/Barry) uses visual WIP limits. The best system is the one with the lowest overhead you'll actually maintain.

nodes.id: Resources/Personal Productivity/The Map of Content (MOC) in Obsidian
nodes.label: The Map of Content (MOC) in Obsidian
nodes.excerpt: A Map of Content is a curated navigation note that organizes related notes around a topic, project, or area. In Obsidian, MOCs help turn dense link networks into intentional pathways.

nodes.label: Time Blocking
nodes.excerpt: Time blocking assigns every hour of the workday to a specific task or task category on the calendar, so priorities compete against real time instead of an abstract to-do list. The core insight is that tasks without scheduled time are wishful thinking — blocking converts intentions into capacity commitments and makes overcommitment visible before it happens.

nodes.label: Weekly Review Process
nodes.excerpt: The weekly review is GTD's 'critical success factor' — without it, any capture/organize system decays within weeks. The process has three phases: Get Clear (empty inboxes, capture stray thoughts), Get Current (review all lists and calendar), Get Creative (check Someday/Maybe, ask what else matters). The common failure is scope creep making it feel overwhelming; the fix is keeping it scoped to 30–90 minutes with a fixed checklist.

nodes.label: Zettelkasten Method
nodes.excerpt: Zettelkasten ('slip box') is Niklas Luhmann's linked-note system — ~90,000 atomic notes connected by explicit relationships, not folder hierarchies. The key innovations: permanent notes written in your own words, unique IDs enabling non-hierarchical linking, and emergent structure through connections. Modern digital implementations (Obsidian, Roam, Logseq) adapt this with bidirectional links and full-text search; the failure mode in practice is link theater without actual thinking.

nodes.label: AI Ethics — Alignment and Safety
nodes.excerpt: AI alignment and safety ask how increasingly capable systems can remain reliably useful, controllable, honest, robust, and human-compatible under real deployment pressure. The issue is both technical and moral: objectives, oversight, incentives, power, and accountability all interact.

nodes.id: Resources/Philosophy/AI Ethics — Bias, Fairness, and Accountability
nodes.label: AI Ethics — Bias, Fairness, and Accountability
nodes.excerpt: AI fairness is not one metric; it is a governance problem about data, proxies, unequal error costs, feedback loops, institutional power, and who can contest automated decisions. Accountability requires traceable responsibility, not vague 'the model decided' explanations.

nodes.label: Absurdism (Camus)
nodes.excerpt: Absurdism, associated with Albert Camus, begins from the clash between human hunger for meaning and an indifferent universe. Camus rejects both false consolation and nihilistic surrender, arguing for lucid revolt: live, choose, and create without pretending the universe guarantees it.

nodes.label: Effective Altruism — Ideas and Critiques
nodes.excerpt: Effective altruism applies evidence and reason to doing more good with limited resources through cause prioritization, cost-effectiveness, and moral scope expansion. Its strongest critiques target uncertainty, measurement limits, elitism, power concentration, moral demandingness, and governance failures.

nodes.label: Epistemology — How We Know Things
nodes.excerpt: Epistemology studies knowledge, justification, evidence, skepticism, testimony, and uncertainty. The practical question is not 'can we be absolutely certain?' but 'what standards make a belief responsible enough to act on, update, or doubt?

nodes.label: Ethics — Consequentialism
nodes.excerpt: Consequentialism judges actions by outcomes, making it powerful for policy, engineering tradeoffs, public health, and AI risk. Its central tension is whether uncertain aggregate benefit can justify rights violations, unfair distribution, or harmful means.

nodes.label: Ethics — Deontology (Kant's Categorical Imperative)
nodes.excerpt: Deontology judges actions by duties, rights, consent, and respect for persons rather than outcomes alone. Kant's categorical imperative asks whether a maxim could be universal law and whether people are treated as ends, not merely as means.

nodes.label: Ethics — Virtue Ethics (Aristotle)
nodes.excerpt: Virtue ethics asks what kind of person a choice trains you to become. Aristotle frames virtues as habituated excellences guided by practical wisdom, aimed at eudaimonia: a flourishing human life.

nodes.label: Existentialism Basics (Sartre, Camus, Heidegger)
nodes.excerpt: Existentialism focuses on freedom, responsibility, authenticity, anxiety, mortality, absurdity, and meaning without guaranteed external scripts. Sartre, Camus, and Heidegger differ, but all ask what it means to live as a choosing being in an uncertain world.

nodes.label: Falsifiability and the Demarcation Problem
nodes.excerpt: Falsifiability says scientific claims should expose themselves to possible refutation, while the demarcation problem asks how to distinguish science from pseudoscience or non-science. It is a powerful test, but real science also depends on prediction, mechanism, replication, measurement, and research norms.

nodes.id: Resources/Philosophy/Free Will — Compatibilism vs Hard Determinism
nodes.label: Free Will — Compatibilism vs Hard Determinism
nodes.excerpt: Free will debates ask whether agency and responsibility survive determinism, causation, neuroscience, and luck. Compatibilists ground freedom in reasons-responsive action; hard determinists deny ultimate desert while often preserving practical accountability.

nodes.label: Hard Problem of Consciousness (Chalmers)
nodes.excerpt: David Chalmers' hard problem asks why physical information processing is accompanied by subjective experience at all. It differs from the easy problems of explaining behavior, attention, memory, report, and neural mechanisms.

nodes.label: Longtermism
nodes.excerpt: Longtermism argues that future people matter morally and may be vast in number, making the long-run future a major ethical priority. The central tension is whether huge stakes justify decisions under extreme uncertainty, especially when present people bear the costs.

nodes.label: Moral Realism vs Anti-Realism
nodes.excerpt: Moral realism says some moral claims are true independently of our attitudes, while anti-realism denies or reinterprets attitude-independent moral facts. The debate shapes whether ethics is discovered, constructed, expressed, or negotiated.

nodes.id: Resources/Philosophy/Personal Identity Over Time (Ship of Theseus)
nodes.label: Personal Identity Over Time (Ship of Theseus)
nodes.excerpt: Personal identity over time asks what makes someone the same person across bodily change, memory change, psychological development, and narrative revision. The Ship of Theseus and teleportation cases show that identity may be less like a hidden essence and more like continuity for practical purposes.

nodes.label: Philosophy of Language (Wittgenstein, Austin)
nodes.excerpt: Philosophy of language studies meaning, reference, use, and speech acts. Wittgenstein emphasized that meaning depends on language games and forms of life; Austin showed that speech does things, not merely describes things.

nodes.label: Philosophy of Mind — Consciousness
nodes.excerpt: Philosophy of mind asks how mental states, consciousness, intentionality, and subjective experience relate to physical brains and behavior. The field matters for AI because intelligence, reportability, and consciousness are not obviously the same property.

nodes.label: Philosophy of Science (Popper, Kuhn, Lakatos)
nodes.excerpt: Philosophy of science studies how science produces reliable knowledge through evidence, theory, criticism, replication, and community norms. Popper emphasizes falsification, Kuhn emphasizes paradigms and revolutions, and Lakatos tries to distinguish progressive from degenerating research programs.

nodes.id: Resources/Philosophy/Philosophy of Time — A-Theory vs B-Theory
nodes.label: Philosophy of Time — A-Theory vs B-Theory
nodes.excerpt: Philosophy of time asks whether past, present, and future are objectively different or equally real. A-theory treats temporal passage and the present as metaphysically special; B-theory treats time as an ordered relation without an objective moving now.

nodes.label: Pragmatism (Peirce, James, Dewey)
nodes.excerpt: Pragmatism evaluates ideas by their practical consequences in inquiry, experience, and problem-solving. Peirce, James, and Dewey differ, but all resist philosophy that floats free of action, evidence, and revision.

nodes.label: Rationalism vs Empiricism
nodes.excerpt: Rationalism emphasizes reason, structure, deduction, and a priori knowledge; empiricism emphasizes observation, experience, and sensory evidence. Serious inquiry needs both: models without data float, and data without models is blind.

nodes.label: Solipsism and External World Skepticism
nodes.excerpt: Solipsism and external world skepticism ask whether anything beyond one's own experience can be known with certainty. They are useful epistemic stress tests, but everyday life usually rests on fallible realism, intersubjective evidence, and pragmatic action.

nodes.label: Stoicism vs Buddhism — Comparison
nodes.excerpt: Stoicism and Buddhism both train attention, desire, suffering, impermanence, and response to events, but they differ in selfhood, metaphysics, virtue, and liberation. The useful comparison preserves those differences instead of flattening both into generic calmness.

nodes.label: Stoicism — Core Principles
nodes.excerpt: Stoicism teaches that virtue, judgment, and what is within one's control matter more than external fortune. Its modern value is disciplined agency under uncertainty, not emotional suppression or passive acceptance.

nodes.id: Resources/Philosophy/Stoicism — Daily Practices (Negative Visualization, Memento Mori, Amor Fati)
nodes.label: Stoicism — Daily Practices (Negative Visualization, Memento Mori, Amor Fati)
nodes.excerpt: Stoic practices turn philosophy into attention training: negative visualization prepares for loss, memento mori clarifies priorities, amor fati reframes events as material for virtue, and evening review audits judgment without self-hatred.

nodes.label: Taoism Key Concepts (Wu Wei, Yin-Yang)
nodes.excerpt: Taoism emphasizes alignment with the Tao, non-forcing action, interdependent opposites, simplicity, humility, and responsiveness to the situation. Wu wei is not laziness; it is skilled action without unnecessary friction.

nodes.label: The Simulation Hypothesis
nodes.excerpt: The simulation hypothesis argues that experienced reality could be generated by a more fundamental computational substrate. Nick Bostrom's version is a probabilistic argument about advanced civilizations and ancestor simulations, not direct evidence that this world is simulated.

nodes.label: Utilitarianism — Bentham and Mill
nodes.excerpt: Utilitarianism is consequentialism focused on maximizing overall welfare, happiness, preference satisfaction, or reduced suffering. Bentham gives the stark quantitative version; Mill adds qualitative distinctions between higher and lower pleasures.

nodes.label: Zen Buddhism Basics
nodes.excerpt: Zen Buddhism emphasizes direct experience, zazen, impermanence, non-attachment, and seeing through conceptual fixation. Its practical value is not exotic calm; it is training attention to meet the present situation without clinging to every story the mind produces.

nodes.label: Chaos Theory and Complexity
nodes.excerpt: Chaos theory studies deterministic nonlinear systems whose outcomes are highly sensitive to initial conditions, while complexity studies many interacting components that produce emergent behavior. Surface this when explaining why prediction can fail even without randomness, especially in weather, software systems, markets, ecosystems, teams, and habits.

nodes.label: Classical Mechanics Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Classical mechanics explains motion through forces, constraints, energy, momentum, and rotation, and remains the default engineering model when speeds are far below light speed and quantum effects average out. Surface this when Kenny needs physical intuition for systems, simulations, robotics, games, or why conservation laws are more fundamental than memorized formulas.

nodes.label: Dark Matter and Dark Energy
nodes.excerpt: Dark matter and dark energy are operational names for two major cosmological gaps: extra gravitating mass inferred from galaxies/lensing/CMB structure, and accelerated cosmic expansion inferred from supernovae and large-scale cosmology. Surface this when explaining how science can strongly infer something exists while still not knowing what it is.

nodes.label: Electromagnetism Basics (Maxwell's Equations, Intuition)
nodes.excerpt: Electromagnetism unifies electric fields, magnetic fields, charges, currents, circuits, light, radio, motors, and electronics through Maxwell's equations. Surface this when explaining how software ultimately runs on controlled charge motion and how changing fields become waves.

nodes.label: Entropy — Physical and Information Interpretations
nodes.excerpt: Entropy measures how many microscopic configurations fit a macroscopic description in physics, and how much uncertainty a message source has in information theory. Surface this when Kenny needs the bridge between thermodynamics, probability, compression, uncertainty, computation, and the arrow of time.

nodes.label: Fermi Estimation (Orders of Magnitude Thinking)
nodes.excerpt: Fermi estimation breaks unknown quantities into rough factors to get an order-of-magnitude answer and expose the assumptions that dominate uncertainty. Surface this when Kenny needs a fast sanity check for system design, traffic, storage, cost, market size, physics, or everyday quantitative claims.

nodes.id: Resources/Physics/General Relativity — Key Ideas (Spacetime, Gravity as Curvature)
nodes.label: General Relativity — Key Ideas (Spacetime, Gravity as Curvature)
nodes.excerpt: General relativity describes gravity as spacetime curvature caused by mass-energy, with objects following geodesics through curved geometry. Surface this when explaining gravitational time dilation, black holes, gravitational waves, cosmic expansion, GPS corrections, or why gravity is deeper than a force pulling at a distance.

nodes.label: Heisenberg Uncertainty Principle
nodes.excerpt: The Heisenberg uncertainty principle says conjugate variables such as position and momentum cannot both have arbitrarily sharp values, with `Delta x Delta p >= hbar/2`. Surface this when correcting the misconception that quantum uncertainty is just measurement clumsiness.

nodes.label: Information Theory (Shannon Entropy)
nodes.excerpt: Information theory quantifies uncertainty, compression, communication, noise, and channel limits through Shannon entropy and related measures. Surface this when connecting physics, communication systems, machine learning, compression, error correction, and why information is not the same as meaning.

nodes.label: Many-Worlds Interpretation of Quantum Mechanics
nodes.excerpt: Many-Worlds is an interpretation of quantum mechanics where the wavefunction evolves without collapse and measurement corresponds to branching into decohered outcome histories. Surface this when explaining quantum measurement carefully: it is a serious interpretation, not settled fact or generic pop-culture multiverse evidence.

nodes.label: Phase Transitions and Emergence
nodes.excerpt: Phase transitions occur when a system changes macroscopic behavior as a control parameter crosses a threshold, while emergence describes higher-level patterns arising from many lower-level interactions. Surface this when explaining water freezing, magnets ordering, traffic jams, network effects, model behavior, or why system-level properties can be real without being obvious from parts.

nodes.label: Quantum Computing Basics (Qubits, Superposition, Entanglement)
nodes.excerpt: Quantum computing uses qubits, unitary gates, superposition, entanglement, interference, measurement, and error correction to solve some problem classes differently from classical computers. Surface this when correcting hype: quantum computers are not universally faster, but they may be transformative for simulation, cryptography, and specific algorithms.

nodes.label: Quantum Entanglement and Superposition
nodes.excerpt: Superposition lets quantum states combine amplitudes, while entanglement creates joint states whose correlations cannot be reduced to independent parts. Surface this when explaining quantum computing, Bell tests, measurement, and why entanglement is precise physics rather than faster-than-light messaging.

nodes.label: Quantum Mechanics Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Quantum mechanics describes microscopic systems with states, amplitudes, operators, interference, quantized observables, and probabilistic measurement outcomes. Surface this when explaining atoms, chemistry, semiconductors, lasers, quantum computing, or why quantum theory replaces classical certainty with structured probability.

nodes.id: Resources/Physics/Special Relativity — Key Ideas (Time Dilation, Length Contraction)
nodes.label: Special Relativity — Key Ideas (Time Dilation, Length Contraction)
nodes.excerpt: Special relativity shows that space and time measurements depend on inertial frame while the speed of light remains invariant, producing time dilation, length contraction, relativity of simultaneity, and `E = mc^2`. Surface this when explaining high-speed physics, GPS corrections, particle lifetimes, or why relativity is precise frame accounting, not generic subjectivity.

nodes.label: String Theory — Accessible Overview
nodes.excerpt: String theory proposes that fundamental particles are vibrational modes of tiny one-dimensional strings, offering a mathematically rich route toward quantum gravity but lacking direct experimental confirmation. Surface this when discussing the gap between elegance, unification, mathematical productivity, and empirical testability.

nodes.label: The Big Bang Theory
nodes.excerpt: The Big Bang theory describes the universe expanding and cooling from an early hot, dense state, supported by cosmic expansion, the cosmic microwave background, and light-element abundances. Surface this when correcting the misconception that it was an explosion into empty space or a complete theory of ultimate origin.

nodes.id: Resources/Physics/The Standard Model of Particle Physics
nodes.label: The Standard Model of Particle Physics
nodes.excerpt: The Standard Model organizes known elementary particles and the electromagnetic, weak, and strong interactions using quantum fields, gauge symmetries, force carriers, and the Higgs mechanism. Surface this when explaining what matter is made of, why the Higgs mattered, and why a wildly successful theory can still be incomplete.

nodes.id: Resources/Physics/Thermodynamics and the Arrow of Time
nodes.label: Thermodynamics and the Arrow of Time
nodes.excerpt: The arrow of time is the asymmetry between past and future, tied in thermodynamics to entropy increase from low-entropy initial conditions despite many microscopic laws being time-symmetric. Surface this when discussing irreversibility, memory, causality, cosmology, and why time's direction is a statistical and cosmological puzzle.

nodes.label: Thermodynamics — Four Laws
nodes.excerpt: Thermodynamics describes heat, work, energy, temperature, and entropy in systems with many particles, using four laws that constrain what transformations are possible. Surface this when explaining engines, batteries, biology, computation, chemistry, climate, or why every useful process has energy and entropy costs.

nodes.label: Wave-Particle Duality
nodes.excerpt: Wave-particle duality is the failure of classical categories: photons and electrons can interfere like waves while being detected in discrete events. Surface this when explaining the double-slit experiment, probability amplitudes, measurement context, and why quantum objects are not simply small classical particles.

nodes.label: Process Reward Models
nodes.excerpt: step-level rewards vs outcome rewards: credit assignment, PRM800K, Monte Carlo estimation, test-time compute scaling via MCTS.

nodes.label: Product Engineering
nodes.excerpt: product engineering guide covering problem framing, MVPs, activation, retention, engagement, funnels, user research, prioritization, onboarding, analytics, and project-specific product risks.

nodes.label: Activation Metrics
nodes.excerpt: Activation metrics measure whether new users reach a meaningful early value moment that predicts retention, not merely whether they signed up or finished onboarding. Surface this when diagnosing early funnel quality, onboarding, acquisition waste, or first-use product value.

nodes.id: Resources/Product Management/Customer Interviews — How to Run Them
nodes.label: Customer Interviews — How to Run Them
nodes.excerpt: Customer interviews reveal problems, workflows, constraints, alternatives, urgency, and language when they focus on specific past behavior instead of hypothetical opinions. Surface this when validating demand, writing JTBD, or avoiding compliments that do not predict adoption.

nodes.label: Design Sprints (Jake Knapp)
nodes.excerpt: Design sprints compress product learning into a time-boxed process: map the problem, sketch solutions, decide, prototype, and test with users. Surface this when a team is debating a high-risk product direction that can be tested with a realistic prototype before full build.

nodes.label: Feature Flags in Product
nodes.excerpt: Feature flags separate deployment from release, letting product teams expose features gradually, run experiments, gate permissions, use kill switches, and learn safely while managing flag debt. Surface this when discussing rollout strategy, beta cohorts, A/B tests, plan gating, or reversible launches.

nodes.id: Resources/Product Management/Metrics Hierarchy (Input vs Output Metrics)
nodes.label: Metrics Hierarchy (Input vs Output Metrics)
nodes.excerpt: A metrics hierarchy links lagging outcomes to leading inputs and guardrails so teams know what they can influence and what tradeoffs to avoid. Surface this when metrics are vanity counts, teams optimize locally, or nobody can explain how a metric changes decisions.

nodes.id: Resources/Product Management/Net Promoter Score (NPS) — Uses and Limits
nodes.label: Net Promoter Score (NPS) — Uses and Limits
nodes.excerpt: Net Promoter Score asks likelihood to recommend, then subtracts detractors from promoters; it can indicate sentiment trends but is weak as a standalone truth because sampling, timing, culture, response bias, and missing behavioral context distort it. Surface this when NPS is being overused as a product scoreboard.

nodes.id: Resources/Product Management/Prioritization Frameworks (RICE, ICE, MoSCoW, Kano)
nodes.label: Prioritization Frameworks (RICE, ICE, MoSCoW, Kano)
nodes.excerpt: Prioritization frameworks make tradeoffs explicit: RICE uses reach, impact, confidence, and effort; ICE is faster but rougher; MoSCoW separates commitment levels; Kano distinguishes basic, performance, and delight needs. Surface this when a team needs structured debate without pretending scores replace judgment.

nodes.label: Product Analytics Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Product analytics uses event instrumentation, funnels, cohorts, segmentation, and experiments to understand user behavior, but it only works when tied to clear product questions and trustworthy data definitions. Surface this when teams need evidence about activation, retention, conversion, churn, or where users get stuck.

nodes.label: Product Discovery vs Delivery
nodes.excerpt: Product discovery reduces uncertainty about value, usability, feasibility, and viability; delivery turns validated direction into reliable production value. Surface this when a team is either researching forever or shipping blindly without testing assumptions.

nodes.id: Resources/Product Management/Product Roadmaps — Types and Tradeoffs (Outcome vs Feature)
nodes.label: Product Roadmaps — Types and Tradeoffs (Outcome vs Feature)
nodes.excerpt: Product roadmaps communicate product intent, sequencing, and tradeoffs; feature roadmaps promise outputs, while outcome roadmaps organize work around customer and business changes. Surface this when planning under uncertainty, aligning stakeholders, or avoiding backlog-as-roadmap theater.

nodes.label: Product Strategy Framework
nodes.excerpt: Product strategy connects customer segments, problems, differentiation, business model, product bets, and metrics into explicit choices about where to play and how to win. Surface this when a team has many feature ideas but no coherent tradeoff logic.

nodes.label: Retention Analysis
nodes.excerpt: Retention analysis measures whether users continue getting value over time using cohorts, return behavior, churn, and natural usage cadence. Surface this when acquisition looks strong but users do not come back, or when product-market fit needs evidence beyond signups.

nodes.id: Resources/Product Management/The Dual Track Agile Approach
nodes.label: The Dual Track Agile Approach
nodes.excerpt: Dual Track Agile runs discovery and delivery in parallel: one track reduces product risk while the other builds production-quality increments, with shared context between product, design, and engineering. Surface this when teams need continuous learning without stopping delivery or shipping unvalidated work.

nodes.label: Usability Testing
nodes.excerpt: Usability testing observes representative users attempting realistic tasks so teams can find friction, confusion, missing feedback, accessibility barriers, and mismatches between design intent and behavior. Surface this when a design looks clear internally but user task success is unproven.

nodes.id: Resources/Product Management/User Stories and Jobs to Be Done
nodes.label: User Stories and Jobs to Be Done
nodes.excerpt: User stories describe desired behavior from a user's perspective, while Jobs to Be Done captures the progress a customer seeks in a specific situation. Surface this when requirements become ticket templates without context, acceptance criteria, or evidence of the underlying job.

nodes.label: Programming Languages
nodes.excerpt: When to use each programming language and why — covering the core mental model (control vs. convenience, performance predictability), plus deep reasoning for every major language from C to COBOL, organized by domain.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/C — Memory Management and Pointers
nodes.label: C — Memory Management and Pointers
nodes.excerpt: C memory management is entirely manual: stack memory is automatic (freed when function returns), heap memory must be explicitly malloc'd and free'd. Pointers are just integers holding addresses — all power and all danger flows from this. The most dangerous bugs — use-after-free, buffer overflow, double free — are undefined behavior that compilers can't catch and that Rust's ownership system was designed to prevent at compile time.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/Compiled vs Interpreted vs JIT Languages
nodes.label: Compiled vs Interpreted vs JIT Languages
nodes.excerpt: The compiled/interpreted/JIT distinction describes when and how code is translated to machine instructions. AOT-compiled (Rust, Go, C) gives predictable startup and no runtime overhead. Interpreted (CPython, Ruby MRI) gives rapid iteration at the cost of raw speed. JIT (JVM, V8, CLR) gets near-native peak throughput after warmup. Most production runtimes combine all three. The right choice depends on: startup time requirements, peak throughput needs, developer iteration speed, and deployment constraints.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/Concurrency Primitives (Mutex, Semaphore, Condition Variable)
nodes.label: Concurrency Primitives (Mutex, Semaphore, Condition Variable)
nodes.excerpt: Concurrency primitives are the atomic building blocks for thread coordination. Mutex = exclusive access to shared state (one thread at a time). Semaphore = counting permits (N threads at a time). Condition variable = sleep until a predicate becomes true. Understanding them at the implementation level explains why deadlocks happen, what lock granularity means, and why high-level abstractions (channels, actors) exist to avoid the pitfalls.

nodes.label: DSL Design
nodes.excerpt: A DSL (domain-specific language) is a mini-language tailored to one problem domain — SQL for queries, HCL for infrastructure, regex for patterns, RSpec for tests. External DSLs have their own parser; internal DSLs use the host language's syntax (Kotlin builders, Ruby blocks, Python with-statements). The key design tensions: expressiveness vs learnability, flexibility vs safety, and power vs 'this is just an underpowered general-purpose language.' Every DSL becomes a product surface requiring docs, versioning, error messages, and escape hatches.

nodes.label: Functional Programming Concepts
nodes.excerpt: Functional programming treats computation as evaluating functions rather than executing statements. Core ideas: pure functions (no side effects, deterministic), immutable data, first-class functions, higher-order functions, composition, and explicit effect handling. FP concepts are not confined to Haskell — map/filter/reduce, closures, and function composition appear in JavaScript, Python, Rust, and Go daily.
