The practical value is reducing accidental complexity from hidden state mutations.

nodes.label: Garbage Collection Algorithms (Mark-Sweep, Generational)
nodes.excerpt: GC algorithms make different tradeoffs among throughput, pause time, and fragmentation. Mark-sweep is simple but pauses and fragments. Copying collectors eliminate fragmentation via semi-space. Mark-compact combines both benefits at higher cost. Generational GC exploits that most objects die young. Reference counting is immediate but can't collect cycles. Modern production collectors (G1, ZGC, Shenandoah) are generational+concurrent to minimize pauses while managing large heaps.

nodes.label: Go — Goroutines and Channels
nodes.excerpt: Goroutines are lightweight coroutines managed by the Go runtime — start with 'go fn()'. Initial stack is 2–8KB (grows dynamically), so you can run millions concurrently. Channels (make(chan T)) are typed conduits: unbuffered channels synchronize sender+receiver (both block until both are ready); buffered channels allow limited decoupling. 'select' waits on multiple channel operations simultaneously. context.Context propagates cancellation and deadlines through goroutine trees. Goroutine leaks happen when goroutines block forever — always ensure goroutines can exit.

nodes.label: Go — Interface System
nodes.excerpt: Go interfaces are satisfied implicitly — a type implements an interface by having all its methods, with no declaration required. Interface values are fat pointers: (type descriptor, data pointer). The nil interface gotcha: a typed nil stored in an interface variable is not nil (the interface has a non-nil type). Interface composition (embedding) builds larger interfaces from small ones. Consumer-owns-interface principle: define interfaces where they're used, not where the type is defined. io.Reader/io.Writer are the canonical examples of single-method minimal interfaces.

nodes.label: Immutability and Pure Functions
nodes.excerpt: Immutability means values aren't changed after creation — updates produce new values. Pure functions produce the same output for the same input with no side effects. Together they enable referential transparency, safe parallelism, and easy testing. Structural sharing (persistent data structures) makes immutability efficient. The practical tradeoff: code is more predictable but requires unlearning in-place mutation habits. Redux, React state, Rust's default, Clojure's data model, and Elixir all center on immutability.

nodes.label: JIT Compilation
nodes.excerpt: Just-in-time compilation profiles running code, identifies hot paths, and compiles them to optimized native machine code while the program executes. JITs achieve near-AOT performance through adaptive optimization — speculative inlining, escape analysis, type specialization — backed by deoptimization when assumptions break. The JVM's C2 compiler and V8's TurboFan are the canonical production examples.

nodes.label: Java — Garbage Collection
nodes.excerpt: Java GC is generational: most objects die in Eden (minor GC is fast, milliseconds), survivors are promoted to Old Gen where major GC is slower. Four collector choices matter in practice: G1 (balanced, default Java 9+), ZGC (sub-millisecond pauses, Java 15+), Shenandoah (similar to ZGC), and ParallelGC (throughput-first, batch jobs). GC pauses = stop-the-world events where all threads pause — minimizing them is the core JVM tuning goal for latency-sensitive services.

nodes.label: Java — JVM Internals
nodes.excerpt: The JVM is a stack-based virtual machine that compiles Java source to platform-independent bytecode, then executes it with a tiered JIT compiler (interpreter → C1 → C2/Graal) that performs aggressive adaptive optimizations. Understanding JVM internals explains Java's warmup curve, GC pauses, memory areas (heap/metaspace/code cache/stacks), and how to tune JVM flags for production services.

nodes.label: JavaScript — Closures
nodes.excerpt: A closure is a function bundled with its lexical environment — references to variables from outer scopes that remain alive as long as the closure exists. Every JavaScript function is a closure. Key patterns: encapsulation (private state via closure), module pattern (IIFE returning API object), factory functions, memoization, debounce/throttle. Classic bug: var in loops closes over the same variable (all callbacks share the final value); fix with let (block scope) or IIFE. Memory leak: closures inadvertently retaining large DOM nodes or arrays.

nodes.label: JavaScript — Promises and Async-Await
nodes.excerpt: Promises represent a future value — pending → fulfilled (value) or rejected (reason). .then()/.catch() chain reactions as microtasks. async/await is syntactic sugar: async functions always return a Promise, await suspends the function and resumes in a microtask. Key patterns: Promise.all (fail-fast parallel), Promise.allSettled (wait for all), Promise.race (first to settle), Promise.any (first to succeed). Common mistakes: forgetting await (fires but ignores result), sequential awaits when parallel is possible, not catching rejections.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/JavaScript — Prototypes and Prototype Chain
nodes.label: JavaScript — Prototypes and Prototype Chain
nodes.excerpt: JavaScript uses prototype-based inheritance: every object has an internal [[Prototype]] link to another object (or null). Property lookup walks this chain until found or null is reached — O(chain length). Constructor functions set up the chain via .prototype; ES6 class syntax is syntactic sugar over the same prototype mechanism. Object.create(proto) creates an object with a specific prototype. Prototype pollution is a security vulnerability: if untrusted input can set __proto__ or constructor.prototype, it poisons all object instances.

nodes.label: JavaScript — The Event Loop
nodes.excerpt: JavaScript is single-threaded: one call stack, executed to completion before anything else runs. The event loop coordinates three queues in priority order: synchronous code (call stack) → microtasks (Promise reactions, queueMicrotask) → macrotasks (setTimeout, setInterval, I/O callbacks). Microtasks drain completely before the next macrotask begins. Node.js adds phases: timers → pending I/O → idle → poll → check (setImmediate) → close callbacks. Long synchronous work blocks everything — rendering, user input, callbacks — which is why you never sleep() in JS.

nodes.label: Language Design Tradeoffs
nodes.excerpt: Language design is a series of tradeoffs with no universally correct answers: static vs dynamic typing, manual vs GC memory management, checked vs unchecked exceptions, null vs Optional vs Result, expressive vs simple syntax, backward compatibility vs evolution. Understanding what each language chose and why helps you pick the right tool and write idiomatic code. Tony Hoare's 'billion dollar mistake' (null), Java's checked exceptions debate, Python's GIL, and Go's delayed generics are canonical design decision case studies.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/Memory Models and Happens-Before Ordering
nodes.label: Memory Models and Happens-Before Ordering
nodes.excerpt: Memory models define which writes are visible to which reads in concurrent programs. Without a model, compilers and CPUs can reorder operations in ways that break multithreaded code — even if it looks correct to a single-threaded reader. Happens-before is the formal relation: if A happens-before B, B is guaranteed to see A's writes. Locks, volatile/atomic operations, thread joins, and channel sends create happens-before edges. Data races (unsynchronized access to shared memory) are undefined behavior in C++ and Java — don't rely on 'it usually works.

nodes.label: Metaprogramming and Code Generation
nodes.excerpt: Metaprogramming lets code inspect, generate, or transform other code — at compile time (Rust macros, C++ templates, Zig comptime) or runtime (Python metaclasses, Java reflection, JS Proxy). Code generation from schemas (protobuf, OpenAPI, sqlc, Prisma) is the most practical form — a single source of truth generates type-safe code across languages. The risks: invisible behavior, poor stack traces, hard debugging, and build complexity. Use when the repetition is real, the source of truth is stable, and the generated output can be read.

nodes.label: Monads (Accessible Explanation)
nodes.excerpt: A monad is a pattern for composing computations that carry context (failure, optionality, async, state, logging) without manually threading that context through every call. The practical instances you already use: Rust's Option/Result with ? operator, JavaScript Promises with .then(), Haskell's do-notation. The 'bind' operation (flatMap/andThen/>>=/?) is the key — it chains the next computation only when the context allows it, handling the context rules (propagate None, short-circuit on error) automatically.

nodes.label: Python — Async-Await and asyncio
nodes.excerpt: Python asyncio provides cooperative concurrency: one thread, multiple coroutines that voluntarily yield control at `await` points. `async def` defines a coroutine; `await` suspends until an awaitable completes. asyncio.gather() runs multiple coroutines concurrently. asyncio.create_task() schedules a coroutine independently. The critical footgun: any blocking call (time.sleep, requests.get, file I/O) freezes the entire event loop — use asyncio.sleep, aiohttp, aiofiles instead. Best for: high-concurrency I/O servers (thousands of simultaneous connections) where threads would be too heavy.

nodes.label: Python — Context Managers
nodes.excerpt: Context managers implement __enter__ and __exit__ for guaranteed setup and teardown — even if exceptions occur. The 'with' statement calls __enter__ at entry and __exit__ at exit with exception info (return True to suppress). @contextmanager lets you write context managers as generator functions with a single yield. contextlib.ExitStack manages multiple dynamic context managers. Real uses: file handles, database transactions, locks, temporary directory/env, mocking. async with uses __aenter__/__aexit__ for async resources.

nodes.label: Python — Decorators Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: A decorator is callable syntax sugar: @dec above 'def f' is equivalent to 'f = dec(f)'. Decorators receive a function (or class) and return a replacement. functools.wraps is mandatory when wrapping — it copies __name__, __doc__, __module__ so debugging, introspection, and frameworks (FastAPI, pytest) work correctly. Decorator factories take arguments and return decorators. Stacked decorators apply bottom-up at definition time. Common built-ins: @property (computed attributes), @staticmethod, @classmethod, @lru_cache, @dataclass.

nodes.label: Python — GIL and Concurrency
nodes.excerpt: CPython's Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) is a mutex that prevents multiple threads from executing Python bytecode simultaneously. This means CPU-bound multi-threaded Python does NOT parallelize — two threads on a 4-core machine use ~1 core. The GIL is released during I/O and by C extensions that explicitly release it (NumPy, OpenSSL). Fix CPU-bound work with multiprocessing (separate processes, no GIL) or NumPy/Cython/C extensions. Python 3.13 introduces free-threaded CPython (PEP 703) — GIL optional, but extension compatibility is still maturing.

nodes.label: Python — Generators and Iterators
nodes.excerpt: Python's iterator protocol: __iter__() returns self, __next__() returns the next value or raises StopIteration. Generator functions use 'yield' to produce values lazily — the function's frame is paused and resumed on each next() call, retaining all local state. 'yield from' delegates to a sub-generator. Generators are single-pass, memory-efficient: processing a 10GB log file with a generator uses O(1) memory. send() enables bidirectional communication (coroutine-like). Async generators combine 'async def' + 'yield' for async iteration.

nodes.label: Python — Internals and CPython
nodes.excerpt: CPython compiles Python source to bytecode, then executes it in a stack-based virtual machine (ceval.c eval loop). Every Python value is a heap-allocated PyObject with ob_refcnt (reference count) and ob_type (pointer to type). The LEGB rule resolves names: Local → Enclosing → Global → Builtin. CPython interns small integers (-5 to 256) and short strings, so 'x is y' can be True unexpectedly. dis.dis() inspects bytecode. PyPy uses JIT compilation — pure Python loops run 10–100x faster than CPython.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/Python — Memory Management and gc
nodes.label: Python — Memory Management and gc
nodes.excerpt: CPython memory management: reference counting frees objects immediately when ob_refcnt hits 0. Reference cycles (A→B→A) can't be freed by refcount alone — the cyclic GC handles them using 3 generations with collection thresholds (700/10/10). Memory arenas are 256KB; freed objects return to Python's pool, not the OS (MemFree may not drop after del). Small integers (-5 to 256) and interned strings are never freed. tracemalloc tracks which line allocated which memory. Use weakref to reference without keeping alive.

nodes.label: Python — Metaclasses
nodes.excerpt: In Python, classes are objects created by metaclasses. 'type' is the default metaclass: type('Foo', (Base,), {'method': fn}) creates a class. Custom metaclasses intercept class creation via __new__ and __init__, allowing attribute injection, validation, registration, and DSL behavior. Django ORM's Model, abc.ABCMeta, and SQLAlchemy all use metaclasses. Modern Python prefers __init_subclass__ and class decorators for most metaclass use cases — metaclasses are the last resort when those don't suffice.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/Python — Type System and mypy
nodes.label: Python — Type System and mypy
nodes.excerpt: Python type hints (PEP 484, 3.5+) add optional static annotations — not enforced at runtime but checked by mypy, pyright, or IDEs. Core types: int/str/list[int]/dict[str, int]/tuple[int, ...]/set[str]. Optional[T] = T | None. Union[A, B] = A | B. TypeVar for generics. Protocol for structural typing (duck-typing formalized). Literal for specific values. TypedDict for typed dicts. Run mypy --strict for maximum checking. TYPE_CHECKING guard imports type-only dependencies without runtime cost.

nodes.label: Rust — Lifetimes
nodes.excerpt: Rust lifetimes are compile-time annotations that describe how long references must remain valid. They prevent dangling references by letting the borrow checker verify that no reference outlives the data it points to. Most lifetimes are inferred through elision rules — explicit annotations are only needed when the compiler can't determine the relationship between input and output references.

nodes.label: Rust — Ownership and Borrowing
nodes.excerpt: Rust's ownership system enforces memory safety at compile time with zero runtime cost: every value has exactly one owner, values are dropped when their owner leaves scope, and references must obey borrow checker rules (one mutable OR many immutable, never both). This eliminates use-after-free, double-free, and data races before the program runs.

nodes.label: Rust — Traits and Generics
nodes.excerpt: Rust traits are like interfaces that types opt into, enabling shared behavior with zero-cost static dispatch via generics (monomorphization) or dynamic dispatch via trait objects (dyn Trait). The difference matters: generics produce specialized machine code per type (fast, no allocation), dyn Trait uses a vtable at runtime (flexible, heap-allocated, one binary). Standard traits like Iterator, Display, From/Into, and Clone are the vocabulary of idiomatic Rust.

nodes.label: TypeScript — Generics
nodes.excerpt: TypeScript generics parameterize types so one definition works across many types without losing type information. Generic functions infer T from arguments (no explicit annotation needed at call site). Generic constraints (T extends U) restrict what types are allowed. The 'infer' keyword extracts part of a matched type inside conditional types. Common patterns: identity function, typed API response wrapper (ApiResponse<T>), repository pattern (Repository<T>), and extracting function parameter/return types (Parameters<F>, ReturnType<F>).

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/TypeScript — Type System Deep Dive
nodes.label: TypeScript — Type System Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: TypeScript's type system is structural (duck-typed: compatible if shapes match, not if explicitly declared), erased at runtime (no runtime type checking), and Turing-complete at the type level. Key features: union types (A|B), intersection types (A&B), type narrowing via typeof/instanceof/in/discriminated union, keyof/typeof operators, conditional types (T extends U ? A : B), mapped types ({[K in keyof T]: ...}), template literal types. 'any' disables checking; 'unknown' forces you to narrow first. 'never' is the empty type — used for exhaustive checks.

nodes.id: Resources/Programming Languages/TypeScript — Utility Types (Partial, Pick, Omit, etc.)
nodes.label: TypeScript — Utility Types (Partial, Pick, Omit, etc.)
nodes.excerpt: TypeScript's built-in utility types transform existing types into related shapes. Most important: Partial<T> (all optional), Required<T> (all required), Readonly<T> (immutable), Pick<T,K> (select fields), Omit<T,K> (remove fields), Record<K,V> (object map), Exclude<T,U> (filter union), Extract<T,U> (keep union members), NonNullable<T> (remove null/undefined), ReturnType<F>, Parameters<F>, Awaited<T>. They're built from mapped types and conditional types — understanding the implementation demystifies TypeScript's type-level programming.

nodes.label: WebAssembly (WASM) Basics
nodes.excerpt: WebAssembly is a binary instruction format that runs in a sandboxed VM at near-native speed — in browsers, servers, and edge runtimes. Rust, C, and C++ compile to WASM via wasm-pack/Emscripten. The JS↔WASM boundary is expensive (marshaling), so WASM wins when computation dominates crossing. Real-world deployments: Figma's rendering engine, Google Earth Web, AutoCAD Web, FFmpeg in browsers. WASI extends WASM beyond browsers with portable system call interfaces.

nodes.label: RAG and Bobby Vector Search
nodes.excerpt: what RAG is, how Bobby's current index-based retrieval works, and when to upgrade to vector search (~300 notes trigger).

nodes.label: RLHF and Alignment
nodes.excerpt: aligning LLMs: SFT, reward modeling, PPO, DPO (with math), Constitutional AI, steering vectors, reward hacking cases, alignment tax.

nodes.label: Rate Limiting
nodes.excerpt: five algorithms (token bucket, leaky bucket, fixed window, sliding window log, sliding window counter), distributed enforcement with Redis, where to enforce, rate limit headers.

nodes.label: VanossGaming - Dewit
nodes.excerpt: tracks the VanossGaming crew reference where Tyler says \"Dewit\" at 0:53, meaning \"Do it.\".

nodes.id: Resources/References/VanossGaming - Oh I Have a Boner
nodes.label: VanossGaming - Oh I Have a Boner
nodes.excerpt: tracks the VanossGaming crew reference where Nogla slurs \"bow and arrow\" into \"boner\" at 3:13 while Evan trolls him invisibly.

nodes.label: Scaling
nodes.excerpt: vertical vs. horizontal scaling, sharding strategies (range/hash/consistent hashing/directory), cross-shard queries, replication, and tail latency mitigation (hedged requests, LIFO queues, load shedding).

nodes.label: Search Systems
nodes.excerpt: inverted index, text processing pipeline, TF-IDF vs BM25, Elasticsearch internals (shards, segments, near-real-time write path), autocomplete (trie, edge n-grams, fuzzy), vector search (HNSW), hybrid search with RRF.

nodes.label: Security
nodes.excerpt: threat modeling, authn vs authz, session auth, JWT (alg:none attack, refresh rotation), bcrypt/Argon2, OWASP Top 10, XSS/CSRF/SSRF/race conditions, secrets management, security headers.

nodes.label: API Key Management
nodes.excerpt: API keys are bearer credentials: possession usually equals access. Good API key management designs for leak containment with scoped keys, environment separation, hashed storage, short rotation paths, usage monitoring, rate limits, ownership metadata, and automated revocation.

nodes.label: Authentication vs Authorization
nodes.excerpt: Authentication proves an actor's identity; authorization decides whether that actor may perform a specific action on a specific resource. Many serious breaches are authorization failures: the user is real, but the system forgets to check ownership, scope, role, or policy at the server boundary.

nodes.label: CSRF — Prevention
nodes.excerpt: Cross-Site Request Forgery tricks a browser into sending an authenticated state-changing request that the user did not intend. Defenses include SameSite cookies, CSRF tokens, Origin/Referer validation, safe HTTP method semantics, and recognizing that XSS can bypass many CSRF controls.

nodes.label: Clickjacking Prevention
nodes.excerpt: Clickjacking tricks a user into interacting with a legitimate page through a deceptive frame or overlay. The primary defense is browser-enforced framing control with CSP frame-ancestors and, for legacy support, X-Frame-Options; sensitive actions should also use clear confirmation and re-authentication where risk justifies it.

nodes.label: Container Security
nodes.excerpt: Container security covers images, Dockerfiles, registries, runtimes, Linux capabilities, secrets, Kubernetes policies, and host boundaries. The core insight is that containers share the host kernel, so least privilege and supply-chain controls matter more than the word 'container.

nodes.label: Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) — Prevention
nodes.excerpt: Cross-site scripting occurs when attacker-controlled content executes as JavaScript in a trusted origin. Prevention depends on contextual output encoding, safe framework defaults, avoiding dangerous DOM APIs, sanitizing allowed rich text, HttpOnly cookies, and a restrictive Content Security Policy as defense in depth.

nodes.label: Data Privacy Regulations (GDPR, CCPA)
nodes.excerpt: GDPR and CCPA/CPRA turn privacy promises into engineering requirements: data inventory, lawful basis or notice, access/export/delete/correct flows, opt-outs, retention limits, vendor controls, and breach response. Treat this as engineering context, not legal advice.

nodes.label: Defense in Depth
nodes.excerpt: Defense in depth layers independent preventive, detective, and responsive controls so one failure does not become total compromise. The useful test is whether each layer breaks a different part of the attack chain rather than duplicating the same brittle assumption.

nodes.label: Encryption — Symmetric vs Asymmetric
nodes.excerpt: Symmetric encryption uses one shared secret key and is fast enough for bulk data; asymmetric cryptography uses public/private key pairs for identity, signatures, and key agreement. Real protocols usually combine them: asymmetric trust setup, symmetric session keys for data.

nodes.label: End-to-End Encryption
nodes.excerpt: End-to-end encryption encrypts content on the sender's device and decrypts it only on intended recipient devices, so servers can route and store ciphertext without plaintext access. It improves privacy but complicates search, moderation, recovery, backups, multi-device sync, and abuse handling.

nodes.id: Resources/Security & Privacy/JWT — Structure, Signing, and Security
nodes.label: JWT — Structure, Signing, and Security
nodes.excerpt: JWTs are compact signed claim containers with header, payload, and signature sections. They are useful for portable identity and authorization metadata, but only if callers verify signature, issuer, audience, algorithm, expiration, key ID, and token purpose every time.

nodes.label: OAuth 2.0 Flow
nodes.excerpt: OAuth 2.0 is a delegated authorization framework: it lets a client obtain scoped access tokens for a resource server without handling the user's password. The modern default for user-facing apps is Authorization Code with PKCE; login identity is OpenID Connect, not OAuth alone.

nodes.label: OWASP Top 10
nodes.excerpt: The OWASP Top 10 is a developer-facing awareness standard for major web application security risk categories; OWASP lists the 2025 release as the current version. Use it as a shared vocabulary and review checklist, not as proof that an application is secure.

nodes.label: OpenID Connect (OIDC)
nodes.excerpt: OpenID Connect adds a standardized authentication layer to OAuth 2.0. It issues ID tokens with identity claims, discovery metadata, and JWKS-based signing keys so applications can implement login without inventing their own identity protocol.

nodes.label: PII Handling Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: PII handling is the engineering discipline of minimizing, classifying, protecting, auditing, and deleting data that identifies or can be linked to people. The biggest leaks often come from secondary copies: logs, analytics, screenshots, exports, warehouses, support tools, and backups.

nodes.id: Resources/Security & Privacy/Passkeys and Hardware Security Keys (WebAuthn)
nodes.label: Passkeys and Hardware Security Keys (WebAuthn)
nodes.excerpt: Passkeys and hardware security keys use WebAuthn/FIDO public-key authentication so the server stores a public key and the authenticator signs origin-bound challenges. The security win is phishing resistance: a fake domain cannot use the credential registered for the real relying party.

nodes.label: Password Hashing (bcrypt, Argon2, scrypt)
nodes.excerpt: Password hashing protects stored passwords after database compromise by using salted, deliberately expensive, one-way functions. Argon2id, bcrypt, and scrypt slow offline guessing; they must be paired with rate limiting, MFA, breach checks, reset hygiene, and parameter upgrades over time.

nodes.label: Penetration Testing Overview
nodes.excerpt: Penetration testing is an authorized adversarial assessment that validates whether weaknesses can be chained into business impact. Good tests have clear scope, rules of engagement, safety limits, evidence, severity rationale, remediation guidance, and retesting.

nodes.label: Phishing — Recognition and Defense
nodes.excerpt: Phishing tricks users into handing over credentials, sessions, approvals, money, or malware execution. Modern defense assumes some lures get through, so it combines phishing-resistant MFA, email authentication, safe reporting, browser/session controls, account recovery hardening, and anomaly detection.

nodes.label: Public Key Infrastructure (PKI)
nodes.excerpt: Public Key Infrastructure binds public keys to identities through certificates, certificate authorities, trust stores, issuance, validation, renewal, and revocation. PKI is why browsers can decide that a TLS public key belongs to a domain rather than an impostor.

nodes.label: SQL Injection — Prevention
nodes.excerpt: SQL injection happens when untrusted input is interpreted as SQL syntax instead of data. The primary defense is parameterized queries or safe query builders, backed by allowlisted dynamic identifiers, least-privilege database roles, safe errors, and tests around high-risk query paths.

nodes.label: Secrets Scanning (GitGuardian, truffleHog)
nodes.excerpt: Secrets scanning detects API keys, tokens, private keys, and credentials in code, commits, logs, containers, tickets, and artifacts. The key point is response discipline: detection only matters if leaked credentials are revoked, rotated, scoped, and traced for use.

nodes.label: Security Auditing
nodes.excerpt: Security auditing checks whether controls exist, operate, and leave evidence. A good audit is scoped, evidence-based, risk-oriented, and tied to remediation; it is different from a pen test because it evaluates control posture, not just exploitability.

nodes.label: Security Headers (CSP, HSTS, X-Frame-Options)
nodes.excerpt: Security headers let servers ask browsers to enforce defenses around scripts, HTTPS, framing, MIME sniffing, referrers, and powerful APIs. They are cheap defense in depth, but CSP and HSTS in particular need deliberate rollout and testing to avoid breaking the app or locking in bad config.

nodes.label: Session Management
nodes.excerpt: Session management keeps an authenticated user connected across stateless HTTP requests while limiting theft, fixation, replay, and excessive lifetime. Secure sessions require high-entropy identifiers, safe cookie flags, rotation on privilege changes, expiration, invalidation, CSRF defenses, and server-side authorization.

nodes.label: Social Engineering Attacks
nodes.excerpt: Social engineering attacks manipulate people and workflows instead of directly exploiting code. They succeed by abusing authority, urgency, helpfulness, routine, and weak verification paths such as help desks, account recovery, vendor payments, and executive approvals.

nodes.label: Supply Chain Security
nodes.excerpt: Supply chain security protects dependencies, build systems, CI credentials, artifacts, registries, vendors, and update channels. The core idea is provenance: production should only run artifacts whose source, build path, signatures, and dependencies are known and trusted.

nodes.label: Threat Modeling (STRIDE)
nodes.excerpt: Threat modeling is structured security design review before an incident or pen test finds the issue. STRIDE walks each asset, actor, trust boundary, and data flow through spoofing, tampering, repudiation, information disclosure, denial of service, and elevation of privilege.

nodes.id: Resources/Security & Privacy/Vulnerability Disclosure and Bug Bounty Programs
nodes.label: Vulnerability Disclosure and Bug Bounty Programs
nodes.excerpt: Explains how vulnerability disclosure and bug bounty programs turn external security reports into reproducible fixes, coordinated public communication, and researcher trust. The key mechanism is triage discipline: scope, safe harbor, severity, ownership, remediation SLA, and disclosure timing.

nodes.label: Zero-Day Vulnerabilities
nodes.excerpt: Zero-day vulnerabilities are flaws with no available vendor patch at the moment defenders need to respond. The practical response is exposure discovery, compensating controls, rapid patch channels, and behavior-based detection rather than waiting for perfect certainty.

nodes.label: Zero-Trust Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Zero-trust architecture removes implicit trust from network location. Every user, device, service, and workload request should be strongly authenticated, explicitly authorized, encrypted, logged, and limited by context-aware policy.

nodes.label: Security Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: deeper security guide covering OAuth/OIDC, sessions vs JWTs, passkeys, XSS/CSRF/SSRF, SQL injection, supply chain, container/Kubernetes security, secrets, threat modeling, and incident response.

nodes.label: Alcohol and Sleep Quality
nodes.excerpt: Alcohol can shorten sleep onset because it is sedating, but it usually worsens sleep architecture by fragmenting the second half of the night, suppressing REM early, increasing awakenings, and worsening snoring or sleep apnea. It is one of the most common hidden causes of 'I fell asleep fine but slept badly.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Blue Light and Melatonin Suppression
nodes.label: Blue Light and Melatonin Suppression
nodes.excerpt: Evening light, especially bright blue-enriched light, can suppress melatonin and delay circadian timing through melanopsin-sensitive retinal cells. Brightness, timing, duration, distance, and content stimulation matter more than treating every pixel as equally dangerous.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Chronotypes (Early Bird vs Night Owl — Genetics)
nodes.label: Chronotypes (Early Bird vs Night Owl — Genetics)
nodes.excerpt: Chronotype is the biological tendency toward earlier or later sleep-wake timing, shaped by genetics, age, light exposure, and obligations. It should be respected when designing routines, but it is not a fixed excuse to ignore light timing, wake consistency, and social constraints.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Circadian Rhythm — How It Works
nodes.label: Circadian Rhythm — How It Works
nodes.excerpt: Circadian rhythm is the body's roughly 24-hour timing system, coordinated by the suprachiasmatic nucleus and reset mainly by light. Morning light, consistent wake time, dim evenings, meal timing, exercise, and social rhythm all help Bobby reason about why sleep timing succeeds or drifts.

nodes.label: Jet Lag Management
nodes.excerpt: Jet lag is circadian misalignment after rapid travel across time zones, not just travel fatigue. Light timing is the strongest lever, with destination schedule, meal timing, caffeine, naps, and sometimes carefully timed melatonin used to shift the clock without worsening sleep.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Melatonin Supplementation — When and How Much
nodes.label: Melatonin Supplementation — When and How Much
nodes.excerpt: Melatonin is mainly a circadian timing signal, not a knockout sedative. It is most useful for jet lag or delayed sleep timing when dose and timing are chosen carefully; routine high-dose nightly use is a poor substitute for light timing, sleep hygiene, or medical evaluation.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Napping — Types and Optimal Duration (Power Nap, Full Cycle)
nodes.label: Napping — Types and Optimal Duration (Power Nap, Full Cycle)
nodes.excerpt: Napping reduces sleep pressure and can improve alertness, mood, and performance, but duration and timing determine whether it helps or causes sleep inertia. Short 10-30 minute naps are usually safest; longer full-cycle naps are recovery tools, not daily compensation for chronic short sleep.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Noise and Sleep (White, Pink, Brown Noise)
nodes.label: Noise and Sleep (White, Pink, Brown Noise)
nodes.excerpt: Noise hurts sleep mainly when it is sudden, meaningful, or variable enough to trigger micro-awakenings. White, pink, brown noise, fans, earplugs, and room changes can help by masking unpredictability, but volume should stay low enough to avoid becoming a new stressor.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Pre-Sleep Nutrition and Sleep Quality
nodes.label: Pre-Sleep Nutrition and Sleep Quality
nodes.excerpt: Pre-sleep nutrition affects sleep through digestion, reflux, blood sugar, body temperature, alcohol, caffeine, hydration, and hunger. The practical rule is to avoid heavy late meals and alcohol as sleep aids while using small protein/carb snacks only when hunger is the actual sleep disruptor.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Sleep Cycles and Ultradian Rhythms
nodes.label: Sleep Cycles and Ultradian Rhythms
nodes.excerpt: Sleep cycles are ultradian rhythms that usually repeat about every 80-120 minutes, shifting from NREM-heavy early cycles to REM-heavy later cycles. The useful lesson is not exact 90-minute bedtime math; it is protecting enough sleep opportunity for multiple complete cycles.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Sleep Deprivation — Cognitive and Physical Effects
nodes.label: Sleep Deprivation — Cognitive and Physical Effects
nodes.excerpt: Sleep deprivation impairs attention, reaction time, learning, emotion regulation, immune function, metabolic health, cardiovascular risk, and physical recovery. Chronic restriction is deceptive because people may feel adapted while objective performance remains degraded.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Sleep Disorders Overview (Apnea, Insomnia, RLS)
nodes.label: Sleep Disorders Overview (Apnea, Insomnia, RLS)
nodes.excerpt: Sleep apnea, chronic insomnia, and restless legs syndrome are not solved by generic sleep hygiene when symptoms are persistent or impairing. Bobby should recognize red flags like choking awakenings, loud snoring, severe daytime sleepiness, weeks of insomnia, restless leg urges, and drowsy driving as reasons for medical evaluation.

nodes.label: Sleep Hygiene Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: Sleep hygiene is the baseline system for reliable sleep: consistent wake time, morning light, enough sleep opportunity, late-caffeine cutoff, dim evenings, cool dark quiet room, and a repeatable wind-down. It helps many people, but persistent insomnia, apnea symptoms, restless legs, or severe daytime sleepiness need medical evaluation rather than more habit tweaks.

nodes.label: Sleep Pressure and Adenosine
nodes.excerpt: Sleep pressure is the homeostatic drive that builds with time awake, partly through adenosine signaling, and falls during sleep. Caffeine blocks adenosine receptors, so it can hide sleepiness without repaying sleep debt.

nodes.label: Sleep Stages (NREM 1-2-3 and REM)
nodes.excerpt: Sleep is organized into NREM 1, NREM 2, NREM 3, and REM, each with different brain-wave patterns and recovery functions. Deep NREM is concentrated earlier in the night, REM increases later, and consumer wearables can estimate stages but cannot read them like a sleep lab.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Sleep Tracking — Metrics That Actually Matter
nodes.label: Sleep Tracking — Metrics That Actually Matter
nodes.excerpt: Sleep tracking is useful for trends in sleep duration, consistency, awakenings, resting heart rate, HRV, SpO2 flags, and subjective recovery. Consumer stage estimates are much weaker than polysomnography, so the data should guide experiments rather than become a nightly grade.

nodes.id: Resources/Sleep Science/Social Jetlag — The Weekend Sleep Shift
nodes.label: Social Jetlag — The Weekend Sleep Shift
nodes.excerpt: Social jetlag is the gap between biological sleep timing and required social timing, often created by late weekends plus early weekday alarms. It can make Monday feel like a time-zone shift, so the practical fix is smaller wake-time swings, morning light, and strategic catch-up sleep without pushing the clock later.

nodes.label: Temperature and Sleep Quality
nodes.excerpt: Sleep depends on thermoregulation: core temperature tends to fall before sleep, and overheating commonly fragments the night. The practical target is a cool, breathable sleep environment, usually around the mid-60s Fahrenheit for many adults, adjusted for comfort, age, bedding, and medical context.

nodes.id: Resources/Society, Culture & History/Climate Change — Science and Policy
nodes.label: Climate Change — Science and Policy
nodes.excerpt: Climate change is an energy-balance problem caused mainly by human greenhouse gas emissions, then made politically hard by global coordination, fossil-fuel dependence, delayed harms, unequal vulnerability, and development tradeoffs. Surface this when Kenny needs the mechanism, policy levers, and why climate debates mix science, economics, justice, and technology.

nodes.id: Resources/Society, Culture & History/Colonialism and Its Long-Term Effects
nodes.label: Colonialism and Its Long-Term Effects
nodes.excerpt: Colonialism was a system of political domination, extraction, settlement, racial hierarchy, and institutional restructuring whose effects persist in borders, wealth gaps, land ownership, state capacity, language, migration, conflict, and global power. Surface this when a present-day inequality or geopolitical dispute has deeper institutional roots.

nodes.label: Demographic Transition Theory
nodes.excerpt: Demographic transition theory explains how societies often move from high birth/high death rates to low birth/low death rates as mortality falls, urbanization changes family economics, women gain education, contraception spreads, and children become costlier but more likely to survive. Surface this for aging, migration, pensions, development, housing, and labor-market conversations.

nodes.id: Resources/Society, Culture & History/Social Media and Political Polarization
nodes.label: Social Media and Political Polarization
nodes.excerpt: Social media can amplify political polarization through identity performance, engagement ranking, outrage incentives, selective exposure, viral decontextualization, and low-cost mobilization. Surface this when Kenny needs a nuanced explanation that treats platforms as accelerants inside broader political, media, economic, and institutional conditions.

nodes.label: Surveillance Capitalism (Shoshana Zuboff)
nodes.excerpt: Surveillance capitalism is Shoshana Zuboff's term for platform business models that extract behavioral data, infer future behavior, and monetize prediction or influence through advertising, recommendations, and personalization. Surface this when discussing privacy, platform power, consent, AI data extraction, or why free digital services often have hidden economic costs.

nodes.label: The Attention Economy
nodes.excerpt: The attention economy treats human attention as the scarce resource competed for by platforms, advertisers, creators, apps, and media. Surface this when explaining why modern digital products optimize for capture, retention, novelty, outrage, and social validation even when those incentives conflict with learning, judgment, well-being, and focus.

nodes.id: Resources/Society, Culture & History/The Civil Rights Movement
nodes.label: The Civil Rights Movement
nodes.excerpt: The US Civil Rights Movement challenged Jim Crow through litigation, direct action, local organizing, church and student networks, media strategy, federal pressure, and mass mobilization. Surface this when discussing social change, protest strategy, democracy, racial hierarchy, law, and why legal victories can coexist with continuing structural inequality.

nodes.id: Resources/Society, Culture & History/The Cold War — Technology and Ideology
nodes.label: The Cold War — Technology and Ideology
nodes.excerpt: The Cold War was a US-Soviet global rivalry fought through ideology, alliances, nuclear deterrence, proxy wars, espionage, development aid, propaganda, and technology races. Surface this when explaining why computing, aerospace, satellites, nuclear strategy, the internet's precursors, and modern geopolitics were shaped by military and ideological competition.

nodes.label: The Enlightenment
nodes.excerpt: The Enlightenment extended scientific reason into politics, religion, economics, and society, elevating rights, public argument, skepticism of inherited authority, and institutional reform. Surface this when discussing democracy, liberalism, science, secularism, human rights, and the tension between universal ideals and exclusionary practice.

nodes.id: Resources/Society, Culture & History/The Future of Work (Automation, Remote, AI)
nodes.label: The Future of Work (Automation, Remote, AI)
nodes.excerpt: The future of work is shaped by automation, AI, remote collaboration, demographics, platform labor, credential shifts, and global competition. Surface this when Kenny is thinking about career strategy: the key question is not whether work disappears, but which tasks get cheaper, which skills become more leveraged, and who captures the productivity gains.

nodes.id: Resources/Society, Culture & History/The Industrial Revolution — Causes and Effects
nodes.label: The Industrial Revolution — Causes and Effects
nodes.excerpt: The Industrial Revolution transformed production through coal, steam, mechanization, factories, wage labor, transport networks, capital accumulation, and global trade. Surface this when explaining modern productivity, capitalism, urbanization, labor conflict, fossil-energy dependence, climate change, and why automation debates are not new.

nodes.id: Resources/Society, Culture & History/The Information Age — Social Impacts
nodes.label: The Information Age — Social Impacts
nodes.excerpt: The Information Age reorganized society around computers, networks, software, data, mobile devices, platforms, and instant communication. Surface this when explaining why knowledge access improved while attention, privacy, misinformation, labor, identity, inequality, and platform power became structural problems.

nodes.id: Resources/Society, Culture & History/The Rise of China — Economic Miracle
nodes.label: The Rise of China — Economic Miracle
nodes.excerpt: China's post-1978 economic rise combined market incentives, state direction, special economic zones, export manufacturing, infrastructure, education, urbanization, foreign investment, and global trade integration. Surface this when discussing development, globalization, manufacturing, state capacity, technology competition, and the tradeoffs of growth under authoritarian governance.

nodes.label: The Scientific Revolution
nodes.excerpt: The Scientific Revolution shifted European knowledge from inherited authority toward observation, mathematics, experiment, instruments, and public verification. Surface this when explaining science as a method for making claims accountable, not merely a list of discoveries.

nodes.id: Resources/Society, Culture & History/Urbanization and Its Effects
nodes.label: Urbanization and Its Effects
nodes.excerpt: Urbanization concentrates people, work, infrastructure, capital, culture, and political power in cities, creating productivity through density while stressing housing, transport, sanitation, inequality, public health, environment, and governance. Surface this when connecting economic development, housing, transit, climate, migration, and social life.

nodes.id: Resources/Society, Culture & History/World War I — Causes and Consequences
nodes.label: World War I — Causes and Consequences
nodes.excerpt: World War I emerged from alliance commitments, militarism, imperial rivalry, nationalism, mobilization plans, crisis mismanagement, and the Sarajevo assassination. Surface this as a case study in brittle systems, escalation, modern industrial war, collapsed empires, revolutionary politics, and unresolved settlements that helped produce World War II.

nodes.id: Resources/Society, Culture & History/World War II — Causes and Consequences
nodes.label: World War II — Causes and Consequences
nodes.excerpt: World War II grew from fascist expansion, Japanese imperialism, interwar economic crisis, unresolved WWI settlements, weak collective security, militarism, and appeasement. Surface this when discussing genocide, nuclear weapons, decolonization, the UN, Bretton Woods, US-Soviet rivalry, and the institutions that define the postwar order.

nodes.label: Software Architecture
nodes.excerpt: architecture guide covering layered, hexagonal, clean architecture, DDD, modular monoliths, microservices, event-driven design, CQRS, dependency inversion, and abstraction tradeoffs.

nodes.label: Software Engineering Tools
nodes.excerpt: full survey of tools modern engineering teams use: design (Figma), IDEs (VS Code, Cursor, Zed), AI assistants (Copilot, Claude Code), CI/CD, observability, databases, and communication tools.

nodes.label: 12-Factor App Methodology
nodes.excerpt: The 12-factor app methodology (Heroku, 2011) is a set of 12 practices for building deployable, scalable SaaS applications. The core insight: separate code, config, and state so that any deploy environment is a function of environment variables and attached services, not hardcoded assumptions. The most commonly violated factors in practice: config in environment (III), stateless processes (VI), and dev/prod parity (X).

nodes.label: API Design — GraphQL
nodes.excerpt: GraphQL gives clients exact control over what data they fetch — no over/under-fetching. The schema is the contract; resolvers are the implementation. Critical operational concerns: N+1 queries (solved with DataLoader), query complexity limits (prevent denial-of-service), and field-level authorization. Best as an API composition layer for complex product surfaces.

nodes.label: API Design — REST Principles
nodes.excerpt: REST API design centers on resource URLs, correct HTTP method semantics, meaningful status codes, and consistent error formats. Practical concerns: idempotency for safe retries, cursor-based pagination for large datasets, URL versioning for breaking changes, and RFC 7807 Problem Details for machine-readable errors.

nodes.label: API Design — Server-Sent Events
nodes.excerpt: Server-Sent Events (SSE) stream text events from server to client over a persistent HTTP connection. The wire format is dead simple: text/event-stream lines with data:, event:, id:, retry: fields. Browsers get EventSource with built-in reconnection and Last-Event-ID resumption. The main real-world use is AI token streaming (OpenAI, Anthropic both use SSE). The main gotcha is proxy buffering — Nginx buffers by default and breaks SSE.

nodes.label: API Design — WebSockets
nodes.excerpt: WebSockets provide a persistent, full-duplex TCP connection over a single HTTP upgrade. They enable real-time bidirectional communication unlike REST (request/response) or SSE (server-to-client only). The hard problems are connection management at scale (Redis pub/sub for horizontal scaling), authentication (token at handshake time), and reconnection (exponential backoff with jitter). Use WebSockets for collaborative editing, live chat, multiplayer, and live dashboards; use SSE for one-way streams.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/API Design — gRPC and Protocol Buffers
nodes.label: API Design — gRPC and Protocol Buffers
nodes.excerpt: gRPC is a high-performance RPC framework using Protocol Buffers for typed binary serialization over HTTP/2. It supports four call types (unary, server-streaming, client-streaming, bidirectional). Strongest for internal polyglot service-to-service calls where latency, streaming, and generated client contracts matter. Weaker for browser clients and ad-hoc debugging.

nodes.label: Anti-Patterns and Code Smells
nodes.excerpt: Code smells are local signals that design may be wrong — long methods, god objects, feature envy, primitive obsession, shotgun surgery. Anti-patterns are repeated bad solutions — big ball of mud, golden hammer, cargo cult code. They're diagnostic prompts, not rules; context decides whether a refactor is worthwhile.

nodes.label: Backward Compatibility Strategies
nodes.excerpt: Backward compatibility means old clients keep working when you deploy new code. The strategy varies by interface type: REST APIs (additive fields, deprecation windows), gRPC/Protobuf (never remove field numbers, use reserved), databases (expand-contract pattern), message queues (consumer tolerance + schema registry), and libraries (semver contract). The golden rule across all of them: additions are safe; removals and renames require migration windows.

nodes.label: Behavior-Driven Development (BDD)
nodes.excerpt: BDD aligns engineers, product, and QA around concrete behavioral examples written in Given-When-Then format before development. The value is clarity on business rules before code is written. The failure mode is writing low-level UI scripts in business language — scenarios that describe clicks, not behavior.

nodes.label: Blue-Green Deployments
nodes.excerpt: Blue-green deployment runs two identical production environments: one live (blue), one idle (green). New version deploys to green; traffic switches atomically. Rollback is instant — flip traffic back to blue. The hard problems are database migrations (must be backward-compatible) and stateful sessions, not the traffic switch itself.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Build Systems Overview (Make, Bazel, Nx)
nodes.label: Build Systems Overview (Make, Bazel, Nx)
nodes.excerpt: Build systems automate the compilation, testing, and packaging pipeline. Make is rule-based and ubiquitous; Bazel is hermetic and graph-based (correctness over convenience); Nx and Turborepo are task orchestrators designed for JS/TS monorepos with remote caching. The key concept across all of them: a build graph where outputs depend on inputs, and incremental builds skip work when inputs haven't changed.

nodes.label: CQRS Pattern
nodes.excerpt: CQRS separates the write model (commands that enforce business invariants) from the read model (queries optimized for UI/reporting). The write model is normalized and consistent; the read model is denormalized and fast. The tradeoff is eventual consistency between writes and reads, and the complexity of maintaining projections.

nodes.label: Canary Releases
nodes.excerpt: Canary releases route a small fraction of real traffic to the new version, then watch production metrics before expanding. The canary name comes from coal mines — if it dies, you know early. Tools: Argo Rollouts and Flagger for automated analysis; Istio/NGINX for traffic splitting. The key discipline is defining automated rollback triggers before you deploy, not after something breaks.

nodes.label: Chaos Engineering
nodes.excerpt: Chaos engineering runs controlled failure experiments to find resilience weaknesses before incidents do. The workflow: define steady state → hypothesize → inject failure → compare → fix. Tools: Chaos Monkey (instance kills), Gremlin (full platform), Chaos Mesh (Kubernetes). Prerequisites: observability, runbooks, and rollback mechanisms must exist before any experiments run.

nodes.label: Clean Code Principles
nodes.excerpt: Clean code is optimized for human understanding and low-risk change. The practical rules are: names reveal intent, functions do one thing at one level of abstraction, comments explain why (not what), and duplication is controlled — but not at the cost of premature abstraction.

nodes.label: Code Complexity — Cyclomatic Complexity
nodes.excerpt: Cyclomatic complexity (McCabe, 1976) counts independent paths through a function via its control-flow graph: V(G) = E - N + 2. Each if/elif/for/while/case/catch adds 1. Thresholds: ≤10 simple, 11-20 moderate, 21-50 high, >50 untestable. High complexity correlates with defect density. Cognitive complexity (SonarSource) is a better human-readability metric — it penalizes nested structure more heavily.

nodes.label: Code Review Best Practices
nodes.excerpt: Code review is the highest-leverage quality gate: it catches correctness bugs, spreads knowledge, and enforces standards before changes harden into production. The discipline: keep PRs small (< 400 lines), distinguish blocking issues from suggestions, automate everything automatable, and review the behavior not the style.

nodes.label: Container Networking
nodes.excerpt: Container networking connects isolated container processes to hosts, other containers, services, and external networks. Understanding bridges, overlays, ports, DNS, and network policies is essential for debugging containerized systems.

nodes.label: Conventional Commits
nodes.excerpt: Conventional Commits is a commit message spec: type(scope): description. Types include feat, fix, chore, docs, refactor, test, perf, ci, build. The real payoff is tooling automation: commitlint enforces the spec in CI, semantic-release uses commit history to determine version bumps and auto-generate changelogs. The spec also makes git log a useful artifact rather than a graveyard of 'fix stuff' entries.

nodes.label: Dependency Management
nodes.excerpt: Dependency management controls what third-party code runs in your product. Core mechanics: package managers resolve dependency graphs, lockfiles pin exact versions for reproducibility, and vulnerability scanners (Dependabot, Snyk, Trivy) flag CVEs in transitive deps. The main failure modes: dependency confusion attacks, supply chain compromises (leftpad, event-stream, xz-utils), version drift, and license violations. Key discipline: minimize dependencies, audit what you have, and automate security updates.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Design Patterns — Behavioral (Observer, Strategy, Command, State, Iterator)
nodes.label: Design Patterns — Behavioral (Observer, Strategy, Command, State, Iterator)
nodes.excerpt: Behavioral patterns (Observer, Strategy, Command, State, Iterator) manage how objects interact and how behavior changes at runtime. Strategy replaces conditionals with swappable algorithms; Command makes actions first-class objects; Observer decouples event producers from consumers; State replaces flag-based conditionals with behavioral objects.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Design Patterns — Creational (Factory, Builder, Singleton, Prototype)
nodes.label: Design Patterns — Creational (Factory, Builder, Singleton, Prototype)
nodes.excerpt: Creational patterns (Factory, Builder, Singleton, Prototype) centralize object construction logic. Factory decouples callers from concrete types; Builder handles complex multi-step construction; Singleton controls shared state; Prototype clones pre-configured objects. All four are overkill for simple construction — apply only when construction is genuinely complex.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Design Patterns — Structural (Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade)
nodes.label: Design Patterns — Structural (Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade)
nodes.excerpt: Structural patterns (Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade) solve object composition problems. Adapter wraps incompatible APIs; Bridge splits abstraction from implementation; Composite treats trees uniformly; Decorator adds behavior without subclassing; Facade hides subsystem complexity. Most useful at integration boundaries.

nodes.label: Docker Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: Docker packages applications into images and runs them as isolated containers. Its practical value is consistent development, CI, and deployment environments, but production use requires attention to layers, networking, volumes, and security.

nodes.label: Domain-Driven Design (DDD)
nodes.excerpt: DDD aligns software models with the business domain through ubiquitous language, bounded contexts, and rich domain objects that enforce their own invariants. Strategic DDD (contexts, context maps) handles system-level design; tactical DDD (entities, aggregates, domain events) handles model internals. Most valuable when domain complexity — not technical complexity — is the main challenge.

nodes.label: Event Sourcing
nodes.excerpt: Event sourcing stores every state change as an immutable event rather than overwriting current state. The event log is the source of truth; current state is derived by replaying events. This gives complete audit history and time-travel debugging, but requires event schema discipline, snapshot optimization, and upcasting strategies for schema evolution.

nodes.label: Event-Driven Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Event-driven architecture coordinates systems through immutable facts about what happened. The core patterns are the outbox pattern (reliable event publishing), choreography vs orchestration (who controls the workflow), and delivery semantics (at-least-once requires idempotent consumers). Complexity shifts from code to event schema design and observability.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Feature Flags and Progressive Rollout
nodes.label: Feature Flags and Progressive Rollout
nodes.excerpt: Feature flags decouple deployment from release — code ships dark, behavior turns on separately. The four flag types (release, experiment, ops kill-switch, permission) have different lifecycles and risk models. Tools: LaunchDarkly and Unleash for production scale, GrowthBook for experiment focus. Critical discipline: flags must have owners and removal dates or they become permanent debt.

nodes.label: Git Flow vs Trunk-Based
nodes.excerpt: Git Flow and trunk-based development are branching strategies with opposite philosophies: Git Flow isolates work in long-lived branches until planned releases; TBD integrates continuously to a single branch. Git Flow suits scheduled releases with extended QA cycles; TBD suits continuous delivery. Most teams that adopted Git Flow in 2010 have since migrated toward TBD. GitHub Flow is the practical middle ground.

nodes.label: Hexagonal Architecture (Ports and Adapters)
nodes.excerpt: Hexagonal architecture places domain logic at the center, isolated from infrastructure by ports (interfaces) and adapters (implementations). The core depends on nothing external; all external systems are swapped via adapters. This makes the domain testable in isolation and infrastructure replaceable without touching business logic.

nodes.label: Integration Testing Strategies
nodes.excerpt: Integration tests verify behavior across real boundaries — databases, message queues, HTTP APIs, filesystems. The key tools are Testcontainers (real infrastructure in Docker), contract tests (Pact for API boundaries), and transaction rollback (fast DB isolation). The test trophy model argues integration tests often provide better ROI than unit tests for CRUD-heavy code.

nodes.label: Kubernetes Basics
nodes.excerpt: Kubernetes is a container orchestration system that keeps declared application state running across a cluster. It manages scheduling, service discovery, rollouts, health checks, scaling, and recovery.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Load Testing and Performance Testing
nodes.label: Load Testing and Performance Testing
nodes.excerpt: Load testing measures system behavior under realistic and peak traffic. Key metrics are p95/p99 latency, throughput (RPS), and error rate — not averages. Tools: k6 for code-based scripts, Locust for Python-native, Grafana K6 Cloud for scale. The analysis process: identify the metric that's degrading, correlate with traces and profiles, find the bottleneck.

nodes.label: Microservices Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Microservices decompose a system into independently deployable services aligned around business capabilities. The key prerequisites are strong domain boundaries, team ownership per service, and operational maturity (observability, deployment automation). Without these, microservices produce a distributed monolith — all the complexity, none of the benefits.

nodes.label: Monolith vs Microservices Tradeoffs
nodes.excerpt: Monolith vs microservices is an organizational and operational tradeoff, not just a technical one. Monoliths are simpler to build and debug; microservices enable team-level deployment independence. The modular monolith is the right intermediate step — clear internal boundaries without distributed systems complexity.

nodes.label: Monorepo vs Polyrepo
nodes.excerpt: Monorepos store all code in one repository; polyrepos split it across many. The real tradeoff isn't storage — it's integration visibility vs deployment independence. Monorepos make cross-cutting changes atomic and dependency drift impossible, but require fast selective builds. Polyrepos give services independent lifecycles but make coordination across repos painful. Tools: Nx and Turborepo for JS/TS monorepos, Bazel for large-scale. Google runs the world's largest monorepo; most startups use polyrepos by default.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Open Source Licensing (MIT, Apache, GPL, etc.)
nodes.label: Open Source Licensing (MIT, Apache, GPL, etc.)
nodes.excerpt: Open source licenses range from permissive (MIT, Apache 2.0 — use however you want, keep attribution) to copyleft (GPL — derivatives must also be open source) to network copyleft (AGPL — even SaaS use requires releasing source). The key engineering decision: GPL in a dependency forces your product open if you distribute it; AGPL does the same even if you only run it as a service. Apache 2.0 includes a patent license MIT doesn't have.

nodes.label: OpenAPI and API Documentation
nodes.excerpt: OpenAPI 3.x is a YAML/JSON spec format for describing HTTP APIs — paths, methods, parameters, request/response schemas, authentication, and errors. The real value is the toolchain it enables: Swagger UI/Redoc for browsable docs, contract testing with Spectral/Schemathesis, client code generation, and mock servers. The main failure mode is spec drift — the spec describes what the API was, not what it is. Enforce the spec in CI.

nodes.label: Property-Based Testing
nodes.excerpt: Property-based testing generates hundreds of random inputs to verify that a general invariant holds — rather than testing specific hand-picked examples. The key contribution is automatic shrinking: when a failure is found, the framework reduces the input to the minimal failing case. Best for parsers, serializers, algorithms, and any code with clear mathematical properties.

nodes.label: Refactoring Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Refactoring is improving code structure without changing behavior. The key techniques from Fowler's catalog: Extract Function, Introduce Parameter Object, Replace Conditional with Polymorphism, and Move Method. The key discipline: always refactor against a test suite in small, committed steps — large rewrites mix structural and behavioral changes and make failures untraceable.

nodes.label: SOLID Principles
nodes.excerpt: SOLID is five OOP design principles for making classes easier to change without breaking unrelated code. The most practically important are SRP (one reason to change), DIP (depend on abstractions not concretions), and LSP (don't break the contract your base type implied). Applying all five mechanically without context produces over-engineered code.

nodes.label: Semantic Versioning
nodes.excerpt: Semantic Versioning (semver) is a contract: MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH where breaking changes bump MAJOR, new backward-compatible features bump MINOR, and bug fixes bump PATCH. The spec only works if you have a clear definition of your public API — the hard part isn't the numbers, it's the discipline. Tooling: semantic-release automates version decisions from commit history. Version ranges (^, ~) in package managers encode consumer trust.

nodes.label: Service Mesh (Istio, Linkerd)
nodes.excerpt: A service mesh adds a dedicated infrastructure layer for service-to-service traffic management, observability, security, and policy. It is powerful in large microservice environments but can be unnecessary operational complexity for simpler systems.

nodes.label: Software Estimation Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Software estimation is predicting effort and uncertainty, not committing to a date. The most reliable techniques: decompose work into <2 day tasks (reduces variance), use three-point estimates with PERT formula, and calibrate against historical velocity. Hofstadter's Law is always true: it takes longer than you think, even when you account for Hofstadter's Law. The output of estimation should be a range with explicit assumptions, not a point estimate.

nodes.label: Static Analysis Tools
nodes.excerpt: Static analysis tools catch bugs, security issues, type errors, and style violations without running code. The key is tuning: too strict and developers ignore it, too loose and it provides no signal. Tools span linters (ruff, ESLint), type checkers (mypy, TypeScript), SAST security scanners (Semgrep, Bandit), and formatters (Black, Prettier). All should run in pre-commit hooks and CI.

nodes.id: Resources/Software Engineering/Technical Debt — Types and Management
nodes.label: Technical Debt — Types and Management
nodes.excerpt: Technical debt is the future cost created by today's shortcuts. Fowler's quadrant distinguishes deliberate/inadvertent × reckless/prudent debt — only prudent-deliberate debt (conscious tradeoff with a paydown plan) is acceptable. Manage by debt register, prioritize by change frequency and blast radius, communicate to product using the interest metaphor.

nodes.label: Test-Driven Development (TDD)
nodes.excerpt: TDD is a design discipline: write a failing test, write minimal code to pass it, refactor. The value isn't test coverage — it's design pressure. TDD forces small interfaces, low coupling, and behavior-first thinking. Hardest to apply at UI boundaries and infrastructure edges; most valuable for domain logic and algorithms.

nodes.label: The Unix Philosophy
nodes.excerpt: The Unix philosophy (McIlroy, 1978) is three rules: do one thing well, make programs work together, handle text streams. The real lesson isn't 'make everything tiny' — it's 'design for composition.' Pipes as the composition primitive, text as the universal data format, silence on success as the failure signaling convention. The philosophy influenced microservices, REST APIs, and CLI tool design. Its tension: composition requires stable interfaces, and sometimes a monolithic design is clearer.

nodes.label: Trunk-Based Development
nodes.excerpt: Trunk-based development (TBD) means everyone commits to a single branch (main/trunk) with branches lasting no longer than 1–2 days. The mechanism that makes it work: feature flags gate incomplete code, not branches. TBD is the highest-correlated practice with DORA's elite deployment frequency metric. The prerequisite is a fast, reliable CI pipeline — without it, TBD just means broken trunk.

nodes.label: Virtualization — VMs vs Containers
nodes.excerpt: VMs virtualize hardware via a hypervisor — each VM has its own OS kernel, strong isolation, and slow startup (seconds). Containers share the host kernel and use Linux namespaces + cgroups for isolation — lighter, faster (milliseconds), but weaker isolation. The security tradeoff is real: container escapes are possible; VM escapes are extremely rare. In practice: cloud VMs provide the trust boundary; containers run inside them for density and deployment flexibility.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Active Directory — How It Works (Domains, OUs, Forest, Trusts, Kerberos)
nodes.label: Active Directory — How It Works (Domains, OUs, Forest, Trusts, Kerberos)
nodes.excerpt: Active Directory is Microsoft's directory service — the identity and policy backbone of most enterprise Windows environments. It combines LDAP (object storage), Kerberos (authentication), DNS (service discovery), and Group Policy (configuration management) into one system. Understanding its structure is essential for enterprise IT administration and security.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Capacity Planning for IT Infrastructure — How to Forecast Growth
nodes.label: Capacity Planning for IT Infrastructure — How to Forecast Growth
nodes.excerpt: Capacity planning ensures that infrastructure resources — CPU, memory, storage, network bandwidth, power — don't run out before the business can respond. It requires tracking current utilization trends, understanding procurement lead times, and translating business growth plans into infrastructure requirements.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Certificate Management — Internal CAs, Let's Encrypt, ACME Protocol
nodes.label: Certificate Management — Internal CAs, Let's Encrypt, ACME Protocol
nodes.excerpt: Certificate management covers issuing, distributing, renewing, and revoking X.509 TLS certificates — both from public CAs like Let's Encrypt and from internal CAs for private infrastructure. Expired or misconfigured certificates are one of the most common causes of sudden outages.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Cron, systemd Timers, and Task Scheduling in Linux
nodes.label: Cron, systemd Timers, and Task Scheduling in Linux
nodes.excerpt: Cron and systemd timers are the two ways to schedule recurring tasks in Linux. Cron is simpler and universal; systemd timers integrate with service management, provide better logging, and support dependency ordering. Knowing when to use each — and how to debug both — is core Linux administration.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/DHCP Server Setup and IP Address Management (IPAM)
nodes.label: DHCP Server Setup and IP Address Management (IPAM)
nodes.excerpt: DHCP automates IP address assignment across a network using a four-step lease exchange (DORA). IPAM extends this by tracking which IPs are assigned, to what devices, and for how long — providing the visibility needed to prevent conflicts and support audits.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/DNS Server Configuration (Bind9, Windows DNS, CoreDNS, Split-Horizon)
nodes.label: DNS Server Configuration (Bind9, Windows DNS, CoreDNS, Split-Horizon)
nodes.excerpt: DNS server administration covers running authoritative and recursive resolvers, managing zone files and record types, and implementing split-horizon DNS for internal/external routing differences. It's a foundational IT skill because DNS failure breaks authentication, email, web services, and Active Directory simultaneously.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Disaster Recovery and Business Continuity Planning (RPO, RTO)
nodes.label: Disaster Recovery and Business Continuity Planning (RPO, RTO)
nodes.excerpt: Disaster Recovery (DR) is the IT-specific plan for restoring systems after a catastrophic event. Business Continuity Planning (BCP) is the broader organizational plan for keeping operations running during a disruption. RPO and RTO are the two metrics that define how much data loss and how much downtime a business can accept — everything in the DR plan flows from these.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Email Server Architecture (MX Records, SMTP Relay, SPF, DKIM, DMARC)
nodes.label: Email Server Architecture (MX Records, SMTP Relay, SPF, DKIM, DMARC)
nodes.excerpt: Email delivery relies on MX records for routing, SMTP for transport, and three DNS-based authentication standards — SPF, DKIM, and DMARC — to prevent spoofing and improve deliverability. Understanding this stack is essential for diagnosing why email ends up in spam or fails to deliver.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Endpoint Management — MDM, Microsoft Intune, Jamf for macOS-iOS
nodes.label: Endpoint Management — MDM, Microsoft Intune, Jamf for macOS-iOS
nodes.excerpt: MDM (Mobile Device Management) lets IT configure, secure, and remotely manage laptops, phones, and tablets at scale. Microsoft Intune handles Windows/macOS/iOS/Android in Microsoft 365 environments; Jamf specializes in macOS and iOS. MDM is a foundational requirement for Zero Trust device trust policies.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Group Policy Objects (GPOs) — How Windows Domain Policy Is Enforced
nodes.label: Group Policy Objects (GPOs) — How Windows Domain Policy Is Enforced
nodes.excerpt: Group Policy Objects (GPOs) are the mechanism Windows domains use to push configuration — security settings, software, drive mappings, firewall rules — to users and computers. Policy is applied hierarchically and automatically; understanding the precedence rules and troubleshooting tools is essential for enterprise Windows administration.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Hardware Asset Management and IT Procurement Lifecycle
nodes.label: Hardware Asset Management and IT Procurement Lifecycle
nodes.excerpt: Hardware asset management tracks every physical IT asset through its full lifecycle — from procurement through deployment, maintenance, and secure disposal. Without it, organizations lose hardware, overpay for support on unknown inventory, and create data breach risk at end-of-life. Procurement feeds directly into refresh planning and budget cycles.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/IT Help Desk Troubleshooting Methodology — The Systematic Approach
nodes.label: IT Help Desk Troubleshooting Methodology — The Systematic Approach
nodes.excerpt: The systematic troubleshooting approach — define the problem, hypothesize, test, implement, verify, document — prevents the common failure mode of random changes that make things worse. Combined with the OSI model for network issues and structured escalation, it's the mental framework every IT professional uses to work through unfamiliar failures.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/IT Security Fundamentals — Hardening, Least Privilege, Network Segmentation
nodes.label: IT Security Fundamentals — Hardening, Least Privilege, Network Segmentation
nodes.excerpt: IT security fundamentals are the core principles and controls that underpin all security work: defense in depth, least privilege, network segmentation, encryption, monitoring, and incident response. Understanding these as a system — not as isolated checklist items — is what separates reactive security posture from a proactive one.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/IT Ticketing and ITSM — ITIL Fundamentals and ServiceNow Overview
nodes.label: IT Ticketing and ITSM — ITIL Fundamentals and ServiceNow Overview
nodes.excerpt: ITSM (IT Service Management) is the practice of delivering IT as a structured service aligned to business needs. ITIL 4 is the dominant framework — it defines processes for incident management, problem management, change control, and more. ServiceNow is the leading enterprise platform that implements these processes in software.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Kerberos Authentication — How Tickets Work in Windows Environments
nodes.label: Kerberos Authentication — How Tickets Work in Windows Environments
nodes.excerpt: Kerberos is the default authentication protocol in Windows Active Directory environments. It uses cryptographic tickets instead of passwords on the wire, allows single sign-on across services, and is the foundation for most enterprise Windows auth — including the attacks that target it (Golden Ticket, Pass-the-Ticket).

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/LDAP and Directory Services — Protocol and Schema
nodes.label: LDAP and Directory Services — Protocol and Schema
nodes.excerpt: LDAP (Lightweight Directory Access Protocol) is the standard protocol for querying and modifying directory services — hierarchical databases of users, groups, and resources. It is the protocol underlying Active Directory, OpenLDAP, FreeIPA, and most enterprise identity systems.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Linux System Monitoring — top, htop, iostat, sar, -proc Filesystem
nodes.label: Linux System Monitoring — top, htop, iostat, sar, -proc Filesystem
nodes.excerpt: Linux system monitoring uses a toolkit of CLI tools — top/htop for live process and resource views, iostat for disk I/O, sar for historical metrics, and the /proc filesystem for raw kernel state. Understanding what each metric means and when it indicates a problem is essential for diagnosing performance issues on Linux servers.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Linux User, Group, and Permission Management (useradd, chmod, chown, ACLs)
nodes.label: Linux User, Group, and Permission Management (useradd, chmod, chown, ACLs)
nodes.excerpt: Linux encodes identity and access in UIDs, GIDs, and permission bits on every file and process. Understanding useradd, chmod, chown, and POSIX ACLs is fundamental to secure multi-user system administration — both for day-to-day access control and for understanding privilege escalation risks.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Log Management and SIEM — Syslog, Splunk, Elastic Stack (ELK)
nodes.label: Log Management and SIEM — Syslog, Splunk, Elastic Stack (ELK)
nodes.excerpt: Log management collects, stores, and searches logs from all infrastructure sources. A SIEM (Security Information and Event Management) adds correlation rules, alerting, and incident workflow on top. Together they provide the visibility needed to detect breaches, investigate incidents, and satisfy compliance audits.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Monitoring Windows Servers — Event Viewer, Performance Monitor, WMI
nodes.label: Monitoring Windows Servers — Event Viewer, Performance Monitor, WMI
nodes.excerpt: Windows Server monitoring uses Event Viewer for log-based diagnostics, Performance Monitor for real-time and historical resource metrics, and WMI/PowerShell for programmatic queries. Together they cover the two fundamental questions: what happened (event logs) and what's the system doing right now (performance counters).

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/NFS and SMB — Network File Sharing Protocols Compared
nodes.label: NFS and SMB — Network File Sharing Protocols Compared
