nodes.excerpt: NFS and SMB are the two dominant network file sharing protocols — NFS is the Linux/Unix standard, SMB (via Samba) is the Windows standard. Understanding when to use each, how they handle authentication, and their failure modes is essential for shared storage administration.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/NTP and Chrony — Why Clock Accuracy Matters and How to Set It Up
nodes.label: NTP and Chrony — Why Clock Accuracy Matters and How to Set It Up
nodes.excerpt: NTP (Network Time Protocol) keeps clocks synchronized across systems. Accurate time is a hard dependency for Kerberos authentication, TLS certificates, distributed transaction ordering, and log correlation — clock drift as small as 5 minutes can break Kerberos logins entirely. Chrony is the modern NTP implementation that replaced ntpd.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Network Documentation — IP Schema, Rack Diagrams, Network Topology Maps
nodes.label: Network Documentation — IP Schema, Rack Diagrams, Network Topology Maps
nodes.excerpt: Network documentation — IP schemas, topology diagrams, rack diagrams, and configuration backups — is what separates a managed network from a mystery. Accurate documentation is the foundation for change management, incident response, and onboarding, and it degrades rapidly without a process to keep it current.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/PAM — Pluggable Authentication Modules and How Login Actually Works
nodes.label: PAM — Pluggable Authentication Modules and How Login Actually Works
nodes.excerpt: PAM is the pluggable authentication layer that sits between a Linux service (SSH, sudo, login) and the actual credential verification. Every login on a Linux system goes through PAM — misconfiguring it can lock out all users or silently bypass security checks.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Patch Management Strategies (WSUS, Ansible, Unattended Upgrades)
nodes.label: Patch Management Strategies (WSUS, Ansible, Unattended Upgrades)
nodes.excerpt: Patch management is the process of keeping systems current with security fixes and bug patches in a controlled, auditable way. It balances speed (patch critical vulns fast) against stability (don't break production). The tooling differs by OS — WSUS for Windows, unattended-upgrades for Debian/Ubuntu, Ansible for fleet-wide orchestration.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/RAID Levels Explained — What They Actually Protect Against (and Don't)
nodes.label: RAID Levels Explained — What They Actually Protect Against (and Don't)
nodes.excerpt: RAID (Redundant Array of Independent Disks) protects against physical disk failure by distributing data and/or parity across multiple drives. It does not protect against accidental deletion, ransomware, logical corruption, or controller failure — those require backups. Choosing the right RAID level is a tradeoff between capacity, performance, and fault tolerance.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Real Scenario - Onboarding a New Employee — Accounts, Access, Equipment Checklist
nodes.label: Real Scenario - Onboarding a New Employee — Accounts, Access, Equipment Checklist
nodes.excerpt: Employee onboarding is the IT process that provisions accounts, access, and equipment for a new hire — and offboarding is its mirror, which revokes all of that immediately on departure. Both are cross-functional workflows touching HR, IT, security, and the hiring manager. Failure on onboarding means the employee can't work on day 1; failure on offboarding is a security incident.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Remote Desktop and Remote Management (RDP, SSH Bastions, Jump Hosts)
nodes.label: Remote Desktop and Remote Management (RDP, SSH Bastions, Jump Hosts)
nodes.excerpt: Remote management covers the protocols and architecture for accessing servers and desktops without physical presence — RDP for Windows GUIs, SSH for Linux shells, and bastion hosts / jump hosts as the hardened single entry point that provides access control and audit logging for all remote sessions.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/SNMP Traps and Network Device Monitoring
nodes.label: SNMP Traps and Network Device Monitoring
nodes.excerpt: SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) is how network devices — switches, routers, UPS units, printers — expose their health metrics and send event notifications. Understanding polling vs traps, OIDs/MIBs, and SNMPv3 security is essential for building a network monitoring stack that actually alerts on problems.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Server Hardening — CIS Benchmarks, Minimal Attack Surface, Fail2ban
nodes.label: Server Hardening — CIS Benchmarks, Minimal Attack Surface, Fail2ban
nodes.excerpt: Server hardening reduces the attack surface of a system by removing unnecessary software, disabling unused services, enforcing strict access controls, and configuring security tools like Fail2ban and firewalls. CIS Benchmarks provide the authoritative, OS-specific hardening checklists used across the industry.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Storage Area Networks (SAN) vs NAS vs DAS — When to Use Each
nodes.label: Storage Area Networks (SAN) vs NAS vs DAS — When to Use Each
nodes.excerpt: DAS, NAS, and SAN are the three storage architectures in enterprise IT. The key distinction: DAS is local to one server, NAS provides shared file access over the network, and SAN provides shared block access over a dedicated storage network — each optimized for different performance and sharing requirements.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Sudoers File and Privilege Escalation (sudoers, polkit)
nodes.label: Sudoers File and Privilege Escalation (sudoers, polkit)
nodes.excerpt: The sudoers file controls which users can run which commands as root (or another user) on a Linux system. A misconfigured sudoers entry is one of the most common local privilege escalation vectors — understanding the syntax and security implications is essential for both hardening and auditing.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/The 3-2-1 Backup Rule — How to Implement Reliable Backups
nodes.label: The 3-2-1 Backup Rule — How to Implement Reliable Backups
nodes.excerpt: The 3-2-1 rule is the foundational backup strategy: 3 copies of data, on 2 different media types, with 1 copy offsite. In the ransomware era, this is often extended to 3-2-1-1-0: add an immutable or air-gapped copy, and verify zero restore errors. An untested backup is not a backup.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Virtualization for IT — VMware vSphere, Proxmox, Hyper-V Compared
nodes.label: Virtualization for IT — VMware vSphere, Proxmox, Hyper-V Compared
nodes.excerpt: Virtualization lets multiple VMs share one physical host by abstracting hardware through a hypervisor. VMware vSphere is the enterprise standard with advanced cluster features; Hyper-V is the Windows-native option; Proxmox is a capable open-source alternative. Understanding the differences — and VM lifecycle, storage, and networking concepts — is core IT infrastructure knowledge.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/Zero Trust for IT — Why VPNs Aren't Enough and What Replaces Them
nodes.label: Zero Trust for IT — Why VPNs Aren't Enough and What Replaces Them
nodes.excerpt: Zero Trust is a security model that eliminates implicit network-based trust — the assumption that users inside the VPN perimeter are safe. Instead, every access request is verified against identity, device health, and context before being granted. It's the architectural response to cloud adoption, remote work, and the reality that perimeters are regularly breached.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/iDRAC, iLO, IPMI — Out-of-Band Server Management
nodes.label: iDRAC, iLO, IPMI — Out-of-Band Server Management
nodes.excerpt: iDRAC (Dell), iLO (HPE), and IPMI are out-of-band server management interfaces — a dedicated subsystem that lets you power cycle, view the console, and monitor hardware health even when the server's OS is down or the server is completely powered off. Critical for remote data center management and after-hours incident response.

nodes.id: Resources/System Administration & IT/iSCSI and Fibre Channel — How SAN Storage Is Accessed
nodes.label: iSCSI and Fibre Channel — How SAN Storage Is Accessed
nodes.excerpt: iSCSI and Fibre Channel are the two protocols that connect servers to SAN storage arrays at the block level. iSCSI runs SCSI commands over standard TCP/IP Ethernet — cheaper and more flexible. Fibre Channel uses a dedicated high-speed storage fabric — lower latency, higher throughput, higher cost. NVMe-oF is the emerging successor to both.

nodes.label: System Design Case Studies
nodes.excerpt: end-to-end design studies for feeds, chat, file sync, collaborative docs, dispatch, payments, video platforms, and job recommendations.

nodes.label: System Design Interview Rubric
nodes.excerpt: 8-phase rubric for FAANG-level system design interviews: requirements, scale estimation, API design, data model, architecture, deep dive, cross-cutting concerns, and evolution.

nodes.label: System Design Notes
nodes.excerpt: scalability, caching, SQL vs NoSQL, queues, load balancing, CAP theorem, rate limiting.

nodes.label: Bulkhead Pattern
nodes.excerpt: The bulkhead pattern partitions resources so one failing or saturated dependency cannot exhaust the entire system. Named after ship compartments that prevent one breach from sinking everything. Two main forms: thread pool bulkheads (separate fixed-size thread pools per dependency) and semaphore bulkheads (concurrent call count caps without pool overhead). Critical for multi-tenant SaaS (noisy neighbor isolation), microservices (recommendation slowness shouldn't kill payments), and queue systems (image processing backlog shouldn't block email delivery).

nodes.label: CAP Theorem
nodes.excerpt: CAP theorem (Brewer's conjecture, 2000; formally proved by Gilbert & Lynch, 2002) states that during a network partition, a distributed data system must choose between consistency (every read sees the latest write or an error) and availability (every request receives a non-error response). Since partitions are inevitable in real networks, the practical design choice is CP (sacrifice availability to preserve consistency) vs AP (sacrifice consistency to preserve availability). The most important clarification: CAP consistency means linearizability — a much stricter guarantee than 'no stale reads' — and the CA combination only applies to non-distributed single-node systems.

nodes.label: CDN Architecture
nodes.excerpt: A CDN (content delivery network) is a geographically distributed network of edge servers that cache and serve content near users, reducing latency and origin load. The core architecture: client DNS resolves to the nearest edge via anycast or GeoDNS; edge serves from cache on hit; on miss, edge fetches from origin and caches per HTTP cache headers. The most important operational decisions are cache key construction (what makes two requests distinct), TTL and invalidation strategy (purge API vs versioned URLs), and when to use edge compute (Cloudflare Workers, Lambda@Edge) for dynamic content that doesn't cache but benefits from edge processing.

nodes.label: Cache Invalidation Patterns
nodes.excerpt: Cache invalidation is the problem of keeping cached data consistent with the source of truth when the source changes. Phil Karlton's quote ('There are only two hard things in Computer Science: cache invalidation and naming things') understates the actual difficulty: invalidation must handle distributed cache layers, concurrent writes, network delays, and the CAP tradeoff between consistency and availability. The practical patterns — delete on write, write-through, versioned keys, event-driven invalidation, and stale-while-revalidate — each make different tradeoffs between correctness, complexity, and performance.

nodes.label: Caching Strategies (LRU, LFU, TTL)
nodes.excerpt: Caching stores computed or retrieved data in fast storage so subsequent requests can skip expensive recomputation or I/O. The hard problems aren't storing data — they're eviction (what to remove when space is full), invalidation (when to discard stale data), and failure handling (what happens when the cache is unavailable). LRU (least recently used) is the default eviction policy; LFU (least frequently used) is better for stable hot-key workloads. The three cache failure modes — stampede, penetration, and avalanche — each require specific mitigations. Redis is the canonical distributed cache; Memcached is simpler but lacks persistence and data structures.

nodes.label: Circuit Breaker Pattern
nodes.excerpt: The circuit breaker pattern stops calls to a failing dependency before failures cascade into system-wide outage. It operates as a state machine with three states — Closed (normal), Open (fail-fast), Half-Open (probing recovery) — tripped by configurable failure thresholds. In practice: a 50% error rate over 20 calls in a 10-second sliding window opens the circuit; after a 30-second wait, one probe request tests recovery. The pattern is popularized by Netflix Hystrix (now Resilience4j) and described in Michael Nygard's 'Release It!' (2007). Critical pairing: circuit breakers stop cascading failures; bulkheads prevent resource exhaustion; timeouts prevent hanging threads.

nodes.label: Consensus Algorithms (Raft, Paxos)
nodes.excerpt: Consensus algorithms let distributed nodes agree on an ordered sequence of values despite node failures and message delays. The problem: any two correct nodes that decide must decide the same value. Paxos (Lamport 1989/1998) is the foundational theoretical protocol; Raft (Ongaro & Ousterhout, 2014) was designed to be understandable and implementable. Real systems use Raft (etcd, CockroachDB, TiKV), ZAB (ZooKeeper, Paxos-like), or Viewstamped Replication. Key insight: consensus requires at least ⌊(N-1)/2⌋ fault tolerance — a 5-node cluster tolerates 2 failures. Engineers use consensus via etcd, ZooKeeper, or Consul — never implement from scratch.

nodes.label: Consistent Hashing
nodes.excerpt: Consistent hashing distributes keys across a cluster of nodes so that adding or removing a node only remaps ~1/N of keys (vs. nearly all keys under modulo hashing). The algorithm places both keys and nodes on a circular hash ring; each key is owned by the first node clockwise from its hash position. Virtual nodes (each physical node occupying multiple ring positions) fix the load imbalance problem from sparse physical node placement. Consistent hashing is used by Cassandra, DynamoDB, Memcached, and many CDN and load balancing systems — anywhere node membership changes frequently and rehashing disruption must be minimal.

nodes.id: Resources/System Design/Content Delivery and Edge Computing
nodes.label: Content Delivery and Edge Computing
nodes.excerpt: Content delivery moves cached data closer to users via CDN PoPs (200+ locations for Cloudflare/Akamai); edge computing runs logic at those locations. The CDN fundamentals (cache keys, TTL, pull vs push, purge) live in CDN Architecture — this note focuses on edge compute: Cloudflare Workers, AWS Lambda@Edge, and Fastly Compute@Edge. Key distinction: CDN = cache static assets; edge compute = run code at the edge. Edge compute uses V8 Isolates (not containers — cold start ~0ms vs 100ms+) with strict runtime limits (~50ms CPU, ~128MB memory, no filesystem). Best uses: A/B testing, geo-routing, auth at edge, request/response transformation. Worst uses: database-heavy operations, strong consistency requirements, long-running computation.

nodes.label: Database Replication
nodes.excerpt: Database replication copies data from one node to others for read scaling, high availability, and disaster recovery. The replication substrate is the write-ahead log (WAL) — every change is logged before being applied, and replicas replay the log to converge. The core tradeoff: synchronous replication (RPO=0, guaranteed no data loss) vs. asynchronous replication (lower write latency, but potential data loss on failover). The consistency anomalies from replication lag — reading your own writes, monotonic reads violations, and causally-inconsistent reads — are what cause most replication-related bugs in production.

nodes.label: Database Sharding
nodes.excerpt: Database sharding horizontally partitions data across multiple nodes, each owning a disjoint subset. It solves the problem that a single database primary eventually saturates on write throughput or storage capacity, regardless of how vertically scaled it is. The shard key selection is the most critical design decision — a bad shard key creates hotspots, prevents efficient queries, or requires expensive cross-shard joins. Resharding (redistributing data as the cluster grows) is operationally difficult and should be anticipated with consistent hashing or virtual nodes. The recommendation: exhaust indexing, caching, read replicas, and vertical scaling before sharding — sharding is one of the hardest architectural decisions to reverse.

nodes.label: Design Twitter X Feed
nodes.excerpt: Twitter/X feed design centers on the fan-out problem: when a user posts, how do followers' feeds get updated? Fan-out on write (push): tweet is inserted into each follower's timeline at write time — reads are O(1) from cache, but a celebrity with 10M followers generates 10M writes per tweet. Fan-out on read (pull): followers' feeds computed on demand by merging followed-user posts — reads are expensive (scatter-gather N users), writes are cheap. Twitter's hybrid: fan-out on write for normal accounts (<~10K followers), fan-out on read for celebrities (>1M followers), merged at read time. Home timeline cached in Redis sorted set per user (max 800 tweets). Ranking: chronological timeline → ML ranking model → reranked feed.

nodes.label: Design Uber Ride-Sharing Backend
nodes.excerpt: Uber's backend is a real-time geospatial marketplace. Core components: location service (drivers stream GPS every 4s → Redis geospatial index with 30s TTL), matching service (geohash/S2 cell lookup for nearby drivers → rank by ETA not distance → offer to top driver, 15s accept window), trip state machine (REQUESTED → DRIVER_ASSIGNED → EN_ROUTE → ARRIVED → IN_PROGRESS → COMPLETED), surge pricing (supply/demand ratio per geographic cell → pricing multiplier). Data consistency split: driver location = eventually consistent (Redis, lose old data), trip state = strongly consistent (MySQL with explicit state transitions). ETA calculation uses Dijkstra on road network graph (OpenStreetMap), updated with real-time traffic data. Payment uses saga pattern: authorize → complete trip → capture → send receipt.

nodes.label: Design a Chat Application
nodes.excerpt: Chat system design combines WebSocket gateway servers (stateful, 10K-100K connections each, sticky load balanced), durable message storage (Cassandra partitioned by conversation_id, clustered by Snowflake message_id for ordering), and a pub/sub broker (Redis for real-time delivery across gateways). Message flow: client → gateway → chat service → Cassandra write + Redis pub/sub → other gateways → connected recipients. Key decisions: fan-out strategy for group chats (write for <500 members, read for large groups), delivery states (sent→delivered→read require recipient ACKs), offline sync (reconnect sends last_seen_message_id, server returns missed messages), and media (client uploads directly to S3 via presigned URL, message stores URL reference).

nodes.label: Design a Distributed Cache
nodes.excerpt: Distributed cache design covers: consistent hashing for key→node routing (adding one node remaps only ~1/N keys vs modulo's ~(N-1)/N), replication (primary-replica for read scale, Redis Sentinel for failover), eviction policies (LRU via doubly-linked list + hashmap O(1); LFU via frequency buckets + decay), and three failure modes (stampede, penetration, avalanche — each with specific mitigations). Hot key problem: single viral key overwhelms one node — solutions: local L1 in-process cache + distributed L2, or key sharding (post:123:shard:0..9 spread across 10 nodes). Redis Cluster uses 16,384 hash slots mapped to nodes, gossip protocol for cluster state, CRC16(key) % 16384 for slot assignment.

nodes.id: Resources/System Design/Design a File Storage System (S3-like)
nodes.label: Design a File Storage System (S3-like)
nodes.excerpt: S3-like object storage addresses objects by (bucket, key) → unique worldwide namespace. Large files use multipart upload (5MB-5GB parts, uploaded in parallel, assembled on CompleteMultipartUpload). Storage nodes hold chunks (default 64MB); a metadata service (MySQL or DynamoDB) maps (bucket, key, version_id) → [chunk_list, checksum, permissions, storage_class]. Durability via erasure coding (8+4 = any 8 of 12 shards can reconstruct the object, 60% storage overhead vs 200% for 3-way replication) for cold storage; 3-replica replication for hot storage. Presigned URLs let clients upload/download directly without routing through the API. S3 achieved strong read-after-write consistency in Dec 2020; prior to that, eventual consistency was the model.

nodes.label: Design a Notification System
nodes.excerpt: Notification system design separates three categories with different guarantees: transactional (2FA codes, payment confirms — must deliver, no batching), social (likes, comments — can aggregate and suppress), and marketing (promotions — user opt-in controls). Architecture: event producers → Kafka → preference engine → aggregation → channel routing → delivery workers (FCM/APNs/Twilio/SendGrid). Idempotency key = hash(event_id + user_id + channel), stored in Redis with TTL matching retry window. Aggregation is the hardest piece: 'Alice and 47 others liked your post' requires time-window bucketing by (user_id, event_type, context_key). Rate limiting prevents fatigue: 5 push/hour/user, 3 SMS/day/user.

nodes.label: Design a Payment System
nodes.excerpt: Payment system design prioritizes correctness over throughput. The central pattern: PaymentIntent (Stripe's model) — create intent → confirm → provider webhook updates status. Idempotency is non-negotiable: client generates UUID before request; server stores (idempotency_key → result) for 24h; on retry returns stored result without re-charging. Ledger uses double-entry bookkeeping: every money movement = debit one account + credit another in same transaction, append-only, never delete. State machine: CREATED → PROCESSING → AUTHORIZED → CAPTURED → REFUNDED → FAILED → DISPUTED. Provider webhook handling: verify HMAC signature → process idempotently → respond 200 immediately → process async. Amounts stored as integers in smallest unit (cents); never use floating point for money.

nodes.label: Design a Rate Limiter
nodes.excerpt: Rate limiter system design (as a service, not just the algorithm) covers: where to enforce (client library, API gateway plugin, or standalone middleware service), what identity to limit by (IP, user_id, API key, tenant — multi-tier applied in order), which algorithm to use (sliding window counter: O(1) storage, ~99.6% accuracy, Cloudflare-validated), and how to handle failures (fail-open = allow all when Redis is down, standard choice for availability-first services). Redis-backed centralized enforcement adds ~1-2ms. Multi-datacenter: regional Redis clusters with slight overcounting accepted. Dynamic rules: limits stored in Redis/DB and hot-reloaded, not hardcoded. See Rate Limiting Algorithms note for the five algorithm implementations in detail.

nodes.label: Design a Recommendation System
nodes.excerpt: Recommendation systems use a two-stage architecture: candidate generation (fast, approximate — ANN retrieval from user/item embeddings, reduces 10M items to 1K candidates in ~10ms) + ranking (slow, accurate — ML model scores each candidate using rich features in ~50ms). Candidate generation uses collaborative filtering (matrix factorization: ALS learns user+item latent vectors, dot product = predicted preference) or content-based filtering. Ranking uses GBDT (LightGBM) or neural models with user features, item features, and context features. Feature pipeline: offline (Spark batch → feature store) + online (Redis for real-time session features). Feedback loops amplify popular items — exploration strategies (ε-greedy, Thompson sampling) counter this.

nodes.id: Resources/System Design/Design a Scheduling System (like Calendly)
nodes.label: Design a Scheduling System (like Calendly)
nodes.excerpt: Scheduling system design (Calendly-like) has three hard problems: time zone correctness (always store UTC, use IANA zone names not fixed offsets, convert at display time — DST changes offsets), slot computation (generate slots from availability rules → subtract busy events from Google Calendar/Outlook sync → return free slots), and double-booking prevention (unique constraint on (host_user_id, start_time) + SELECT FOR UPDATE + booking hold TTL). Calendly's availability model: rules define working hours per day-of-week, with minimum buffer between meetings, maximum meetings per day, and blackout dates. Calendar sync: OAuth2 → Google Calendar API polling + webhook push for real-time busy event updates.

nodes.label: Design a Search Engine
nodes.excerpt: Search system design (product/enterprise scope, not web crawling) builds an inverted index: term → postings list [{doc_id, frequency, positions}]. Ranking uses BM25 (the standard lexical algorithm, controlled by k1=1.2 and b=0.75 parameters) for lexical search, or vector cosine similarity for semantic search, or a weighted hybrid. Index pipeline: document → tokenization → normalization → stemming → inverted index construction → distributed across shards. Query path: query parse → lexical expansion (synonyms, spell correction) → shard fan-out → merge + rank → rerank → return. Autocomplete via Trie or Redis ZADD prefix scoring. Freshness via near-real-time indexing (Elasticsearch 1-second refresh interval). Evaluation: NDCG and MRR offline; CTR and conversion online.

nodes.label: Design a URL Shortener
nodes.excerpt: A URL shortener maps long URLs to 7-character base62 codes and redirects users via HTTP 302. Classic interview design that tests key generation (counter-based Snowflake ID encoded to base62 vs random vs hash), caching (hot codes in Redis), 301 vs 302 redirect semantics (301 = browser caches, kills analytics; 302 = server always called, enables click tracking), and async analytics (Kafka → ClickHouse). Scale: 100M URLs/day = 1,157 writes/sec; 10:1 read:write = 11,570 reads/sec, almost all served from Redis cache. Storage: ~10 years × 100M/day × 500 bytes/record ≈ 182TB of URL mappings.

nodes.id: Resources/System Design/Design a Video Streaming Platform
nodes.label: Design a Video Streaming Platform
nodes.excerpt: Video streaming architecture has two separate pipelines: upload/transcoding (async, expensive) and playback (real-time, CDN-critical). Upload: client → S3 via presigned multipart URL → transcoding job queued on Kafka → FFmpeg workers generate multiple renditions (360p/480p/720p/1080p/4K) in H.264 (broad compat) and H.265 (50% better compression). Output: HLS segments (2-6s each) + manifest (.m3u8). Adaptive Bitrate (ABR): player measures bandwidth, selects quality tier, switches seamlessly per segment. CDN is non-optional: 1080p H.264 = ~8 Mbps × 100K concurrent viewers = 800 Gbps → requires CDN. Storage: originals in S3 Standard, transcoded in Standard-IA, cold content moves to Glacier after 90 days. View count: Redis incr → batch flush to DB every 60s.

nodes.label: Design an API Gateway
nodes.excerpt: An API gateway centralizes cross-cutting concerns for client-facing APIs: request routing (path/host/header-based, path rewriting), authentication (JWT verification, API key lookup in Redis), rate limiting (token bucket per API key, multi-tier), TLS termination, request/response transformation, and observability (every request gets a trace ID, logs request+response+latency). Adds ~1-5ms latency. Must be stateless (all state in Redis/DB) and deployed behind an L4 load balancer for HA. Key distinction from service mesh: gateway handles edge traffic (external clients); service mesh handles internal traffic (service-to-service). Real implementations: Kong (Nginx/OpenResty plugin-based), AWS API Gateway (managed), Envoy (proxy-based, sidecar-capable).

nodes.label: Distributed Locking
nodes.excerpt: Distributed locking coordinates exclusive access to a shared resource across multiple processes or machines. Harder than local locking because nodes can pause (GC, OS scheduling), crash, or disagree about time. The standard patterns: Redis SET NX EX for non-critical locks (single Redis instance, O(1) acquisition), Redlock for higher fault-tolerance (5-node Redis quorum, but Martin Kleppmann's 2016 critique shows it's unsafe under clock drift/GC pauses), etcd/ZooKeeper leases for strong guarantees (consensus-backed). Fencing tokens (monotonically increasing counter from the lock server) are the key safety mechanism: storage systems reject writes from stale lock holders. Best answer: design to avoid needing distributed locks — idempotency + optimistic concurrency often suffice.

nodes.label: Distributed Transactions (2PC, Saga Pattern)
nodes.excerpt: Distributed transactions coordinate atomic changes across multiple independent databases or services. Two-phase commit (2PC) achieves atomicity through a coordinator that sequences prepare and commit phases, but blocks indefinitely if the coordinator fails between phases — making it a poor fit for microservices across the internet. Sagas decompose a cross-service workflow into local transactions connected by events or orchestration, with compensating transactions for rollback. The Outbox Pattern solves the reliability problem of publishing events after a local database write. The practical recommendation: avoid distributed transactions by design; when unavoidable, prefer saga over 2PC for microservice architectures.

nodes.label: Eventual Consistency
nodes.excerpt: Eventual consistency is a liveness guarantee: if no new updates are made to a data item, all replicas will eventually converge to the same value. Werner Vogels (Amazon CTO) formalized the concept in a 2009 ACM Queue paper. The key practical insight: eventual consistency is not a single model but a spectrum — BASE (Basically Available, Soft state, Eventually consistent) vs ACID describes the family, but real systems offer session guarantees like read-your-own-writes and monotonic reads that are much stronger than raw eventual consistency. Conflict resolution strategy (last-write-wins, vector clocks, CRDTs) determines what 'converge' means when concurrent writes occur.

nodes.label: Idempotency in Distributed Systems
nodes.excerpt: Idempotency means an operation applied multiple times has the same effect as applying it once. Essential for safe retries in distributed systems where clients can't know if a request succeeded before the network dropped. The key mechanism: idempotency keys (client-generated UUID stored server-side with the result, checked on duplicate requests). Stripe's implementation is canonical: Idempotency-Key HTTP header, server stores (key → response) for 24 hours, deduplicates in-flight requests. HTTP method semantics: GET/PUT/DELETE are idempotent by spec; POST is not and needs explicit idempotency design. Critical for: payments, order creation, email sends, message consumers receiving at-least-once delivery.

nodes.label: Leader Election
nodes.excerpt: Leader election selects one node from a group to act as coordinator — for write routing, job scheduling, consensus group management, or any operation requiring a single decision-maker. The hard problem: split-brain, where two nodes simultaneously believe they are leader. Prevention requires quorum (majority of nodes must vote for a candidate) and term/epoch numbers (monotonically increasing counter that stale leaders can't fake). In practice, Raft (etcd) and ZooKeeper ZAB implement correct leader election — teams should not build their own. Kubernetes uses leader election via ConfigMap/Lease API for controller HA. The election timeout tradeoff: too short = unnecessary elections; too long = slow failover (Raft default: 150–300ms).

nodes.label: Load Balancing Strategies
nodes.excerpt: Load balancing distributes traffic across multiple backend servers to improve throughput, availability, and fault tolerance. The strategy choice (round robin, least connections, consistent hash, weighted) depends on the protocol, statefulness of backends, and failure behavior requirements. L4 load balancers route by TCP/UDP headers and are extremely fast; L7 load balancers understand HTTP semantics and enable path-based routing, header-based auth, and request rewriting. The most critical design concern is not the routing algorithm but the failure behavior: health checks, connection draining, retry semantics, and what happens when the load balancer itself fails.

nodes.label: Message Queues (Kafka, RabbitMQ, SQS)
nodes.excerpt: Message queues decouple producers from consumers by persisting messages until a consumer processes them. Kafka is a distributed commit log designed for high-throughput event streaming with long retention and replay capability; RabbitMQ is a traditional message broker with rich routing and worker queue patterns; SQS is AWS's managed queue service optimized for simplicity and serverless integration. The choice depends on: do you need replay/event streaming (Kafka) or task distribution/flexible routing (RabbitMQ) or managed simplicity (SQS)? The most critical operational concern for all: dead-letter queues, idempotent consumers, and backpressure handling.

nodes.label: PACELC Theorem
nodes.excerpt: PACELC (Daniel Abadi, 2010) extends CAP by adding the latency/consistency tradeoff that exists during normal operation (no partition). CAP only covers partition behavior; PACELC covers the rest of the time. The formulation: if Partition, choose between Availability and Consistency; Else choose between Latency and Consistency. The EC side is often more practically important than the PA/PC side — partitions are rare events, but the latency/consistency tradeoff governs every normal request. Cassandra is PA/EL (prioritize availability and low latency); Spanner is PC/EC (strong consistency everywhere); DynamoDB is PA/EL by default, tunable toward PC/EC.

nodes.label: Pub Sub Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Pub/sub (publish/subscribe) is a messaging pattern where publishers emit events to topics without knowing who consumes them, and subscribers receive all events from topics they've subscribed to. It enables fanout (one event → many consumers), loose coupling (producers and consumers can be added or removed independently), and event-driven integration across services. The critical design decisions are: push vs pull delivery, at-least-once vs exactly-once semantics, topic granularity (too coarse = all consumers see irrelevant events; too fine = topic proliferation), and schema evolution strategy (Avro/Protobuf with a schema registry protects consumers from breaking producer changes).

nodes.id: Resources/System Design/Rate Limiting Algorithms (Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket, Sliding Window)
nodes.label: Rate Limiting Algorithms (Token Bucket, Leaky Bucket, Sliding Window)
nodes.excerpt: Rate limiting controls how many requests a client can make in a time period, protecting services from abuse, cost overruns, and overload. The five algorithms — token bucket, leaky bucket, fixed window, sliding window log, and sliding window counter — trade off burst tolerance, smoothness, precision, and storage cost. Token bucket is the most widely used: allows controlled bursts up to bucket capacity, refills at a fixed rate. Distributed rate limiting uses Redis atomic operations (INCR + EXPIRE, or Lua scripts for token bucket) so limits apply across multiple API server instances. Response headers (X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, Retry-After) are the standard way to communicate limits to clients.

nodes.label: Real-Time Systems Design
nodes.excerpt: Real-time systems deliver updates fast enough that delay changes the user experience — chat, multiplayer games, collaborative editing, live trading dashboards, notifications. The core architecture: persistent client connections (WebSockets) → gateway servers (handle 10K–100K connections each via non-blocking I/O) → internal pub/sub broker (Redis, Kafka) → backend services. Key design challenges: connection fan-out (celebrity problem: one event → 10M notifications), presence detection (heartbeat every 30s + Redis sorted set + TTL), reconnection (exponential backoff + last event ID for SSE replay), and backpressure (server drops slow clients or uses bounded buffers). Transport choice: WebSockets for bidirectional; SSE for server→client only; long polling as fallback. Conflict resolution in collaborative editing: OT (Google Docs) or CRDTs (Figma, Notion).

nodes.label: Scalability — Horizontal vs Vertical
nodes.excerpt: Scalability is a system's ability to handle more load without sacrificing performance or reliability. Vertical scaling (bigger machine) is simpler but hits hard limits; horizontal scaling (more machines) has near-infinite ceiling but requires stateless service design, distributed coordination, and load distribution. The most important practical constraint: stateful components (databases, caches with local state, anything using a sticky session) are hard to scale horizontally, while stateless services (API servers, workers) scale trivially. Amdahl's Law quantifies the ceiling: if 10% of work is serial, no amount of parallelization can exceed 10x speedup.

nodes.label: System Design Interview Framework
nodes.excerpt: A system design interview framework gives a repeatable structure for turning vague product prompts into scalable architectures under time pressure. The canonical flow is: clarify requirements → estimate scale → define APIs → high-level design → deep dive bottlenecks → discuss tradeoffs. The most common failure modes are jumping to architecture before clarifying requirements, staying at the whiteboard-boxes level without reasoning about why each component is there, and not surfacing tradeoffs explicitly. Strong candidates drive the conversation, state assumptions, and use scale estimates to justify every major design decision.

nodes.label: WebRTC Architecture
nodes.excerpt: WebRTC enables browser-native real-time audio/video/data P2P communication. Connection setup: Offer/Answer SDP exchange via signaling (WebSocket) → ICE candidate gathering (local, reflexive via STUN, relay via TURN) → connectivity checks → best path selected. STUN discovers public IP behind NAT; TURN relays traffic when direct P2P fails (symmetric NAT, strict firewall) — target <30% of calls needing TURN. Group calls: full mesh (N×(N-1)/2 connections, breaks at 5+ people) → SFU (Selective Forwarding Unit: participants send 1 upstream, SFU routes to all others, supports simulcast) → MCU (server-side mixing, 1 downstream per participant but CPU-expensive). Production SFU implementations: Livekit (Go), mediasoup (Node.js), Janus, Jitsi. Media is SRTP (encrypted by default via DTLS key exchange).

nodes.label: Testing Strategy
nodes.excerpt: testing strategy by layer and risk: unit, integration, E2E, contract, property, load, frontend/backend/database/LLM tests, mocks, flaky tests, and CI.

nodes.label: Transformer Architecture
nodes.excerpt: full transformer internals: tokenization, attention mechanism (Q/K/V), multi-head attention, residual stream, FFN, KV cache, modern improvements (RoPE, GQA, SwiGLU).

nodes.label: Vault Structure
nodes.excerpt: what each folder is for (Projects vs Areas vs Resources vs Maps vs Templates) and the PARA logic behind the structure.

nodes.label: WSL2 Setup
nodes.excerpt: WSL2 gotchas solved: PulseAudio audio fix, Windows host IP, Obsidian portproxy.

nodes.label: Web Animation
nodes.excerpt: CSS transitions/keyframes, GPU-only rule, GSAP, Framer Motion, React Spring, Lottie pipeline, Rive state machines, Three.js/R3F, what makes animations feel good (easing, stagger, spring physics, reduced motion).

nodes.label: Accessibility (a11y) Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Accessibility means interfaces work for screen reader users, keyboard users, and people with motor, visual, or cognitive disabilities. The practical foundation: semantic HTML (free accessibility), keyboard navigation (focusable, logical order, visible focus), ARIA (only when HTML semantics can't do it), sufficient contrast (4.5:1), and prefers-reduced-motion.

nodes.label: Animation Easing Curves (Cubic Bezier)
nodes.excerpt: Easing functions map animation time (0→1) to progress (0→1). Cubic Bezier curves define this mapping with two control points. Ease-out feels responsive (fast start, gentle settle); ease-in feels heavy (slow start, fast end). The right curve makes the difference between UX that feels polished vs mechanical.

nodes.label: Browser Security Model (Same-Origin Policy)
nodes.excerpt: The browser security model isolates origins so that scripts from one site cannot read another site's data. The same-origin policy is the foundation; CORS, CSP, SameSite cookies, sandboxed iframes, and storage partitioning are its extensions. Understanding what the browser enforces — and what it doesn't — is essential for secure web development.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/Browser Storage (localStorage, sessionStorage, IndexedDB, Cookies)
nodes.label: Browser Storage (localStorage, sessionStorage, IndexedDB, Cookies)
nodes.excerpt: Browser storage options differ by capacity, persistence, synchronicity, and security. Cookies are HTTP state and ride requests to the server. localStorage/sessionStorage are synchronous JS-readable key-value stores. IndexedDB is an async transactional database for large structured data. Never store sensitive tokens in JS-readable storage.

nodes.label: CORS — How It Works
nodes.excerpt: CORS is a browser-enforced protocol that lets servers opt into cross-origin reads. It has no effect on non-browser clients. A preflight OPTIONS request is sent for 'complex' requests; simple requests go directly. The most common mistake is using wildcard origins with credentials, which is explicitly forbidden.

nodes.label: CSS Animation and Transitions
nodes.excerpt: CSS transitions interpolate a single property change between states; animations use keyframes for multi-step, repeating motion. Both should favor transform/opacity for GPU-composited rendering. The performance rule: compositor-only properties = 60fps; layout-triggering properties = jank risk.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/CSS Architecture (BEM, CSS Modules, Tailwind)
nodes.label: CSS Architecture (BEM, CSS Modules, Tailwind)
nodes.excerpt: CSS is globally scoped and cascade-order-dependent — at scale, this becomes a specificity war. BEM solves it with naming conventions; CSS Modules with build-time local scoping; Tailwind by eliminating semantic class names in favor of utility-first design tokens. Each is a different tradeoff between readability, scoping, and design consistency.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/CSS Grid vs Flexbox — When to Use Each
nodes.label: CSS Grid vs Flexbox — When to Use Each
nodes.excerpt: Flexbox distributes items along one axis (content-out); Grid defines both axes simultaneously (layout-in). The practical rule: use Flexbox for components, nav bars, and single-direction flows; use Grid for page structure, dashboards, and any layout that needs rows AND columns to align.

nodes.label: Canvas API
nodes.excerpt: Canvas is an immediate-mode 2D drawing API — you issue draw commands, pixels are committed, no retained object tree. It's the right choice for games, custom charts, particle effects, and image manipulation. High-DPI handling (devicePixelRatio), requestAnimationFrame for smooth animation, and offscreen canvas for workers are the key advanced concerns.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS)
nodes.label: Core Web Vitals (LCP, INP, CLS)
nodes.excerpt: Core Web Vitals are Google's three user-experience metrics: LCP (loading — when main content appears), INP (responsiveness — how fast interactions respond), and CLS (visual stability — unexpected layout shifts). They affect both UX and Google Search ranking.

nodes.label: Critical Rendering Path
nodes.excerpt: The critical rendering path is the sequence DOM → CSSOM → Render Tree → Layout → Paint → Composite that must complete before the first visible pixel. CSS blocks rendering; synchronous JS blocks parsing. Optimizing means minimizing render-blocking resources on the critical path.

nodes.label: Design Tokens
nodes.excerpt: Design tokens are the named values that encode visual decisions — color, spacing, typography, radius, shadow, motion, breakpoints. They create a shared vocabulary between design tools (Figma) and code. The three-tier model (primitive → semantic → component tokens) separates raw values from meaning and enables theming.

nodes.label: Framer Motion Patterns
nodes.excerpt: Framer Motion is the standard React animation library. Its core patterns: motion components (animate prop), variants (coordinated tree animation), AnimatePresence (exit animations), layout (auto-animate size/position changes), and useMotionValue/useSpring (imperative gesture-driven animation).

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/How Browsers Work (Parsing, Rendering, Compositing)
nodes.label: How Browsers Work (Parsing, Rendering, Compositing)
nodes.excerpt: Browsers render pages through a multi-stage pipeline: HTML parsing → DOM, CSS parsing → CSSOM, JavaScript execution, style calculation, layout (box positions), paint (draw commands), composite (GPU layer merging). Each stage has different cost implications — transform/opacity only trigger compositing; width/height trigger full relayout.

nodes.label: Next.js App Router Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Next.js App Router is a file-system routing architecture built on React Server Components. The primary design skill is boundary placement: which components run on the server, which on the client, and how data flows between caching layers.

nodes.label: OpenGraph Protocol
nodes.excerpt: OpenGraph meta tags control how your page appears in link previews on Twitter/X, LinkedIn, Slack, iMessage, Discord, and Facebook. The og:title, og:description, og:image, and og:url tags are the critical four. Images must be public, 1200×630px for best results, and must not require authentication to fetch.

nodes.label: Progressive Web Apps (PWAs)
nodes.excerpt: PWAs are web apps that use a Web App Manifest + service worker + HTTPS to become installable, offline-capable, and app-like. The manifest describes the install experience; the service worker powers offline and caching; together they unlock 'Add to Home Screen' on mobile and desktop. iOS PWA support is meaningful but more limited than Chrome/Android.

nodes.label: React — Component Lifecycle
nodes.excerpt: React's lifecycle has three phases: render (pure computation), commit (DOM mutation), and effects (post-paint synchronization). Function components express lifecycle via hooks; the key mental model is that rendering is pure and effects are for external synchronization.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/React — Hooks Deep Dive (useState, useEffect, useRef, useMemo)
nodes.label: React — Hooks Deep Dive (useState, useEffect, useRef, useMemo)
nodes.excerpt: React hooks are implemented as a linked list per component fiber; their rules (no conditionals, no loops) exist because position in that list is identity. Each hook solves a distinct problem: state (useState), synchronization (useEffect), mutable identity (useRef), caching (useMemo/useCallback).

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/React — Performance Optimization (memo, lazy, Suspense)
nodes.label: React — Performance Optimization (memo, lazy, Suspense)
nodes.excerpt: React performance problems are almost always caused by too many renders, too much JavaScript shipped to the browser, or slow data loading. memo/useMemo/useCallback reduce renders; lazy/Suspense split code and manage async boundaries; profiling before optimizing is essential.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/React — Reconciliation and Virtual DOM
nodes.label: React — Reconciliation and Virtual DOM
nodes.excerpt: React reconciliation is the O(n) algorithm that diffs component trees to determine minimal DOM updates. The virtual DOM is an in-memory JS object tree React uses as the intermediate representation; Fiber is the modern reconciler that enables incremental rendering and concurrent mode.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/React — State Management (Zustand, Redux, Context API)
nodes.label: React — State Management (Zustand, Redux, Context API)
nodes.excerpt: React state management is a classification problem: local state, shared client state, URL state, and server cache each have different tools. Context rerenders all consumers on any value change; Zustand solves this with selector-based subscriptions; Redux centralizes with actions/reducers; TanStack Query owns async server state.

nodes.label: Responsive Design Principles
nodes.excerpt: Responsive design adapts layouts to viewport size, device capabilities, and user preferences. The modern toolkit: CSS Grid auto-fill, Flexbox wrap, clamp() for fluid type, container queries for component-level adaptation, and logical properties for i18n. Mobile-first and content-first breakpoints beat device-preset breakpoints.

nodes.label: SEO Fundamentals
nodes.excerpt: Technical SEO for engineers: crawlability (robots.txt, sitemap, render strategy), on-page signals (title, meta description, H1, semantic HTML), indexing controls (canonical, noindex, hreflang), structured data (JSON-LD), and Core Web Vitals. The underlying model: Googlebot crawls, renders, indexes, ranks.

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/Server-Side Rendering vs Static Site Generation vs CSR
nodes.label: Server-Side Rendering vs Static Site Generation vs CSR
nodes.excerpt: SSR, SSG, ISR, and CSR are different answers to 'when and where does HTML get produced?' Each tradeoff involves freshness, TTFB, caching, personalization, and JavaScript cost. Modern frameworks mix strategies per route.

nodes.label: Service Workers
nodes.excerpt: A service worker is a background script that proxies all network requests for its origin. It enables offline support, aggressive caching, and push notifications. The lifecycle (install → activate → fetch) and cache versioning are the tricky parts — stale caches can silently serve broken app versions.

nodes.label: Spring Physics in Animation
nodes.excerpt: Spring physics models animation using mass, stiffness, and damping — the same equations as a physical spring. Unlike fixed-duration easing, springs are velocity-aware and duration-less: interrupting a spring mid-flight continues smoothly from current velocity rather than snapping. Dominant in mobile UI and gesture systems.

nodes.label: Web Components
nodes.excerpt: Web Components are three browser standards: Custom Elements (define new HTML tags with lifecycle hooks), Shadow DOM (encapsulated markup and styles that don't leak), and HTML Templates (inert reusable fragments). Their main value is framework-agnostic reusable components — a button you build works in React, Vue, Svelte, or plain HTML.

nodes.label: Web Performance Optimization
nodes.excerpt: Web performance optimization is a layered discipline: network (CDN, caching, compression), rendering (SSR/SSG, critical CSS), JavaScript (code splitting, tree shaking, defer), assets (image formats, lazy loading), and runtime (avoiding layout thrashing, reducing main-thread work).

nodes.id: Resources/Web Technologies/Web Scraping Ethics and Techniques
nodes.label: Web Scraping Ethics and Techniques
nodes.excerpt: Web scraping extracts data from websites via HTTP + HTML parsing or browser automation. Techniques: fetch + cheerio for static pages, Playwright/Puppeteer for JS-rendered content. Ethics: robots.txt is a convention not a law, but ToS violations, CFAA, and GDPR create real legal exposure. Rate limiting and identifying your client reduces harm.

nodes.label: WebGL Basics
nodes.excerpt: WebGL is a JavaScript API for executing GLSL shader programs on the GPU. The pipeline: geometry (vertex buffer) → vertex shader (positions) → rasterization → fragment shader (colors) → framebuffer. Three.js abstracts this into scenes, cameras, meshes, and materials. WebGPU is the modern successor with compute shaders and a better API.

nodes.label: What Is Obsidian
nodes.excerpt: what Obsidian is, what it can do, and the right workflow for this vault (discuss with AI first, then write clean notes).

nodes.label: Hyper-V Architecture
nodes.excerpt: Hyper-V is Microsoft's virtualization platform and underpins features such as WSL2, Windows Sandbox, some container workflows, and virtual machines. Understanding its hypervisor, virtual switches, disks, isolation, and resource management explains many WSL2 and Windows virtualization behaviors.

nodes.label: PowerShell Deep Dive
nodes.excerpt: PowerShell is an object-oriented shell and automation language for Windows and cross-platform administration. Its core difference from traditional shells is that pipelines pass structured .NET objects, making it strong for system inspection, scripting, and administrative automation.

nodes.id: Resources/Windows & WSL2/WSL2 Architecture (Lightweight VM, VirtIO, Plan 9)
nodes.label: WSL2 Architecture (Lightweight VM, VirtIO, Plan 9)
nodes.excerpt: WSL2 runs Linux inside a lightweight Hyper-V virtual machine with integration layers for filesystem, networking, process interop, graphics, and devices. It feels like a local Linux shell, but VM boundaries explain many performance, networking, and filesystem quirks.

nodes.id: Resources/Windows & WSL2/WSL2 Audio Setup (PulseAudio, WSLg RDPSource)
nodes.label: WSL2 Audio Setup (PulseAudio, WSLg RDPSource)
nodes.excerpt: WSL2 audio depends on whether the setup uses older PulseAudio workarounds or modern WSLg integration through RDP-based audio sources. Knowing which path is active helps debug microphone, playback, GUI app, and voice-assistant issues across Windows and Linux.

nodes.id: Resources/Windows & WSL2/WSL2 Interop (Windows Executables from WSL)
nodes.label: WSL2 Interop (Windows Executables from WSL)
nodes.excerpt: WSL2 interop lets Linux shells launch Windows executables and lets Windows tools access Linux distributions, making mixed workflows convenient but occasionally confusing. Path translation, quoting, environment variables, filesystem location, and process context are the usual sources of bugs.

nodes.id: Resources/Windows & WSL2/WSL2 Networking (NAT, Gateway IP, Port Proxying)
nodes.label: WSL2 Networking (NAT, Gateway IP, Port Proxying)
nodes.excerpt: WSL2 networking usually places the Linux VM behind NAT with a virtual gateway, which affects localhost, inbound access, port forwarding, and connections from Windows to Linux services. Understanding the host/guest boundary helps debug dev servers, APIs, databases, and mobile-device testing.

nodes.label: Windows Permissions and UAC
nodes.excerpt: Windows permissions combine users, groups, access control lists, integrity levels, and UAC elevation prompts. UAC is not just an annoyance: it separates normal user context from administrative changes and explains why scripts, installers, services, and terminals behave differently when elevated.

nodes.id: Resources/Windows & WSL2/Windows Registry — How It Works
nodes.label: Windows Registry — How It Works
nodes.excerpt: The Windows Registry is a hierarchical configuration database for the operating system, installed software, device settings, users, services, and shell behavior. It is powerful but easy to damage, so registry edits should be targeted, backed up, and treated like system-level changes.

nodes.id: Resources/Windows & WSL2/Windows Services vs Scheduled Tasks
nodes.label: Windows Services vs Scheduled Tasks
nodes.excerpt: Windows services are long-running background processes managed by the Service Control Manager, while scheduled tasks run programs based on triggers such as time, login, idle state, or events. Choosing between them depends on whether work must be continuously supervised or triggered at specific moments.

nodes.id: Resources/Windows & WSL2/Windows Startup and Task Scheduler
nodes.label: Windows Startup and Task Scheduler
nodes.excerpt: Windows startup behavior is controlled by several places: Startup folders, registry Run keys, services, scheduled tasks, shell extensions, and app-specific launch settings. Task Scheduler is the structured way to run jobs on triggers such as login, boot, idle, time, or system events.

nodes.label: Windows Subsystem for Android
nodes.excerpt: Windows Subsystem for Android was Microsoft's Android app runtime for Windows, built on virtualization and integration with the Windows host. Microsoft Support says WSA and the Amazon Appstore are no longer available in the Microsoft Store starting March 5, 2025, so it is now mostly useful as subsystem-design context rather than a current Windows app strategy.

nodes.label: Windows Terminal Configuration
nodes.excerpt: Windows Terminal centralizes command-line profiles for PowerShell, Command Prompt, WSL distributions, SSH, fonts, themes, keybindings, startup behavior, and panes. A clean configuration makes Windows and WSL feel like one coherent dev environment instead of separate shells.

nodes.id: Resources/Windows & WSL2/winget and Windows Package Management
nodes.label: winget and Windows Package Management
nodes.excerpt: winget is Microsoft's command-line package manager for discovering, installing, upgrading, and exporting Windows applications. It makes Windows setup more reproducible, especially when combined with PowerShell scripts, terminal profiles, WSL distributions, and documented dev-environment bootstrap steps.

nodes.id: SAN vs NAS vs DAS — Storage Architecture Compared
nodes.label: SAN vs NAS vs DAS — Storage Architecture Compared

nodes.id: Shortest Path Algorithms (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, A*, Floyd-Warshall)
nodes.label: Shortest Path Algorithms (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, A*, Floyd-Warshall)

nodes.id: Synaptic Plasticity (Hebbian Learning — "Neurons that Fire Together")
nodes.label: Synaptic Plasticity (Hebbian Learning — "Neurons that Fire Together")
